questions.rationale: Nursing goals for all individuals and their families seeking preventive care are improvement of quality of life through positive lifestyle choices and taking responsibility for health and wellness. Nurses can refer patients to a variety of personal health quizzes, located in the online version of Healthy People 2020, for risk assessments of their health status and lifestyle. The quizzes allow people to track their health and wellness status over a period of years and identify trends in disease risk factors that can be modified through lifestyle interventions or preventive measures before the disease occurs. The Healthy People 2020 initiative helps nurses provide educational materials for individuals, families, and communities, enabling them to lead healthier lifestyles and to make permanent changes in wellness habits.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that use of seatbelts and airbags in automobiles is an example of which term?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Secondary prevention

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tertiary prevention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Holistic care

choices.label: D
choices.text: Primary prevention

questions.rationale: Primary prevention is instituted before disease becomes established by removing the causes or increasing resistance. Examples include the use of seatbelts and airbags in automobiles, helmet use when riding bicycles or motorcycles, and the occupational use of mechanical devices when lifting heavy objects. Secondary prevention is undertaken in cases of latent (hidden) disease. Although the patient may be asymptomatic, the disease process can be detected by medical tests. Nu NrsUesRmSaIyNuGseTsBcr.eeniOng tests to assess for latent disease in vulnerable populations. Examples of screening tests used as secondary prevention strategies include the purified protein derivative (PPD) skin test for tuberculosis, fecal occult blood test for colorectal cancer, and mammograms for breast cancer. Tertiary prevention, also known as the treatment or rehabilitation stage of preventive care, is implemented when a condition or illness is permanent and irreversible. The aim of care is to reduce the number and impact of complications and disabilities resulting from a disease or medical condition. Interventions are intended to reduce suffering caused by poor health and assist the patients in adjusting to incurable conditions. Nursing care is focused on rehabilitation efforts in the tertiary stage of prevention. Holistic care is an approach to applying healing therapies. Nurses participate in holistic care through the use of natural healing remedies and complementary interventions. These include the use of art and guided imagery, therapeutic touch, music therapy, relaxation techniques, and reminiscence.

questions.rationale: Secondary prevention is undertaken in cases of latent (hidden) disease. Although the patient may be asymptomatic, the disease process can be detected by medical tests. Nurses may use screening tests to assess for latent disease in vulnerable populations. Examples of screening tests used as secondary prevention strategies include the purified protein derivative (PPD) skin test for tuberculosis, fecal occult blood test for colorectal cancer, and mammograms for breast cancer. Primary prevention is instituted before disease becomes established by removing the causes or increasing resistance. Examples include the use of seatbelts and airbags in automobiles, helmet use when riding bicycles or motorcycles, and the occupational use of mechanical devices when lifting heavy objects. Tertiary prevention, also known as the treatment or rehabilitation stage of preventive care, is implemented when a condition or illness is permanent and irreversible. The aim of care is to reduce the number and impact of complications and disabilities resulting from a disease or medical condition. Interventions are intended to reduce suffering caused by poor health and assist the patients in adjusting to incurable conditions. Nursing care is focused on rehabilitation efforts in the tertiary stage of prevention. Holistic care is an approach to applying healing therapies. Nurses participate in holistic care through the use of natural healing remedies and complementary interventions. These include the use of art and guided imagery, therapeutic touch, music therapy, relaxation techniques, and reminiscence.

questions.stem: The patient asks the nurse toNexUpRlaSinIcNolGlaTboBra.tiCveOhMealth care partnerships. The nurse gives a correct description when making which statement regarding collaborative care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Does not require participation of the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Is individual and cannot be mandated or legislated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Education needs are delegated to assistive personnel.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Is designed to provide care to the patient as a whole.

questions.rationale: Collaborative health care partnerships are designed to deliver well-balanced care to the patient as a whole, rather than rendering fragmented care involving a single element of a disease process. Prevention is not solely the responsibility of the nurse; it involves active participation by the individual and the combined services of practitioners in a spectrum of health care disciplines as varied as nutrition, physical therapy, exercise physiology, and pharmacy. Collaborative preventive care can be mandated in the form of health care legislation, with rates for reimbursement of practitioners determined by the individual provider‘s ability to collaborate and develop innovative methods for delivering high-quality, cost-effective health care services. The role of the professional nurse is to collaborate and communicate health education to the patient and family, care provider, or surrogate. Patient education responsibilities are not delegated to assistive personnel or other members of the health care team and are considered a cornerstone of nursing care.

questions.stem: A patient is diagnosed with pneumonia after an abrupt onset of fever, cough, and malaise. The patient is started on antibiotic therapy and is expected to improve in 2 to 3 weeks. Which statement by the nurse correctly identifies this illness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remission

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exacerbation

questions.rationale: Acute illness is typically characterized by an abrupt onset and short duration (<6 months). Clinical manifestations of acute illness appear quickly. They may be severe or lethal, or they may soon resolve because they respond to treatment or are self-limiting. Chronic illness is characterized by a loss or abnormality of body function that lasts longer than 6 months and requires ongoing long- term care. Chronic health conditions may be controlled with lifestyle management or drug therapy, but they are considered to be irreversible. Chronic illness may be characterized by periods of wellness (i.e., remission) and exacerbation (worsening) of clinical manifestations, which can be life threatening. Individuals learn to adjust their lifestyles accordingly.

questions.stem: The nurse needs to consider which approach when caring for patients with chronic illness?

choices.label: B

choices.label: C

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acknowledge the limitations placed on the patient by his suffering.

questions.rationale: Nurses can help patients establish a daily routine of care by educating them about how to manage their care and the symptoms associated with the condition, including emergency or life- threatening situations. Emphasis is on improving quality of life through preventive behaviors. The attitude of being a victim, suffering with, or being afflicted by a chronic illness is viewed by nurses as a counterproductive behavior that needs positive intervention. Nurses can assist patients with strategies that help them cope with their chronic conditions and associated feelings of anger, frustration, and depression. Encouragement and positive support from a professional nurse can help individuals gain control over the alternating periods of health and illness and improve their quality of life.

questions.stem: A patient presents to the clinic for illness, and the sick role is legitimized by the provider. The nurse recognizes this as what stage of illness according to Suchman‘s Model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: I

choices.label: B
choices.text: II

choices.label: C
choices.text: III

choices.label: D
choices.text: IV U

questions.rationale: In stage III (Medical care contact), professional advice from health care providers is sought by the individual. A professional health care provider identifies and validates the illness and legitimizes the sick role. During stage II (Assumption of the sick role), the person decides that the illness is genuine and that care is necessary. This stage gives an individual permission to act sick and to be excused temporarily from typical social and personal obligations. During stage I (Symptom experience), a clinical manifestation of disease is experienced, and the person acknowledges that something is wrong and seeks a cure. The outcome of stage I is that the person accepts the reality of symptoms and decides to take action in seeking care. During stage IV (Dependent patient role), the person, who is designated as a patient, usually undergoes treatment. During this stage, patients often feel dependent on others and may experience ambivalent or fearful thoughts that cause them to reject treatment, the advice of health care providers, and the illness.

questions.rationale: Assessment of the patient be g Nin sRw i tIh r iGs k fBac.toCrs t hMa t take into account the person‘s age andthe associated level of immune system function. The very young, especially neonates and infants born prematurely, are more susceptible to infections because of the immaturity of their immune systems. Likewise, older adults have decreased immune system function because of the aging process. Older patients are at risk for opportunistic infections resulting from harmless organisms that become pathogenic and illness from the spread of community-acquired disease. Complications from comorbidities of chronic disease may also increase suffering in the aged population.

choices.text: It is nonresponsive to alteration by way of lifestyle modification.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is not a factor in mental illness because it is behavioral.

questions.rationale: The genetic vulnerability of an organism, or risk of disease expression based on genotype, is involuntarily passed from biologic parents to their offspring. Societal attitudes about testing and management of high-risk populations depend on the potential for expression of genetic disorders that may be triggered by environmental factors. Controlling factors that place stress on physiologic function can reduce pathologic genetic expression and susceptibility to disease. For example, a person with a family history of hyperlipidemia and atherosclerosis is at risk for developing cardiovascular disease later in life. Lifestyle-modifying factors, such as weight reduction, daily exercise, and balanced nutritional intake, can help reduce the likelihood that the genetic risk factor for heart disease will be expressed. Diabetes, cancer, mental illness, and renal dise Nase Ra l s oIh a vGe gBen.etCic cMomponents and are amenable to interventions that reduce risk. U S N T O

questions.stem: The patient is asking about using the Internet for resources regarding lifestyle behaviors and benefits of modification. What is the best response that the nurse should provide the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Information on lifestyle behaviors is not available on the Internet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient should use websites that are easy to understand.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Most websites are designed for health care providers only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only negative outcomes are evaluated on the Internet.

questions.rationale: Information on lifestyle behaviors that lead to disease is available at research-sponsored websites that have peer-reviewed material and expert analyses. Website content should be easy to read and understandable for the general population. Most sites that discuss the latest information about health risks, lifestyle behaviors, and outcomes have separate information specifically for health care providers. Research that evaluates positive and negative lifestyle- behavior outcomes is constantly evolving as discoveries are made about the physiologic changes bodies experience with disease and illness.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing a patient‘s environment and its impact on outdoor activity and notes that the child rarely plays outside. Which is true regarding the indoor environment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Indoor environments protect the patient from toxics chemicals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Indoor activity is sometimes a result of unsafe outdoor conditions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Indoor activity decreases the risk of respiratory illness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Indoor lifestyles reduce the risk for sedentary behaviors.

questions.rationale: Outdoor environments affect individual health in the areas of sanitation and waste disposal, water quality, air quality, and safety. Children living in areas where there are safety issues related to gang activity, sexual predators, or heavy traffic are less likely to engage in outdoor play activities. Their limited access to safe outdoor play space increases their risk for sedentary behaviors, excessive calorie intake, and obesity. Indoor environments may harbor toxic household cleaning agents, chemicals (e.g., radon, carbon monoxide, unused drugs), tobacco smoke, and energy sources (e.g., microwave ovens). Exposure to mold, household pests (e.g., dust mites, spiders), and unsanitary living conditions in an enclosed space increases the likelihood of respiratory illness and skin disorders.

questions.stem: The nurse correctly recognizes which one of the following illnesses to trigger the broadest range of emotional and behavioral responses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ear infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mild concussion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rheumatoid arthritis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Influenza

questions.rationale: Chronic, debilitating disease such as rheumatoid arthritis and severe illness can produce a broad range of emotional or behavioral responses in patients and their families. A short-term, self- limited illness that is not life threatening does not evoke emotions or actions that cause fundamental changes in daily lifestyle. More often, illnesses such as the flu, ear infections, and sore throats are viewed as minor irritations or inconveniences. They usually require a short-term adjustment in daily routines, and treatment of symptoms is the priority so that the individual can continue with normal activities. The emotional and behavioral changes associated with non–life- threatening illness are usually minimal, and the individual quickly returns to the previous baseline level of emotional functioning.

questions.stem: Self-concept refers to the way in which individuals perceive unchanging aspects of themselves, such as social character, cognitive abilities, physical appearance, and body image. Which additional point does the nurse the nurse recognize as part of the definition of self- concept?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If negative, self-concept will allow the patient to compensate for weaknesses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If positive, self-concept will cause the patient to see challenges as devastating.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Self-concept is a concept that is derived from the patient internally.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Self-concept depends on relationships with family and friends.

questions.rationale: Self-concept refers to the way in which individuals perceive unchanging aspects of themselves, such as social character, cognitive abilities, physical appearance, and body image. It is a mental image of self in relation to others and the surroundings. If the image is positive, the person will develop strengths, compensate for weaknesses, and experience life in a healthy way. If the image is negative (e.g., frail), the person will find life‘s challenges devastating and sometimes insurmountable. The impact of illness on the self-concept of a patient and the patient‘s family members depends on how secure the parties‘ relationships are with one another.

questions.stem: Which recommendations would the nurse identify as appropriate screening guidelines? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Women ages 21 to 29 should have a Pap test every 3 years.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Self-breast exams should be addressed with male and female patients.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adolescent males should perform monthly self-testicular exams.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Women ages 30 to 65 should receive Pap tests every 10 years.

choices.label: E
choices.text: After a total hysterectom Ny,UPRapStIesNtinGgTsBho.uCldObMe more frequent.

questions.rationale: All women should begin cervical cancer screening at the age of 21 years. Women between the ages of 21 and 29 years should have a Papanicolaou (Pap) test every 3 years. A priority assessment task for nurses in a variety of care settings is to ask female and male patients about breast self- examination. An adolescent male should be assessed for testicular self-examination habits, and older males should have an annual prostate examination. Women between the ages of 30 and 65 years should have a Pap test plus a human papillomavirus (HPV) test (i.e., co-testing) every 5 years. Women 65 years of age or older who have had normal results for previous Pap tests should no longer be screened. Women who have had a total hysterectomy (i.e., removal of the uterus and cervix) should not be tested, unless the surgery was done as a treatment for cervical cancer or pre-cancer.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which of the following to be a benefit of regular physical exercise? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enhances the immune system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreases bone density.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Limits joint mobility.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Improves mental health.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Helps to prevent type 2 diabetes.

questions.rationale: Exercise is essential for the prevention of illness and promotion of wellness. Physical exercise is any bodily activity or movement that enhances or maintains physical fitness levels and overall health. Exercise strengthens muscles, improves cardiovascular performance, hones athletic skills and endurance, and reduces or maintains weight, and it is performed for enjoyment (Powers and Howley, 2012). Regular physical exercise enhances the immune system, builds and maintains healthy bone density, increases joint mobility, and helps to prevent cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and obesity. Exercise also improves mental health and helps to prevent depression through the release of endorphins and other neurotransmitters that are responsible for exercise- induced euphoria (Powers and Howley, 2012).

questions.stem: The economic stability of individuals or families can determine whether they are willing to seek preventive care or screening examinations. The nurse knows which statements about screening examinations to be true? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Free or low-cost screening ensures patient screening.

choices.label: B
choices.text: People may not screen due to fear of testing positive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Early screening ensures minimal treatment costs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Employment stability is enhanced by early screening.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Treatment of disorders often means lost wages.

questions.rationale: The economic stability of individuals or families can determine whether they are willing to seek preventive care or screening examinations. Even if screening is free or low cost, the patient or family members may decline because of the potential for testing positive for a disease. Treatment of a disorder often requires time spent away from work, lost wages, and expensive drug therapies and diagnostic tests. The financial impact can be devastating to families or individuals who have a limited or fixed income and fear that employment stability may be compromised. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse is asked by the parent of a pediatric patient to explain the difference between growth and development. Which response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Growth is physical while development relates to physical, emotional, and cognitive function.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―There is no difference between the two since they occur simultaneously.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Development refers to musculoskeletal and nervous system abilities and growth is a change in height and weight.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Both refer to an increase in abilities and functions of the child that occur sequentially over time.‖

questions.rationale: Growth relates to physical changes in height and weight. Development refers to changes in ability across several dimensions such as physical, emotional, and cognitive. Stating that the two are not different does not show understanding of this difference. Development is not related strictly to changes in specific body systems. Although both refer to increases in abilities and functioning over time, this answer is too vague to give the parent useful information.

questions.stem: The pediatric nurse is treating a patient who has questions about safer sexual practices. The patient states, ―I think I should wait until marriage to be sexually active because I‘m not sure sex is OK outside of marriage.‖ The nurse understands the student is acting with which component of Freud‘s theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Id

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ego

choices.label: C
choices.text: Superego

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anal

questions.rationale: The superego is the structure that houses the moral branch of personality . The Id acts strictly on instinct without consideration of reality. The Ego is partly conscious but does not consider right from wrong. Freud‘s theory contains the ―anal phase.‖

questions.stem: The nurse is collecting a history from the parents of a 4-year-old female at a well-child visit. The parents express concern that they often find their daughter performing what appears to be masturbation. The nurse offers reassurance by explaining which stage of development according to Freud?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phallic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Latency

questions.rationale: The phallic stage occurs between the ages of 3 and 6 years, and pleasure centers on the child‘s discovery that self-stimulation is enjoyable. The oral stage is seen in infants where pleasure centers around the mouth and putting things in the mouth. The Anal stage occurs between 18 months and 3 years of age and is when tension and release of tension occur through anal elimination. The latency stage occurs between the ages of 6 years and puberty during which interest in sexuality is repressed. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Psychosocial Integrity

questions.stem: A nurse is providing anticipatory guidance to a new mother about the Erikson stage of trust versus mistrust. What education should the nurse provide to the mother to help her child successfully master this stage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Consistently provide your child with food and attention.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure someone is able to feed your child on a schedule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allow unrestricted crawling and exploring as the child develops.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide firm guidelines for behavior and activities.

questions.rationale: The most important item needed for a child to master this stage of development is a consistent caregiver who provides food and attention. If the caregiver is inconsistent or unable to meet these needs, the child will develop mistrust of those around him. Ensuring that someone feeds the child is not providing consistency. Allowing exploration within limits (setting boundaries) is important to master initiatiNveUvReSrsuIsNsGhaTmBe.anCdOdMoubt. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Psychosocial Integrity

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient that is actively trying to conceive a  child but continues to drink alcohol. The patient states that she‘ll stop drinking once she is pregnant. What is the most appropriate response by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Abstaining is best since most fetal development occurs before you realize you are pregnant.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Small amounts of alcohol are safe at any time during pregnancy.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Things will be okay if you quit drinking alcohol once you know you are pregnant.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Alcohol use should be avoided early in pregnancy but is acceptable past week 20.‖

questions.rationale: Rapid development occurs before many women know that they are pregnant, making alcohol consumption unsafe at any time during pregnancy. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questions.stem: The perinatal clinic nurse is going to teach a woman from a culture unfamiliar to the nurse about child-rearing practices. What action by the nurse is best before planning the education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure the availability of written material to give the woman.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess what practices are important to her cultural group.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determine if the woman is the primary family decision maker.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Refer the woman to a prenatal educational class.

questions.rationale: The nurse must ensure he/she has a solid understanding of important child-rearing concepts in the woman‘s culture or risk that any teaching done will be irrelevant and perhaps in opposition to important beliefs. Since the nurse is unfamiliar with this culture, the first step is to assess. Written material is helpful if the patient can read and comprehend it. It would be important to determine if the woman is the decision maker, but this is not as much of a priority as learning about the culture. Referring the woman to an educational group may or may not be helpful.

questions.stem: A home health care nurse is making a well-baby visit to the home of a new mother who has an infant. What assessment finding leads the nurse to provide further anticipatory guidance  and teaching to the mother?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mother states she does not breastfeed but uses a recommended formula.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Crib has colorful blankets and pillows for the baby to cuddle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A mobile is hanging well above the crib playing soft music.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Several rattles and plush toys are available in different textures.

questions.rationale: Objects such as pillows and blankets pose a suffocation hazard to infants and should be kept out of cribs. The other items are appropriate for a newborn.

questions.stem: To help a hospitalized infant master the tasks in Erikson‘s stage of Trust versus Mistrust, which action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide calming music during quiet time so the infant can sleep.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the family food vouchers for the hospital cafeteria.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arrange to have a cot or small bed placed in the infant‘s room.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Do not allow unlicensed assistive personnel to care for the infant.

questions.rationale: Caregiver consistency is vital to accomplishing this task. The nurse should provide the parent(s) a comfortable place to stay in the infant‘s room. Giving food vouchers is also a good intervention, but not as important as ensuring the parent(s) can stay with the child. Calming music is appropriate for a child this age but does not help the child master tasks in this phase. Sleep is important for any hospitalized patient but is unrelated to mastering the tasks in this phase.

questions.stem: The home health care nurse is visiting a family with a 3 year old to observe a meal. The parent gives the child a plate with 1/2 cup of pureed meat. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document how well the child eats the serving of meat.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inquire if the child still drinks from a bottle between meals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the parents what they serve the child for snacks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide teaching on the appropriate serving size for this child.

questions.rationale: An appropriate serving size is 1 tablespoon per year of age, so an appr opriate amount of meat for this child is 3 tablespoons, not 1/2 cup (which is 8 tablespoons). The nur se should provide more education to the family. The other options are appropriate but are not  directly related to the serving size of meat.

questions.stem: A preschool-aged child got into the cookie jar and ate several cookies before dinner. When confronted by the parent, the child responds, ―My pet horse ate them. ‖ What does the nurse teach the parents about this response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is normal for children to have imaginary friends at this age.

choices.label: B
choices.text: This vivid imagination will lead the child to misbehave later on.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lying is disobedient and should be punished consistently.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The child is obviously afraid of the parents‘ response.

questions.rationale: It is common for toddlers to hNa v eRi mIa g i nGa r yBf .r i eCn d sM. They are especially important in allowingthe child to express something unpleasant. The other responses are not appropriate. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questions.stem: A toddler has been hospitalized. The parents become upset when the toddler starts wetting his bed, saying that he has been potty trained for some time now. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Don‘t worry, this behavior will stop when he gets home.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Maybe he has a urinary tract infection; I‘ll get a urine sample.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I can call the Child Life Specialist for diversionary activities.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―It is common for kids in the hospital to regress to earlier behaviors.‖

questions.rationale: The stress of hospitalization often causes toddlers to regress in their behaviors, and the nurse should provide this information to the parents. Stating that the behavior will stop, although accurate, does not provide an explanation. There is no need for a urine sample. Using Child Life is always a good idea for hospitalized children but is not related to the question.

questions.stem: The nurse is conducting a home visit on a newborn. What observation would require the nurse to provide further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The caregiver warms the bottle and tests heat on the inside of the wrist.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The parents state the infant is sleeping with them until they buy a crib.

choices.label: C
choices.text: One parent states that when the child gets frustrating, the other parent takes over.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Caregivers consistently wash their hands before holding the baby.

questions.rationale: Infants should not sleep in the same bed as their parents because of the risk of suffocation. The other actions are appropriate.

questions.stem: The parents of a 4 year old express concern that the child is wearing the same size clothing as she did last year. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weigh and measure the child and compare with last visit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reassure parents that their child is growing normally.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the child‘s eating and activity patterns.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage the parents to provide the child a multivitamin.

questions.rationale: Physical growth slows during the preschool years, with most children only gaining about 5 lb and 2 1/2 to 3 inches a year. The nurse should weigh and measure the child and compare the readings to those taken at the last visit. Showing the parents these results and educating them on expected growth will reassure them. Simply telling the parents their child is normal does not provide objective informNatUionRaSnIdNisGdTisBm.issCiveMof their concern. The nurse should assess each child‘s eating and activity habits. The child may or may not need a vitamin. This can be discussed with the provider.

questions.stem: A nurse is conducting a preschool screening in the community. Which child would the nurse refer for further assessment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 4 year old who throws a ball over-handed but better under-handed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A 4 year old who can skip across the room after being shown how.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 5 year old who is able to ride a bicycle with training wheels.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 5 year old who is unable to ride a tricycle without falling.

questions.rationale: A 3 year old should be able to ride a tricycle, so a 5 year old unable to perform this task needs further assessment. The other activities are appropriate for each child‘s age.

questions.stem: A father expresses frustration that his school-aged child is suddenly ―sick all the time.‖ What action by the nurse is best? U S N

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encourage the father to give the child a multivitamin each day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Explain that illness is frequent in this age-group because of exposure to others.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encourage the father to discuss testing the child‘s immunity with the provider.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Make sure the parents are washing their hands frequently in the home.

questions.rationale: Children in this age-group tend to have a higher incidence of minor illnesses because of exposure to others. The nurse can reassure the father by explaining this. No other action is needed at this point. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questions.stem: A school-aged child is scheduled for a minor procedure and is very nervous. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reassure the child the procedure is too minor to worry about.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Read the child a pamphlet about what to expect during the procedure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the child you will have the provider ―put her to sleep‖ during the procedure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Explain the procedure and what to expect in simple terms.

questions.rationale: School-aged children benefit from simple explanations they can understand. Just telling the child not to worry is dismissive of the child‘s concerns. A school-aged child may not be able to read and/or understand a written pamphlet. Using phrases such as ―put you to sleep‖ should be avoided since they can be misinterpreted. Applying

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing an adolescent female who began menstruating 2 years ago. She has grown 1/2 inch in the last 2 years but has not gained any weight. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the teen to provide a 24-hour diet recall.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Talk to the teen about healthy dietary practices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the teen she will have a growth spurt soon.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Collaborate with the provider for endocrine testing.

questions.rationale: During the adolescent growth spurt, teens achieve approximately 20% to 25% of their final height. This occurs during the time span ending about 2 years after the onset of menses. Since this teen has already reached that mark with little growth, the nurse should assess the teen‘s knowledge and practice of healthy eating. Poor eating habits are common with this age-group. A 24-hour diet recall can be utilized but the nurse‘s assessment should encompass more than just the recall. The teen most likely will not have another growth spurt later. Endocrine testing is not warranted at this point.

questions.stem: A parent is concerned that her 16 year old is spending most of his time away from the family in his room and does not want to be involved in family activities he used to enjoy. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reassure the parent the teen is exerting independence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the parent about the teen‘s friends and activities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the teen for depression and possible suicide risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Refer the family to the community depression support group.

questions.rationale: Teens typically begin to withdraw from the family to maintain privacy and exert independence, so this alone is not concerning. However, since the teen is not participating in activities he once enjoyed, the nurse should conduct a depression assessment. If the teen is depressed, the nurse should assess his suicide risk. If these screenings are normal, the nurse can reassure the parent. The teen himself is the best source of information about friends and activities, although the parent can be a good secondary source. A referral is not warranted without further assessment.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses a 4-month-old infant and notes the baby does not follow a moving object with her eyes. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the findings and continue the assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Refer the child and parent to a pediatric neurologist.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the child for other age-appropriate behaviors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the child for signsNof RchilId abGuseBo.r nCe glMe c t .

questions.rationale: A 3-month-old child should be able to follow a moving object with his or h er eyes. However, one single abnormal assessment finding does not necessarily mean that the child has a growt h and developmental delay. The nurse should assess for other age-appropriate behaviors. Documentation should occur but is not the priority action at this point. A referr al is not warranted nor is assessing for child abuse based on the data.

questions.stem: A home health care nurse notes a parent becoming irritated when his toddler repeatedly throws his rattle from the high chair to the floor. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Teach the parent about age-appropriate discipline.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Educate the parent on age-appropriate behaviors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the parent to stop giving the rattle back to the child.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the child for signs of abuse or neglect.

questions.rationale: Throwing an object down to watch someone else pick it up is a typical be havior for this age- group. The nurse should teach the parent about how this behavior relates to toddler gro wth and development. The other actions are not appropriate in this situation. U S N T O NCLEX Client Needs Category: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching parents about actions to assist in developing a critical skill in the concrete operations phase of Piaget‘s developmental theory. What activities does the nurse suggest the parents participate with their child in? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Separating a collection of toy horses into functions each type performs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exploring a space and astronomy museum and planetarium together.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Making a scrapbook of leaves sorted by color or type of tree.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Having the child explore how common objects can be used for different purposes.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Asking the child to describe an event from several different points of view.

questions.rationale: In the concrete operational stage of Piaget‘s theory, seriation is an important task. This task includes separating or sorting objects using specific criteria. Separating toy horses by functions and arranging a leaf album by color or tree type are examples of seriation. Exploring museums does not contribute to seriation. Learning how objects can be used for unusual purposes and describing other points of view are part of the formal operations stage.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a 12 monthNoldRat Ia weGll-bBa. Cby visit. For what developmental milestones does the nurse assess this child? ( Se l e c t a l l that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Attempting to walk with help

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transferring objects from one hand to the other

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ability to roll around on the floor holding a bottle independently

choices.label: D
choices.text: Searching for objects that are out of sight

choices.label: E
choices.text: Moving from lying on abdomen to sitting unassisted

questions.rationale: A 12 month old should be attempting to walk with help, hold a bottle independently and move from lying on abdomen to sitting up unassisted. Transferring objects from one hand to the other and rolling from front to back are milestones seen around 7 months of age and holding a bottle independently occurs at 4 to 6 months.

questions.stem: A nurse is planning a community education event for parents on the topic of sc hool-aged children and the risks of too much social media time. What topics should the nurse plan to include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased bullying

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased physical activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased understanding of spatial relationships

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weight loss and malnutrition N R I G

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased aggressiveness

questions.rationale: Some of the risks associated with social media include bullying, decreased physical activity with resultant obesity, and aggressiveness.

questions.stem: A high-school nurse is planning an educational presentation for juniors. What activities are most appropriate for the nurse‘s plan to include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Video showing the aftermath of a drunk driving car crash

choices.label: B
choices.text: Confidential depression and suicide risk assessment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Same-age speaker sharing her story about the impact of HIV disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Charts and graphs showing the physical changes of puberty

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bicycle helmet fitting station to see if child has outgrown the helmet

questions.rationale: Adolescents need education on drinking and driving, suicide and depression, safer sexual practices, and physical changes that occur during puberty. A bicycle helmet fitting st ation would not be a priority for this age-group. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questions.stem: A pregnant woman in her second trimester is scheduled for quad testing. What conditions does the nurse explain are s creUe n e dSf o Nr in Tt his assOessment? ( Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood clotting abnormalities

choices.label: B
choices.text: Neural tube defects

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heart abnormalities

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trisomy 18

choices.label: E
choices.text: Trisomy 21

questions.rationale: Quad testing includes assessing for neural tube defects, trisomy 18, and trisomy 21 (Do wn syndrome). It does not screen for heart or blood-clotting problems. 3rd Edition

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse working in long-term care knows that there are multiple theories regarding aging. The one the nurse most identifies with proposes that the body‘s cells are leading to damaged organs and organ systems. This description is congruent with which theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cross-linking theory of aging

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wear-and-tear theory

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gould‘s theory on adult development

choices.label: D
choices.text: Senescence theory of aging

questions.rationale: The wear-and-tear theory states that body cells are damaged from years of hard use. The cross- linking theory relates changes of aging to cross-linked and connected cells and systems become hardened over time, decreasing function. Gould‘s theory is a psychosocial one looking at tasks the adult completes, not physical changes. ―Senescence‖ means biologic aging; there is no senescence theory of aging.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A nurse is obtaining a history from a 37-year-old patient. What statement by the patient indicates that he has met the NagUe-RapSpIr oNp Gr i aTt eBd ev eOl op men t a l task according to Gould?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient describes moving out of his parents‘ house into an apartment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient reminisces about past life events and accomplishments.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient questions his life choices such as profession and decision not to marry.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient expresses satisfaction in having his own family and successful career.

questions.rationale: According to Gould, this patient is in the midlife decade, which occurs after the upheaval of entering the adult world and questioning one‘s decisions, but prior to reconciling one‘s life and becoming stabilized. The patient who has moved out of his parents‘ house is demonstrating activities seen in the early adulthood stage in which leaving the parents‘ world is paramount. Reminiscing about the past life occurs as part of the reconciliation stage, seen in an older person. Questioning and reexamining are typically seen in the stage for 28- to 34-year olds.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse plans to develop a comprehensive screening tool to use with young adults , assessing their lifestyles and healthy living habits. What barrier must the nurse plan to overcome to make this screening successful?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Young adults may not see a health provider regularly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Young adults are so diversified that a screening tool may not be appropriate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Young adults have too many risky lifestyle behaviors to make education relevant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Young adults are too busy with their lives to see a health care provider regularly.

questions.rationale: Since young adults are at the peak of their physical development and abilities, they may not see a health care provider on a regular basis. Screening tools can be used with any population. When riskier behaviors are demonstrated, the more education is needed. Time constraints are generally not the main reason young adults do not have regular medical care.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A nurse who uses Havighurst‘s theory of development is assessing a young adult. What question does the nurse ask to provide the most relevant information about this person‘s successful negotiation of this developmental stage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Do you find yourself doing familiar tasks in new ways to accomplish them?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Please count backwards from 100 by 7s, such as 100, 93, and so on.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―What occupation have you chosen for your life‘s work?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Do you still have a good relationship with your parents and siblings?‖

questions.rationale: According to Havighurst, one of the tasks of the young adult is to select an occupation. Changing the approach one performs familiar tasks is seen in the middle adult stage. Seriation activities test fluid intelligence and are often used in middle adulthood. Although for Havighurst, achieving emotional independence from parents is important, that does not preclude having a good relationship with them.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A nurse reads on a patient‘s chart that she has sarcopenia. What assessment does the nurse perform to confirm this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mini-mental state exam

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tests of muscle strength

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gait and balance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vision and hearing

questions.rationale: ―Sarcopenia‖ means loss of tissue. Muscle tissue and muscle mass both tend to decrease starting in the 30s. The nurse assesses muscle strength to get information about possible sarcopenia in this patient. Tests of cognition, gait and balance, and sensory perception are not related.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a middle-aged adult for cognitive skills. The patient has difficulty with seriation tests. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the findings and continue the assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform another test for fluid intelligence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Consult with the provider about dementia screening.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient about family medical history.

questions.rationale: Middle-aged adults (especially younger ones) often have trouble with math processing (fluid intelligence) because of the prevalence of calculators and computers and dependence upon them to do work formerly done by the individual. The nurse should conduct other tests for fluid intelligence. The findings should be documented, but this is not the only action needed. Dementia screening is not indicated with one test result. Family medical history should be part of all screenings but is not directly related to this issue.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A nurse working with a middle-aged adult is concerned that the adult is not meeting developmental tasks associated with Erikson‘s theory. What question by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Are there community organizations you would like to volunteer with?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Do your children come to see you on a regular basis?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Do you get at least 30 minutes of exercise most days of the week?

choices.label: D
choices.text: How do you feel about reading for a leisure time activity?

questions.rationale: According to Erikson, this adult is in the Generativity versus Stagnation phase. Successful completion of the tasks associated with this stage includes reaching out to others beyond the nuclear family to communit yNgroRup sIanUdGsoSBcie.t yCat Mlarge. Volunteering with an organization would be one way to meet the task. The other questions are related to individual-oriented behaviors.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A nurse is planning a community event in which participants will be assessed for their risk of having a stroke. Which site does the nurse choose to access the highest risk population?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Community elder center

choices.label: B
choices.text: African American church

choices.label: C
choices.text: Synagogue in a rural area

choices.label: D
choices.text: Asian American grocery store

questions.rationale: African Americans have a higher rate of stroke death that do white Americans, even at younger ages. The nurse chooses the African American church for stroke screening. The other places will not have as large of a high-risk population.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: An adult caregiver for an older adult reports the adult is doing well other than sleeping more frequently and for longer periods. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the older adult for exercise habits.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform a screening for depression.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the caregiver that this is normal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the older adult to provide a sleep diary.

questions.rationale: Depression is common in the older adult population and is frequently overlooked or misdiagnosed. People may think withdrawal and excessive sleeping are normal age-related changes, but they are not. The nurse should assess the older adult for depression. Other assessments can follow because they are not the priority for this patient.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A community nurse is working with a family that consists of a middle-aged adult, an older parent with dementia, and two school-aged children. Which assessment by the nurse is most important for this family?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stress-relieving methods

choices.label: B
choices.text: Child care arrangements

choices.label: C
choices.text: Functional ability of the older adult

choices.label: D
choices.text: Knowledge of health screening needs

questions.rationale: Burnout can occur when caring for an older adult with dementia because their needs are great without lessening over time. Caring for both an older adult and school-aged children (often called the sandwich phenomeNnUonRoSrIgeNnGerTatBio.n)CaOddMs even more stress. The priority assessment for this family is methods used to reduce stress.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A young nursing student is assessing an older patient. The student nurse questions if a sexual history needs to be taken. What response by the faculty is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Since procreation is not an issue, you do not need to discuss this.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only discuss this topic if you are comfortable in doing so.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient if he/she wants to talk about sexuality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sexuality is a basic human need and needs to be assessed.

questions.rationale: Sexuality is a basic human need. The faculty should tell the student to complete the assessment. Procreation is not an issue currently; however, this does not eliminate the need to discuss sexual issues. Asking permission may be an important part of taking a sexual history, but that response is implying the student can ―get out of‖ the assessment if the patient is agreeable. The student needs practice to improve his/her comfort with this assessment.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse working with older adults encourages them to stay healthy. What instruction by the nurse takes priority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eat at least seven servings of produce a day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Get at least 8 hours of sleep a night.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Get some exercise at least most days of the week.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stay away from people who are ill.

questions.rationale: One normal age-related change seen in the older adult is decreased immune function. The older adult should place high priority on avoiding illness by staying away from people who are sick and avoiding large crowds during peak communicable illness periods. The other instructions are also relevant but do not address this age-related change. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse is performing wellness checks at a community center for older adults. Which person would the nurse evaluate as having the highest risk of stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Caucasian, 55 years of age, BP 148/92 mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: African American, 70 years of age, BP 150/100 mm Hg

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asian American, 40 years of age, BP 146/78 mm Hg

choices.label: D
choices.text: Caucasian, 74 years of age, BP 150/82 mm Hg

questions.rationale: African Americans have a higher rate of stroke than whites at any age. Hypertension is also a risk factor for stroke. Stroke risk also increases with age overall. Therefore, the person with the highest risk of stroke is thNeUoRldSerIhNypGeTrteBn.siCveOAMfrican American.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse working with an adult population knows that many age-related declines in function begin occurring at what age?
questions.rationale: Many age-related functions peak before age 30 and begin to decline after that.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A young adult asks the nurse why she should participate in health screening and educational events. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Your choices now affect your future health.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―It‘s free and full of good information.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Wouldn‘t you want to know if you had a problem?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―You can change bad habits now if you know about them.‖

questions.rationale: Health behaviors entrenched in the young adult stage impact future health and well-being. While these events are free and full of information and bad habits can be changed if the person has knowledge and motivation, those responses do not give the person useful information. Asking if the person wants to know about health problems sounds accusatory.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A young adult tells the nurse he has quit smoking cigarettes and now ―vapes‖ (uses electronic cigarettes [e-cigarettes]). What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Excellent! That is so much better for you than tobacco.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―The health consequences of e-cigarettes are not known.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Using e-cigarettes actually is much worse for your health.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Tobacco or e-cigarettes … doesn‘t matter. You need to quit.‖

questions.rationale: The health consequences of using e-cigarettes are not yet known because they are new products. The nurse educates the young adult to this fact. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A nurse notes an older adult puts excessive amounts of salt on her food. What intervention by the nurse is best? NUR ISG BN.CTM O

choices.label: A
choices.text: Teach the adult how salt intake relates to hypertension.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the older adult why she puts so much salt on food.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encourage the older adult to use less salt on her food.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Explore other herbs and flavor enhancers with the adult.

questions.rationale: Older adults tend to lose their sense of taste and smell. Food becomes less attractive to them and they may respond by adding salt. The nurse who understands this concept will help the older adult explore other flavor enhancers for food. Teaching about the relationship of sodium to hypertension is important but does not address the problem. Encouraging the adult to use less salt does not give her a strategy to do so. Asking ―why‖ questions is a communication barrier that often causes people to become defensive.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: A nurse in the family practice clinic is assessing an older adult who h as dementia. The adult daughter/caregiver expresses concern that the parent should no longer be left alone while t he daughter is at work. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Refer the family to a social worker.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encourage the daughter to look into nursing homes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the daughter there are medications for dementia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Help the daughter explore adult day care options.

questions.rationale: Adult day care facilities offer care of the older person during the working hours. This might be a good option for the family. A social worker can help, but the nurse should be active in problem solving with the daughter. Medications are available for dementia, but dementia remains a progressive disorder, so this does not help solve the problem. A nursing home may not yet be needed.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: An adult child brings his father to the emergency department and describes the sudden onset of a panic attack and aggressiveness. After ruling out an infectious process, what action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient for mental illness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform a mini-mental state exam on the patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask about risk factors for delirium.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient for illicit drug use.

questions.rationale: Abuse of illicit drugs can cause many symptoms, including panic attacks and aggressive behavior. After assessing for an infectious process, the nurse should determine if the patient has used any recreational drugs. The other assessments are not as important and can be completed later. M S C : N C L E X C l i e n t N e e d s C NategRory:IPhyGsi olBog.icCal InMtegrity

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse knows that which attributes are characteristics of the young adult age-group? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of high school graduates going to college is decreasing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: More than 88% of people aged 25 to 34 have completed high school.

choices.label: C
choices.text: More males aged 20 to 24 were married than females in the same age-group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A significant percentage of those aged 25 to 34 has advanced degrees.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adult roles for the young adult are more diverse than for other age-groups.

questions.rationale: More than 88% of people aged 25 to 34 have completed high school. Adult roles, which are influenced by many factors, are diverse and are not normed for this age-group. The number of high school graduates going to college is increasing. More females than males aged 20 to 24 are married. Only about 9% of those 25 to 34 have advanced degrees.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse is planning an educational workshop on health risks for the young adult. What topics does the nurse plan to include as priorities? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sexually transmitted diseases

choices.label: B
choices.text: Falling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Responsible alcohol use

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intimate partner and sexual violence

choices.label: E
choices.text: Distracted driving

questions.rationale: Health risks for this population include sexually transmitted diseases, alcohol and illicit drug use, violence, and distracted driving. Fall prevention is more appropriately directed tow ard an older audience.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse working in a family practice clinic has very limited time to assess patients for health concerns. When working with middle-aged patients, which problems does the nurse assess for as the priorities? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heart disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cancer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sexually transmitted diseases

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stroke

choices.label: E
choices.text: Functional abilities

questions.rationale: Specific health concerns for this age-group include cardiovascular disease and cancer. The nurse should assess for heart disease, stroke, and cancer. Sexually transmitted diseases can occur in any group but is moNr e a Rp r i oIri t yGf o rBt h.eCyo uMn g adult. Functional abilities ar e more a priority for the older adult. U S

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse tells the student that which disorders are related to the presence of free radicals? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cancer

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cataracts

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glaucoma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arthritis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Liver disease

questions.rationale: Free radicals are naturally occurring chemicals that can cause cellular damage. They are implicated in such diseases as cancer, cataracts, and arthritis. They are not implicated as a causative factor in glaucoma and liver disease.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The student of adult development learns that cognitive abilities improve during the young adult stage because of the influence of which experiences? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Physical growth of the brain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Formal education

choices.label: C
choices.text: Occupational training

choices.label: D
choices.text: Overall life experiences

choices.label: E
choices.text: Specific profession chosen

questions.rationale: Formal education, occupational training, and overall life experiences contribute t o refining cognitive skills such as rational thinking and problem solving. Physical growth of the brain and specific profession chosen are not as directly related.

questions.chapter_title: Human Development: Young Adult Through Older Adult
questions.stem: The nurse is assessing hospitalized older adults for risk factors that could lead to delirium. For which patients does the nurse plan extra care to prevent delirium? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 95 year old

choices.label: B
choices.text: On multiple pain medications

choices.label: C
choices.text: Is blind

choices.label: D
choices.text: 2 days postoperative

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intractable pain

questions.rationale: There are several risk factors for developing delirium, including advanced age, polypharmacy, pain, surgery, and hospitaliz aNtiUonR. SBIeinNgGbTliBnd.iCs nOoMt a risk factor. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: A nurse notes a patient has abnormal vital signs. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the findings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Notify the provider.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compare with prior readings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Retake the vital signs in 15 minutes.

questions.rationale: Individual vital signs are not as important as the trends. For instance, a patient may have a blood pressure higher than ―normal‖ that is normal for the patient. Trends give more useful information than a single reading. Documentation is important, but the nurse needs to do more. If the readings are significantly abnormal, the provider should be notified. The nurse may retake the vital signs if he/she is not confident of the first set of measurements, but should not wait for time to pass.

questions.stem: A patient returned from a procedure and has vital sign measurements ordered every hour. The patient‘s blood pressure has dNr o pRp e dIf r oGm B13.2/C82 mMm Hg an hour ago to 90/66 mm Hg. What priority action by the nurse is m ost app ropri ate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take the vital signs again in another hour.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the findings in the patient‘s chart.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have another nurse recheck the vital signs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plan to take the vital signs more often.

questions.rationale: The nurse uses clinical judgment to determine how often the patient‘s vital signs should be checked when there is a change in patient condition. The nurse should plan to assess vital signs more often in this patient. Since this is a significant change, the nurse should not wait another hour even though this is what the provider prescribed. It is not necessary for another nurse to double-check the vital signs. Documentation needs to occur, but the priority is to plan to take the vitals more often.

questions.stem: A nurse is told in the hand-off report that a patient is afebrile. What assessment finding correlates with this statement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure 152/98 mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: Temperature 98.4 °F (36.8 °C)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apical pulse 82 beats/min

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respirations 16 breaths/min

questions.rationale: A temperature of 98.4 °F is normal. ―Afebrile‖ means having a normal temperature. The other readings are not related to this term. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who has an elevated temperature. The nurse plans to help the patient regain a normal temperature through conduction. What technique does the nurse use?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Placing a cooling fan in the patient‘s room

choices.label: B
choices.text: Putting ice packs in the patient‘s axillae

choices.label: C
choices.text: Spraying the patient with a fine mist of water

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turning the temperature down in the room

questions.rationale: Conduction is the transfer of heat through direct contact with another object, such as an ice pack. A cooling fan would help lower temperature by convection. Spraying the patient with a mist of water would lead to evaporative cooling. Turning the temperature down is an example of radiation.

questions.stem: A nurse is going to take an oral temperature on a patient who has just consumed a cup of coffee. What action by the nursNe isRbesIt? G B.C M

choices.label: A
choices.text: Have the patient drink roomUt eSmp eNratuTre wateOr.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Return in 30 minutes to take the patient‘s temperature.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take the patient‘s temperature rectally instead.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document that temperature is unable to be obtained.

questions.rationale: Oral temperatures will be inaccurate if the patient has been drinking or eating hot or cold foods. The nurse instructs the patient not to continue drinking the coffee and returns in 30 minutes to take the temperature. Drinking room temperature water will not ―even out‖ the patient‘s mouth temperature. The rectal route is not preferred by patients and should not be used in this situation. The nurse needs a temperature and so should not document that it was not obtained.

questions.stem: A nurse observes a student taking an adult patient‘s tympanic temperature. What action by the student requires the nurse to intervene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Student washes hands prior to patient contact.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Student pulls the pinna of the patient‘s ear down and back.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Student explains the procedure to the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Student pulls the pinna of the patient‘s ear up and back. U S N T

questions.rationale: For an adult, the correct procedure for taking a tympanic temperature includes pulling the pinna of the patient‘s ear up and back. Children‘s pinnae are pulled down and back. Washing hands and explaining the procedure are appropriate.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses a patient‘s radial pulse rate to be 110 beats/min and regular. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient for causes of tachycardia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take an apical heart rate and compare the two.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the findings in the patient‘s chart.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the patient‘s health care provider.

questions.rationale: Tachycardia (rapid heart rate) is often caused by factors such as pain, anxiety, fever, or fluid volume alterations. The nurse should assess the patient thoroughly for possible causative factors. Since the pulse is regular, there is no reason to take an apical pulse. The findings should be documented, but the nurse needs to do more. The provider may or may not need to be notified, depending on the outcome of the nurse‘s assessment.

questions.stem: The student nurse is assessinNg a pRa t i eIn t ‘ Gs puBls.esC. WMh at action by the student requires the nurseto intervene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assessing apical pulse between the fifth and sixth intercostal spaces

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assessing the dorsalis pedis pulse by palpating behind the patient‘s knee

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessing the radial pulse on the patient‘s wrist

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assessing the brachial pulse on the patient‘s inner elbow

questions.rationale: The dorsalis pedis pulse is palpated on the top of the foot. The other assessment locations and pulses are correct.

questions.stem: The nurse assesses a patient‘s pulse and finds it hard to obliterate with palpation. What action by the nurse is the most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient for fluid volume overload.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the patient for fluid volume deficit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s apical heart rate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s pulse deficit.

questions.rationale: A pulse that is hard to obliterate (a bounding pulse) can be caused by fluid volume overload, or overhydration. The nurse should assess for this situation. The other actions are not necessary.

questions.stem: The nursing faculty member is observing a student taking a patient‘s carotid pulse. What action by the student requires intervention by the faculty member?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Counts pulse for 30 seconds and multiplies by two.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Performs hand hygiene prior to patient contact.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compares pulses in both carotid arteries at the same time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assesses pulse on one side then assesses the other side.

questions.rationale: The carotid arteries are the main supply route of blood to the brain. Compressing both sides of the carotid arteries at the same time can lead to ischemia. The other actions are appropriate.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who has orthopnea. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encourage deep breathing and coughing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Medicate the patient for pain as needed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keep the head of the bedNelUevRaSteId.NGTB.COM

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monitor the length of time the patient doesn‘t breathe.

questions.rationale: Orthopnea is difficulty breathing in positions other than sitting up. To assist the patient who has orthopnea, the nurse keeps the head of the bed elevated to ease breathing.

questions.stem: The nurse has applied a pulse oximeter to the finger of a patient who is hypothermic. The pulse oximeter does not provide a good reading. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Move the oximeter probe to another finger.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the fingers for good circulation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document that the reading cannot be obtained.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remove any fingernail polish present on the fingernail.

questions.rationale: A patient who is hypothermic may not have good circulation to the extremities. The nurse should assess the patient‘s circulation, and if it is poor to the extremities, choose another spot at which to measure the oxygen saturation. Moving the probe to another finger or removing nail polish will not help if the problem is poor circulation. The nurse should document appropriately but needs to do more than just charting that the reading could not be obtained. U

questions.stem: A patient‘s blood pressure is 142/76 mm Hg. What does the nurse chart as the pulse pressure?
questions.rationale: The pulse pressure is the difference between the systolic and diastolic blood pressure readings. In this case, 142 76 = 66.

questions.stem: A nurse performs orthostatic blood pressure readings on a patient with the following results: lying 148/76 mm Hg, standing 110/60 mm Hg. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient not to get up without help.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the findings and continue to monitor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reassure the patient that these findings are normal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reassess the blood pressures in 1 hour.

questions.rationale: This patient has orthostatic hypotension, which is a drop of 20 mm Hg in systolic reading and 10 mm Hg in diastolic readinNg wRh e nIt h eGp atBi e.n tCs t a nMd s up fr om a sitting or lying position. The patient‘s cardiovascular system does not compensate for this, so the patient is at risk of becoming dizzy and fainting. The nurse instructs the patient to call for assistance before getting up to prevent a fall. The nurse should document the findings but needs to do more. These findings are not normal, so the nurse should not tell the patient that they are. The patient may need to be assessed sooner than 1 hour. Analyzing

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a woman who had a right-sided mastectomy 2 years ago. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place a sign above the bed: ―No blood pressures on the right arm.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place a sign above the bed: ―No continuous blood pressures on the right arm.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place a sign above the bed: ―Blood pressures in legs only.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: No specific action is needed for this situation.

questions.rationale: After a mastectomy or after lymph nodes have been removed, the patient should not have blood pressures taken on the operative side. Doing so can cause lymphedema. The nurse communicates this to all staff with a sign stating that no blood pressures are to be taken on the right side. The other actions are not warranted.

questions.stem: Which patient assessment result would require the nurse to assess that patient further?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 40-year-old woman with a radial pulse of 68

choices.label: B
