choices.text: A 65-year-old man with a respiratory rate of 10

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 12-year-old with a pulse of 92 after ambulating in the hallway

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 50-year-old man with a BP of 112/60 upon awakening in the morning

questions.rationale: The normal respiratory rate is 12 to 20 breaths/min for an adult, so a rate of 10 would require further assessment. The other options are all within normal limits.

questions.stem: The nurse receives a hand-off report on four patients. Which patient finding should the nurse assess first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulse oximetry 96%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood pressure 102/62 mm Hg

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulse 42 beats/min

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respiratory rate 18 breaths/min

questions.rationale: A pulse of 42 beats/min is considered bradycardia and the patient should be assessed first because perfusion could be cNo m pRr o mIi s eGd . TBhe.bClooMd pressure, pulse oximetry, and respiratory rate are normal. U S N T O

questions.stem: A nursing student is caring for a patient with metabolic acidosis. The student asks the registered nurse why the patient‘s respiratory rate is so high. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―The patient‘s metabolic rate is increased from being ill.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―The lungs are trying to rid the body of extra carbon dioxide.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―The patient is trying to reduce his temperature through panting.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Patients who are acutely ill often have abnormal vital signs.‖

questions.rationale: The body tries to compensate for excess carbon dioxide (seen in acidosis) by increasing the rate and depth of respirations to ―blow off‖ the carbon dioxide.

questions.stem: The nursing student learns that the purpose of measuring a patient‘s vital signs includes which of the following rationale? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Monitor body systems functioning.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identify early signs of problems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evaluate effectiveness of interventions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determine if a cure has been obtained.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Provide a baseline to compare against.

questions.rationale: Vital signs give information on the functioning of body systems, can lead the nurse to identify early signs of problems, can be used to evaluate the effectiveness of interventions, and provide a baseline to compare against subsequent readings. They are not used to solely determine if a disease has been cured. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is delegating taking vital signs to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP). What instructions does the nurse provide the UAP? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Let me know if Mr. Smith‘s blood pressure is low.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Take Mrs. Jones‘ blood pressure every 15 minutes.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Call me if Ms. Walsh‘s systolic blood pressure drops to under 100 mm Hg.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Do you want me to demonstrate using the electronic blood pressure cuff?‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―I‘ll take Mr. Derby‘s blood pressure since he is not stable.‖

questions.rationale: The nurse can delegate measuring vital signs to UAPs if the patient is stable. The nurse must ensure the UAP knows the proper technique for taking vital signs and knows which readings must be reported. Telling the UAP to report a blood pressure that is ―too low‖ is too vague.

questions.stem: The nurse understands that which factors can increase blood pressure? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Head injury

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased fluid volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing age

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recent food intake

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pain

questions.rationale: Head injury, increasing age, recent food intake, pain, and increased (not decreased) fluid volume all can increase blood pressure. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse assessing respirations understands that problems in what organs can directly affect the process of respiration? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Brain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lungs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heart

choices.label: D
choices.text: Liver

choices.label: E
choices.text: Skeletal muscle

questions.rationale: Problems in the brain, heart, and lungs can directly lead to changes in respiratory rate and effort. Problems in the liver and skeletal muscle do not affect respirations directly. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a patient and the patient‘s family about self-care measures for hypertension. Which topics should the nurse include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase exercise on most days.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maintain a normal body weight.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abstain from any alcohol.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduce dietary sodium to 2.4 g/day.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Follow the DASH diet.

questions.rationale: Self-care measures for hypertension include 30 minutes of aerobic exercise on most days of the week, maintaining a normal body weight, limiting alcohol to two drinks/day for men and one drink/day for women, redNuUciRngSsIoNdiGumTBin.taCkeOtMo 2.4 g/day, and following the DASH diet.

questions.stem: Which patient-specific factors does the nurse include when assessing pulse? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Age

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gender

choices.label: C
choices.text: Religion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exercise

choices.label: E
choices.text: Medications

questions.rationale: The nurse should consider several patient-specific factors when assessing the pulse, age, gender, exercise, presence of fever, medications, fluid volume status, stress, and underlying disease processes. Religion is not an appropriate response.

questions.stem: The nurse is performing a morning assessment and notes the patient to be experiencing dyspnea. Which patient assessment findings would most indicate this respiratory condition? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Occasional productive cough

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulse oximetry 89%

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient in orthopneic position

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respirations 26 & shallow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Temperature 100.1 °F

questions.rationale: Dyspnea is difficult, labored breathing, usually with a rapid, shallow pattern, that may be painful. Anxiety usually is present as well. Accessory muscles in the chest and neck are used in dyspneic breathing. Many patients experiencing dyspnea find it easier to breath in an upright position. Difficulty breathing experienced in positions other than sitting or standing is termed orthopnea. Occasional productive cough and slight temperature elevation are not indicators of dyspnea. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: A nurse is conducting a physical assessment in a clinic with a partly undressed patient. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offer the patient a small pillow for under his/her head.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide a method for ensuring the patient stays warm.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Raise the head of the bed to about 30 degrees.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure there is enough lighting for an adequate examination.

questions.rationale: The important fact in this question is that the patient is partly undressed, and the nurse provides a means to keep the patient warm. All answers are appropriate for any examination but keeping the patient warm is specific to this situation.

questions.stem: A patient wishes to review his medical record. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I‘m sorry, we don‘t allow you to look at your chart.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Let me check to see if we can allow you to do that.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Yes, I can sit with you while you look at it, so you can ask questions.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Yes, all patients can reviNeUwRthSeIir NchGarTtBs a.t CanOy time they wish.‖

questions.rationale: Patients have the right to look at their records. It is best if a health car e provider is present to answer any questions the patient may have or to help interpret any information foun d within the record.

questions.stem: A clinic nurse is examining an older, confused patient on an examination table and realizes a piece of needed equipment was left outside in the hall. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the patient to lie still and go get the equipment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Call for another staff member to bring the equipment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have the patient get into a chair and get the equipment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Finish the rest of the exam, get the equipment, and use it.

questions.rationale: For patient safety, some patients should never be left alone on an examination table: infants; small children; older adults who are confused, combative, or uncooperative, and people who are physically or chemically restrained. The nurse calls for another staff member to get the missing equipment. Getting up and down off the table is inconvenient and may be difficult. Finishing the exam and then retrieving the piece of equipment also involves the patient changing locations and is inconvenient for the patient.

questions.stem: A student nurse is preparing to auscultate a patient‘s lungs. What action by the student leads the instructor to intervene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Student asks to turn the television volume down.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Student warms the bell of the stethoscope before use.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Student uses the stethoscope bell to listen to bowel sounds.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Student places the stethoscope diaphragm on the patient‘s skin.

questions.rationale: The diaphragm is used to listen to bowel sounds. The other actions are appropriate.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing a patient‘s alcohol intake. What question is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Do you drink alcohol at all?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―You don‘t drink much dNo UyoRuS? ‖INGTB.C OM

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―When was your last drink?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―How much alcohol do you drink daily?‖

questions.rationale: The nurse needs to be nonjudgmental when inquiring about topics that might be sensitive, such as alcohol or drug use. The nurse asks a neutral, objective question such as ―How much alcohol do you drink daily?‖ that allows the patient to quantify the intake. Avoid yes/no questions because they are closed ended and do not lead to further discussion or disclosure. Avoid a negatively charged question such as, ―You don‘t drink much, do you?‖; this demonstrates the nurse‘s displeasure with drinking. Asking when the last drink was is not as important in a general survey as quantifying the amount of intake.

questions.stem: The nurse is planning to educate four patients on preventing skin cancer and early warning signs. Which patient is the priority for this education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adolescent who uses a tanning bed

choices.label: B
choices.text: Middle-aged adult who walks for fitness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Older woman who sits in the sun for 10 minutes daily

choices.label: D
choices.text: Person who works indoors under fluorescent lights

questions.rationale: Research indicates that indoor tanning before the age of 35 increases a person‘s risk for the deadliest form of skin cancer, melanoma, by 59% with each exposure. The adolescent who tans is the highest priority for this education. The others do not have as high a risk.

questions.stem: A nurse has assessed a patient‘s capillary refill, which was 5 seconds. What action by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the findings and continue the examination.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient about the use of artificial nails.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient about his/her occupation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient for signs of hypoxia.

questions.rationale: Normal capillary refill is 2 to 3 seconds. Prolonged capillary refill can indicate hypoxia, anemia, or circulatory insufficiency. The nurse should document the findings, but further action is not needed. Asking about artificial nails and occupation are not warranted.

questions.stem: The student nurse asks if it matters whether a healthy eye or a diseased eye should be examined first. What response by the faculty is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diseased eye first bec ausNe iUt RisSthIeNprGioTriBty.COM

choices.label: B
choices.text: Healthy eye first to prevent spread of disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: It does not matter if both eyes are examined

choices.label: D
choices.text: Start with the eye the patient wants you to start with

questions.rationale: To prevent cross contamination, the healthy eye is examined before the diseased eye.

questions.stem: A nurse observes a patient sitting up in bed, leaning forward with the arms braced against the over-the-bed table. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient for a barrel-chest appearance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Palpate the patient‘s abdomen for tenderness.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inspect the patient‘s spine for deformities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient if he/she is experiencing dizziness.

questions.rationale: This patient is sitting in a tripod position, often seen in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. These patients also often have a barrel-chest appearance, so the nurse assesses for this finding. The other actions are not related to a tripod position.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing a patient whose chart indicates a Grade 3 heart murmur. What action is best to hear the murmur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure that the room is extremely quiet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use a specialized stethoscope with amplification.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Auscultate the patient‘s chest with a stethoscope.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Place the stethoscope diaphragm on the patient‘s back.

questions.rationale: A Grade 3 murmur should be readily heard with a regular stethoscope. Although the room should be quiet for all auscultation tasks, an ―extremely‖ quiet room and an amplification stethoscope should not be necessary. The bell of the stethoscope is usually used to listen to heart murmurs, but the stethoscope needs to be on the patient‘s chest.

questions.stem: A nurse has conducted an Allen‘s test on a patient and the result was 8 seconds. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document the findings and continue the assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Notify the health care provider immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevate the patient‘s arm above the level of the heart.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient for other signs of circulatory problems.

questions.rationale: After the hand blanches in an Allen‘s test, when the nurse releases the pressure, normal color should return within 10 seconds. This patient‘s findings were normal, so the nurse should document the results and continue with the assessment. The other actions are not needed.

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient complains of bilateral leg pain and asks the nurse to massage her legs. One calf is noticeably larger than the other and is warm and slightly reddened. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only massage the leg with normal assessment findings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Massage the front of both legs and avoid the posterior surfaces.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform a Homan‘s test to both legs prior to massaging either of them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Educate the patient on why a massage would be contraindicated.

questions.rationale: This patient has manifestations of a deep vein thrombosis, and the nurse should not massage the patient‘s legs. The nurse should inform the patient of why this is contraindicated. The other actions are not warranted.

questions.stem: A nurse is told in hand-off report that a patient opens eye spontaneously, is confused but able to answer questions, and demonstrates purposeful movement to painful stimuli. What does the nurse calculate the patient‘s Glasgow Coma Scale to be?
questions.rationale: This patient‘s eye opening would be scored 4, verbal response would be 4, and motor response would be scored at 5; this equals a score of 13.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient‘s abdomen and hears bowel sounds every 20 to 25 seconds. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid palpating this patient‘s abdomen.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the findings in the patient‘s chart.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have another nurse verify the findings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient when the last food intake was.

questions.rationale: These findings are normal; it may take up to 30 seconds of listening to hear bowel sounds. The nurse documents the findings; no other action is needed. eOssment

questions.stem: A nurse is assisting a patient who is having an examination of the female genitalia. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Get the provider; assist patient into lithotomy position.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assist the patient into lithotomy position; get the provider.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Get the provider; assist patient into Sims position.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assist the patient into Sims position; get the provider.

questions.rationale: The lithotomy position is used to examine female genitalia. It is an uncomfortable and embarrassing position, so the nurse ensures time spent in that position is limited. The nurse gets the provider, then assists the patient into the position. The Sims position is used to examine the rectal and perineal areas.

questions.stem: A nurse assesses a patient‘s lungs and notes the presence of low-pitched snoring sounds that clear with coughing. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prepare to treat the patient for asthma. U S N

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prepare to treat the patient for pneumonia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach the parent how to prevent croup.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient for heart failure.

questions.rationale: The patient has rhonchi. Rhonchi are caused by increased secretions in large airways and can be seen in pneumonia or in other conditions, leading to increased mucus production. The nurse prepares to treat the patient for pneumonia. Asthma would manifest with wheezing, croup with stridor, and heart failure with rales or crackles.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing a patient‘s cranial nerve III. What technique is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Have patient identify a common scent with closed eyes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shine a light into the patient‘s eyes to assess pupil response.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have the patient read a newspaper or use the Snellen chart.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess if patient can hear both spoken and whispered words.

questions.rationale: Cranial nerve III (oculomotor nerve) is assessed by observing the patient‘s pupil size and reaction to light and the direction of gaze. Identifying a common scent would test cranial nerve I. Assessing the patient‘s visual acuity tests cranial nerve II. Assessing hearing is cranial nerve VIII. M S C: NC LEX C l i ent Needs CaNtegoRry: HI e aGl th PBr o.mCo t i o n an d M a in ten a nc e

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a patient‘s cranial nerves and notes an abnormal response to testing cranial nerve VI. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient about recent facial trauma.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inform the provider immediately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document findings in the patient‘s chart.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have the patient frown and lift the eyebrows.

questions.rationale: Cranial nerve VI (abducens) is responsible for outward gaze of the eyes. Abnormal findings could indicate a fracture of the orbit or a brain tumor. The nurse asks the patient questions related to these two conditions. The provider needs to be informed and the nurse must document, but first the nurse conducts a thorough assessment. Frowning and lifting the eyes assesses cranial nerve VII.

questions.stem: The nurse reads in a chart that a patient has a paronychia. What assessment technique is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Auscultate the patient‘s bowel sounds.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Test the cranial nerves for sensory function.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inspect the patient‘s nails and surrounding skin.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inspect the skin using the ABCDE mnemonic.

questions.rationale: A paronychia is inflammation at the base of the nail, so the nurse assesses the patient‘s nails and the surrounding skin. The other assessments are not related to this diagnosis.

questions.stem: A new nurse is conducting a patient interview. What behaviors observed by the experienced nurse require education on this process? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Typing intently on a keyboard when asking questions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allowing family to accompany the patient as requested

choices.label: C
choices.text: Using gestures and eye contact to demonstrate interest

choices.label: D
choices.text: Closing the door to the room to ensure privacy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Providing nonverbal cues to negative thoughts

questions.rationale: During the interview process, the nurse needs to demonstrate interest in the patient by leaning slightly toward him/her, allowing requested family or friends to accompany the patient, and closing the door to the room to ensure privacy. Typing intently when the patient is talking can be interpreted as lack of interest. Providing nonverbal cues to the nurse‘s negative thoughts (such as scowling when the patient mentions something negative) does not promote comfort or trust. NURSINGTB.CO

questions.stem: A nurse is conducting a physical examination using palpation. Which assessments might the nurse note? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rebound tenderness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Crepitation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Guarding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turgor

choices.label: E
choices.text: Consistency

questions.rationale: Crepitation is crackling or rubbing felt (and perhaps heard) during palpation. Turgor is the amount of tension in body tissues caused by fluid content. Consistency compares organs for their location and size related to the norms. Rebound tenderness occurs after the stimulation is discontinued but is elicited with palpation. Guarding is positioning to prevent movement of a painful body part. In this scenario, the patient would guard to prevent the nurse from palpating a painful area.

questions.stem: A nurse conducting the general survey of a patient includes which items? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hygiene and grooming

choices.label: B
choices.text: Affect and mood

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sex and gender orientation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sexual preferences and practices

choices.label: E
choices.text: Age

questions.rationale: Components of the general survey include age, race, hygiene and grooming, affect and mood, clothing, sex and gender orientation, age, and safety. Sexual preferences and practices are not included.

questions.stem: The nurse examining a patient‘s skin correlates which conditions with which underlying pathology? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Albinism: Full-thickness burns

choices.label: B
choices.text: Peripheral cyanosis: poor circulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Purpura: clotting disorders

choices.label: D
choices.text: Jaundice: liver disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vitiligo: skin infestation

questions.rationale: Peripheral cyanosis can result from poor circulation. Purpura can be seen in patients with clotting disorders. Jaundice often indicates liver disorders such as liver failure. Albinism is genetically determined. Vitiligo is thought to be an autoimmune response. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: A nurse is educating women on breast cancer risk reduction. What topics does the nurse include in the presentation? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exercise

choices.label: B
choices.text: Limiting alcohol

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low-fat diet

choices.label: D
choices.text: Breast self-exams

choices.label: E
choices.text: Milk intake

questions.rationale: Reducing breast cancer risk can be accomplished by getting regular exercise, limiting alcohol, and eating a low-fat diet. Performing breast self-examinations will not reduce risk but may help women find abnormalities early. Milk intake is not related.

questions.stem: A nurse has finished examining a patient. What actions does the nurse take next? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Document all findings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide privacy for dressing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provide any hygiene material needed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tells the patient he/she can leave.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cleans the room after the patient leaves.

questions.rationale: After finishing the exam, the nurse provides the patient with privacy for changing back into street clothes and any needed hygiene material. The nurse also documents the findings and cleans the room before the next patient is seen. The nurse does not simply tell the patient he/she may leave. The nurse should indicate what will happen next before the patient leaves (i.e., providing written material summarizing the visit, scheduling the next appointment). 3rd Edition

questions.stem: A nursing faculty member is contrasting culture and ethnicity to students. Which statement is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Culture is biologically determined; ethnicity is chosen.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Culture is socially transmitted; ethnicity is identification with a group.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Culture is a chosen identity whereas ethnicity is biologically based.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Culture and ethnicity are similar constructs used interchangeably.

questions.rationale: Culture refers to the learned, shared, and transmitted knowledge of values, beliefs, and ways of life of a group that generally are transmitted from one generation to another and influence the individual person‘s thinking, decisions, and actions in patterned or certain ways. Ethnicity is the person‘s identification with or membership in a racial, national, or cultural group and observation of the group‘s customs, beliefs, and language. The words may be used interchangeably by some people, but this is not correct.

questions.stem: A nursing student wants to obsNervRe enIcultGuratBio.n practices of an ethnic minority community. What action by the student is bUe s t ?S NT O

choices.label: A
choices.text: Attend a community dance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Learn to cook an ethnic meal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Visit the group‘s worship service.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Observe a grandmother teaching a child.

questions.rationale: Enculturation is the process of passing a culture down from generation to generation. Culture can be taught directly, for instance, with the grandmother teaching the child. Culture can a lso be taught indirectly as when a child observes a role in the community. The stude nt observing the grandmother teaching a child is the best example of enculturation.

questions.stem: The student nurse learns that which item is the most important symbolic aspect of culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Flags

choices.label: B
choices.text: Language

choices.label: C
choices.text: Art

choices.label: D
choices.text: Music

questions.rationale: Language is the most extensively used set of symbols in a culture. The other items are important symbols but are not as important as language because words are used to represent objects and ideas.

questions.stem: A charge nurse works on an inpatient unit in a diverse city. To provide culturally congruent care to the patient, which action by the nurse would be most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using puns and sarcasm to help draw the patient into sharing information

choices.label: B
choices.text: Working to understand the socioeconomic status of the patient so teaching is culturally sensitive and appropriate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assuming a patient from a minority population does not have the economic means to pay for home care follow-up

choices.label: D
choices.text: Admonishing a Hispanic patient for showing up for a preoperative teaching class 15 minutes late

questions.rationale: Nurses need to be cognizant of the impact of a patient‘s socioeconomic status to health care practices. The use of puns, sarcasm, and colloquialisms are not easily comprehended or interpreted by those who speak a different primary language. While the level of poverty in minority populations within all cultures is disproportionally higher, it is inappropriate to base an action on an assumption. According to research, some Hispanics believe that time is flexible and events will begin when they arrive. However, admonishment is not the best approach to dealing with this behavior.

questions.stem: A nurse has been told he has many obvious stereotypes about a specific cultural group. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask to not care for members of this cultural group.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask to take care of as many members of this group as possible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Begin to educate himself on aspects of this cultural group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vow to not allow his stereotypes to show when providing care.

questions.rationale: Stereotypes are fixed ideas, often unfavorable, about groups of people. They occur because of being unwilling to gather all the information needed to make fair determinations. The nurse would benefit most from beginning to learn about this cultural group. Caring or not caring for members of this group will not help him obtain new information. The nurse should not let stereotypes show, but this is not the best option.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a homeless patient and tells the manager, ―I will make sure he doesn‘t steal food from our nourishment center.‖ What action by the manager is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the nurse she is right to monitor the patient‘s activity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inform the nurse that not all homeless people will steal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Educate the nurse that hunger might make the patient steal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remind the nurse to initiate a social work consultation.

questions.rationale: This nurse is guilty of being prejudiced against the patient, who is a member of the homeless culture. Although hunger might drive a homeless person to steal, prejudice leads the nurse to believe that all homeless people steal. The manager informs the nurse of this information, gently pointing out the nurse‘s bias. A social work consultation may be a good idea for the patient but does not address the prejudiced nurse.

questions.stem: The new nurse tells the preceptor that since she is not prejudiced against ethnic minorities, they will not be discriminated against while in the hospital. What statement by the preceptor is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discrimination can occur at the societal level.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The hospital needs more nurses like her.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prejudice and discrimination are not the same thing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is always some discrimination against minorities.

questions.rationale: Discrimination can occur at the societal level, so even though this nurse is not prejudiced, patients from ethnic and cultural minorities can still suffer from discrimination. The other answers do not explain how discrimination can occur. N R I GOBBJ:.2C1.2M

questions.stem: What does the nursing student learn about race?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is biologically based.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a social construct.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is chosen by the person.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It helps establish superiority.

questions.rationale: Race is often thought to be inherited and biologically based, but this is not true. Race is a social construct that is used to group people together based on common physical characteristics, heredity, or common descent. People are placed into racial categories by the larger society. One race is not superior to any other.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient from a culture that is unfamiliar. The patient nodded her head ―yes‖ when asked if she will take her prescriptions as ordered, but the nurse discovers the patient does not take the medication but uses herbs for treatment. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warn the patient of the consequences on noncompliance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tell the patient how the medication will help the condition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient why herbal preparations are preferred.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient to explain the meaning of the herbal products.

questions.rationale: Ethnocentrism is the belief that one‘s cultural beliefs are superior to others. To avoid practicing in an ethnocentric manner, the nurse needs to understand the meaning of the herbal preparation to the patient. Warning the patient of bad outcomes will not achieve the desired results if the herbs are culturally important and meaningful to her. Patient education is always important but is not the best answer because it does not allow the nurse to learn from the patient. Asking ―why‖ question is a communication barrier likely to put the patient on the defensive.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a refugee patient who wants the community shaman to perform a healing ritual at the bedside. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Work with the patient to allow the shaman to perform the ritual.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Investigate whether the ritual will harm the patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check to see if the ritual breaks laws or policies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Offer to call the hospital chaplain instead.

questions.rationale: Rituals are deeply powerful and have great meaning for individuals who  practice them. The nurse should work with the patient to facilitate the ritual. Investigating the rit ual for patient harm or illegality is ethnocentric; the nurse‘s first thoughts should not be on the potential negative aspects of a deeply NmeUaRninSIg fulGactiBvt.iyC.  T hMe  patient has not requested the chaplain; offering to call the chaplain shows ethnocentrism and lack of respect for the patient. While working to facilitate the ritual, the nurse will discover if any aspect of it might be problematic and can collaborate with the patient and shaman to resolve the situation (e.g., if lighted candles are needed but prohibited by policy and fire code).

questions.stem: A new graduate nurse tells the manager that she does not believe she needs more in-service training on culturally congruent care because she already recognizes that there are significant differences among cultures to consider when providing care. What response by the manager is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―You have done a great job becoming culturally competent.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Providing culturally congruent care takes ongoing work and effort.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―That is a great start but be sure to sign up for the in-service.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Cultural sensitivity and cultural competence are not the same.‖

questions.rationale: Cultural sensitivity is the recognition that there are profound differences among cultures that can affect health care. But to provide culturally congruent care, the nurse must do more than just recognize these differences. This is an ongoing process. Option B is the only one that provides useful information to the nurse as to why she must continue to work on this aspect of her profession.

questions.stem: A nurse is working with a patient who has limited English proficiency. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use a qualified interpreter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask family members to translate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use drawings and pictures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Speak in simple sentences.

questions.rationale: Qualified interpreters should be utilized when working with non- or limited-English speaking persons. Using a family member to interpret can upset the balance of power within the family, cause embarrassment, and lead to inaccuracies. Using drawings and pictures or speaking in simple sentences is not as effective as using an interpreter.

questions.stem: The nurse is working with a patient from an unfamiliar culture. After assessing the patient and the patient‘s cultural beliefs related to health care, what action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Create a nursing plan of care for the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recheck cultural beliefs with the patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use a standard plan of care for consistency.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have an interpreter validaNtUe tRheSiInfNorGmTaBtio.nC. OM

questions.rationale: According to Leininger, the nurse should recheck assumptions and findings related to culture with the patient. This is an important step prior to creating a care plan. A standard plan will not be culturally congruent. The stem does not indicate that the patient has limited English, but if he did, using an interpreter would be important.

questions.stem: A patient from an unfamiliar culture appears disinterested when the physician is telling her about options for treatment of a new diagnosis. After the physician leaves, the nurse attempts to talk to the patient and notices the same behavior. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Give the patient the information in writing to read later.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient about the meaning of the patient‘s behavior.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Investigate nonverbal communication patterns of this group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Leave the patient alone to come to terms with the diagnosis.

questions.rationale: U S N T O Communication differences can lead to misunderstandings and possible medical errors. Many cultural groups have verbal and nonverbal communication patterns that differ from other groups. Variations can occur due to personal or social situations. The nurse should attempt to learn about the cultural group‘s communication patterns. Giving the patient written material and leaving the patient alone do not help solve this dilemma, and the patient may not have the literacy skills to understand the material. Asking the patient the meaning of behavior is unlikely to elicit useful information because the patient herself may not totally understand it or be able to articulate it. This may be a deeply seated cultural custom that is simply part of who the patient is.

questions.stem: A patient in the emergency department needs an emergency operation. The patient refuses to consent and wants the nurse to call a respected elder in the community for consent. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Explain that this violates privacy laws.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Call the elder to get consent for the operation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the woman she has the right to consent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arrange for admission without the operation.

questions.rationale: In some cultures, decisions are made by men or community leaders. Although the patient may have the legal right to consent, if she comes from a culture in which gender and/or social roles do not permit decision making, she will likely refuse to consent. The best action is for the nurse to contact the elder and have him participate in the decision-making process per the patient‘s wishes. If the patient Nhas RgiveIn peGrmiBss.ioCn toMshare the information, doing so does not violate privacy laws. Admi ttin g th e patient wi thout the operation does not help her medically.

questions.stem: A patient refuses to take his blood pressure medication because ―I feel totally fine and don‘t need it.‖ What action should the nurse take first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s time orientation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the patient‘s noncompliance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Educate the patient about the medication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Warn the patient about possible complications.

questions.rationale: People with a present time orientation typically live in the ―here and now‖ and may not see the benefit of adhering to medical regimens when they are not symptomatic. The nurse should assess the patient‘s time orientation. Documentation and education are both important but are not likely to secure the patient‘s cooperation.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient from a different cultural background. What action by the nurse best demonstrates cultural maintenance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assist the patient with a healing ritual.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Teach the patient a heart healthy diet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Instruct the patient on monitoring blood glucose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discuss what self-care activities the patient is willing to do.

questions.rationale: Cultural maintenance maintains and preserves relevant cultural care values pertaining to health care. Assisting the patient with a healing ritual important to him/her is an example. Teaching a heart-healthy diet and blood glucose monitoring falls into cultural care repatterning. Discussing what changes the patient is willing to accommodate is an example of cultural care accommodation.

questions.stem: A student nurse is caring for a patient who is a refugee. The patient will take his own blood glucose readings and will self-administer a set dose of insulin but will not follow a sliding scale regimen in which the patient has to choose what dose of insulin to give. What action by the student nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the provider to prescribe only a set insulin regimen.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruct the patient on the benefits of sliding scale insulin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach the patient that strict carbohydrate limits are needed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient to explain the meaning of making this decision.

questions.rationale: The patient may have a mor eNf atUaRl i sStIi c NwGorTldBv.iCew tMh a n is common in Western societies. The patient may follow ―orders‖ from an authority figure but may feel like it is not his place to determine his insulin dose, or the patient may not feel competent in making that decision. Many explanations are possible. The student needs to determine what the patient feels related to this type of decision making before doing anything else.

questions.stem: A home health care nurse is visiting the home of a patient whose culture is totally unfamiliar to the nurse. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Perform nursing care with a high degree of professionalism.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Watch family interaction patterns closely and try to copy them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the family you need to learn about their culture.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apologize after performing tasks that make the patient uncomfortable.

questions.rationale: Nurses should observe family dynamics carefully, including communication, and try to copy them as much as possible. For instance, if the family does not make eye contact with the nurse, he/she should avoid trying to make direct eye contact with the family. The other options are reasonable, although telling the family you need to learn about their culture may place the burden of educating the nurse on them.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient from a different culture. After assessing the patient and formulating the care plan, what action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Review the care plan for acceptance by the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delegate appropriate tasks to unlicensed assistive personnel.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Go over the care plan with the charge nurse.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begin implementing the planned interventions.

questions.rationale: Care plans, with their goals and interventions, should always be validated by the patient. This is especially true when the patient is from a different culture than the nurse. The ch arge nurse may or may not need to view the care plan, but after validation with the patient, the nurse can begin implementing the plan, including delegating appropriate tasks.

questions.stem: A patient has hypertension and is on a very-low-sodium diet. However, the patient is going to celebrate an important religious holiday soon that includes many food items high in sodium. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the patient you are so sorry she can‘t have any of these foods.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Consult with the prescriber about increasing the blood pressure medications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Collaborate with the patient and dietitian to include some of these foods.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell the patient eating these foods once won‘t hurt her condition.

questions.rationale: Food has important meaning to many people, especially when they are part of celebrations, religious, or cultural activities. The nurse should collaborate with the patient and dietitian and try to find ways to incorporate some of these items. The nurse should not just tell the patient she can‘t have them. Increasing the medications or encouraging the patient to be nonadherent could lead to adverse outcomes.

questions.stem: The student studying culture learns that which are characteristics of all cultures? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Integrated

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shared

choices.label: C
choices.text: Learned

choices.label: D
choices.text: Symbolic

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inherited

questions.rationale: Cultures are learned, symbolic, shared, and integrated. Since culture refers to patterns of beliefs, actions, values, and ways of life that are taught, they are not inherited.

questions.stem: The nurse understands that which are important in the process of developing a cultural identity? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: School

choices.label: B
choices.text: Church/religious institution

choices.label: C
choices.text: Family

choices.label: D
choices.text: History

choices.label: E
choices.text: Community

questions.rationale: Many institutions and groups, both formal and informal, assist an individual in developing a cultural identity, including school, religious institutions, family, and community.

questions.stem: The nursing student learns that which are correct regarding acculturation and assimilation? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assimilation is forced entry into a different culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acculturation depends on first-hand contact between groups.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acculturation results in c NhaUnRgeSs ItoNthGeTmBin.oCriOtyMculture only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assimilation can occur at the group or individual level.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Assimilation causes a minority group member to blend into the majority group.

questions.rationale: Acculturation occurs from first-hand contact between a minority group and the majority cultural group and can result in changes to one or both cultures. Assimilation occurs when members of a minority group blend into the majority group and can occur at the group or individual level. Assimilation is not a forced change.

questions.stem: The nurse is using Giger and Davidhizar‘s Transcultural Assessment Model to gain information about a patient from an unfamiliar culture. What questions does the nurse ask that are relevant to this mode? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Who would you like present to help answer questions?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―What do you believe caused your current illness?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―How important is planning for the future to you?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Why don‘t you want to shake my hand?‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―What activities would you do to control your health?‖

questions.rationale: The Giger and Davidhizar Transcultural Assessment Model looks at communication, space, social orientation, time, environmental control, and biological variation. The questions all address these factors; however, asking why the patient does not want to shake the nurse‘s hand sounds judgmental and ―why‖ questions are a communication barrier. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The student nurse asks why he needs to assess a patient‘s spirituality when he can call the chaplain. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―This way you learn what is involved in a spiritual assessment.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Students need to perform all aspects of patient care.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Regulatory organizations list this as a required BSN competence.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―All patients should have a spirituality assessment.‖

questions.rationale: Although there is some truth to all options, several regulatory groups list conducting a spiritual assessment as a vital skill for nurses, including the American Association of Colleges of Nursing, The Joint Commission, and the American Nurses Association.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for four patients. Which one should the nurse assess for spirituality needs as a priority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: New mother, older child at home.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Faces terminal diagnosis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Needs to change medicat Ni on sR.   IGB.CM

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pleasant but quiet.

questions.rationale: U S N T O There are many cues to alert the nurse that a patient might have unmet spiritual needs, including facing a terminal illness. The nurse should conduct spiritual assessments on all patients, but this one is the priority.

questions.stem: A patient has the Nursing diagnosis Spiritual Distress. What assessment by the patient best indicates that an important goal has been met?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Observed praying quietly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Indecisive about treatment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asks nurse if God exists.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Executes living will.

questions.rationale: Patients may have spiritual distress when facing situations that threaten their meaning and purpose in life, such as in the face of a terminal diagnosis. Patients often express anger, frustration, neediness, or crying. The patient who has worked through this situation and is able to pray has best shown goal attainment. Indecision and questioning do not indicate the resolution of this diagnosis. Executing a living will may be an indication of pragmatism.

questions.stem: The nurse concerned about a patient‘s spiritual needs can best address this by which action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Leaving a note on the chart for other professionals

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calling the chaplain to come see the patient

choices.label: C
choices.text: Collaborating during interdisciplinary rounds

choices.label: D
choices.text: Informing the provider of the patient‘s needs

questions.rationale: Spiritual care must be multidisciplinary to be most effective. The nurse best addresses patients‘ spiritual needs by discussing them during interdisciplinary rounds. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Psychosocial Integrity

questions.stem: A patient is hesitating to accept a blood transfusion as a course of treatment. What Nursing diagnosis is most appropriate for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spiritual distress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anxiety

choices.label: C
choices.text: Moral distress

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decisional conflict

questions.rationale: Moral distress is cultural conflict between medical treatment and religious beliefs, expressions of concern about rejection by religious community, hesitation in accepting blood transfusion . The other diagnoses are not related. aOnning

questions.stem: A patient is finding conflict when trying to maintain personal beliefs while making health care decisions. What Nursing diagnosis is a priority as the nurse plans care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spiritual distress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired religiosity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Moral distress

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decisional conflict

questions.rationale: Decisional conflict is unclear personal beliefs, questioning of personal beliefs while making decisions, delayed decision making. The other diagnoses may exist as well, but they are not manifested by this conflict.

questions.stem: A patient asks the nurse to pray with him. The nurse is an atheist and uncomfortable with this request. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deny the request because of atheistic beliefs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Offer to call the chaplain instead.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Agree to sit with the patient while he prays.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient if he will meditate instead.

questions.rationale: Although the nurse is uncomfortable with the request, the patient‘s needs (not the nurse‘s) come first. The nurse should attempt to honor the request while not imposing his/her ideas of religion and spirituality on the patient. The best option is to agree to sit with the patient while he prays himself. This is consistent with caring behaviors and fulfilling the patient‘s needs. Denying the request does nothing to address the patient‘s needs. The nurse can offer to call the chaplain in addition to sitting with the patient. Asking the patient to change his practices is unethical.

questions.stem: A nurse is concerned about not consistently meeting the spiritual needs of patients. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Care for own spiritual needs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Begin a meditation practice.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Consult the chaplain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Read books on the subject.

questions.rationale: To avoid burnout and a decreased ability to attend to the spiritual needs of patients, nurses must take care of their own spiritual needs first. This may include meditation, consultations, and reading, but other activities can guide the nurse into a reflective practice that will allow better spiritual care.

questions.stem: The student nurse asks why spirituality is important in health care. What response by the registered nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―All people have a spiritual aspect to their beings.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Spirituality affects behavior, which also affects health.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Knowledge of it is needed to understand a patient holistically.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―People who are less spiritual have worse outcomes.‖

questions.rationale: Spirituality affects behavior, which has a direct impact on health. Spirituality is a universal concept, but all people may not recognize it in themselves. Holistic knowledge is indeed based in part on spirituality, but that does not give the student information on a concrete link. Less spiritual people may or may not have worse outcomes.

questions.stem: A patient who claims to be very involved in church is near death. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Get permission to contact the religious leader.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allow the family to stay at the patient‘s bedside.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Call the hospital chaplain to come to the bedside.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask if the patient and family want to pray. U S N T

questions.rationale: Organized religions use rituals to mark important life events such as birth, marriage, and death. This patient would most likely want end-of-life rituals as practiced in his/her church. The nurse‘s best action is to contact the religious leader (with permission) of that church or institution. Allowing the family to remain at the bedside is important but not the best option to care for the patient‘s spirituality needs. The hospital chaplain is a valuable resource, but the patient‘s own religious leader would be better. Praying with the family is always acceptable, but it is best to let the family take the lead in prayer.

questions.stem: A patient, who is an adherent Muslim, is in a burn unit with severe burns. The patient has high caloric requirements but is refusing to eat during Ramadan. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insert a feeding tube and provide enteral feedings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the provider about Total Peripheral Nutrition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Call the patient‘s religious leader for advice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell the patient he has to eat to get better.

questions.rationale: With permission, the nurse should consult with the patient‘s religious leader on this situation. There may be exceptions to the rule to fast during Ramadan for medical conditions. The other options ignore the patient‘s religious preferences, and both the tube feeding and parenteral nutrition have potential serious side effects.

questions.stem: A patient in the hospital is an adherent Muslim. Which of the five pillars of Islam can the nurse assist the patient in meeting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Praying five times a day

choices.label: B
choices.text: Having privacy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Personal cleanliness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Giving alms

choices.label: E
choices.text: Maintaining modesty

questions.rationale: The five pillars of Islam are: believe in one God, pray five times a day facing Mecca, giving alms to the less fortunate, fasting during Ramadan, and making a pilgrimage to Mecca. The nurse is best able to help the patient maintain the practice of praying five times a day while hospitalized.

questions.stem: A nurse works in a pediatric oncology unit and is feeling depressed and discouraged. What initial action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply for a job transfer to another unit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Consult with the hospital chaplain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Make an appointment with Employee Assistance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask other nurses how they deal with the stress.

questions.rationale: Hospital chaplains are great resources for nurses experiencing burnout, moral distress, or spiritual distress. The nurse can take all options, but a consultation with the chaplain is the best place to start to see if the issue can be resolved. The chaplain has a wider range of perceptions and tools than do the other staff nurses.

questions.stem: A patient died suddenly in the emergency department. Which action by the nurse best provides the family connection with others?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offering the family written information on grief support groups.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Asking the family if there is someone the nurse can call for them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Having the hospital social worker or chaplain sit with the family.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Offering to stay with the family during this difficult time.

questions.rationale: Promoting connectedness means recognizing that family and friends are providing at least some of the patient‘s spiritual care. The nurse best assists when offering to call someone for the patient or family. The other options may be appropriate but are not directly related to connectedness. M S C : N C L E X C l i e n t N e e d sC NategRor y:IPsyGchosBoi.caCl InMtegrity

questions.stem: The charge nurse overhears a new nurse telling a patient that he should no longer follow his vegetarian diet because his protein needs are so high and because ―God made animals for us to eat.‖ What action by the charge nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No action is necessary for the charge nurse to take.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reinforce the nurse‘s teaching on proper diet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offer to call the dietitian to work with the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Privately speak to the nurse about this conversation.

questions.rationale: The nurse should not share opinions or religious edicts with patients when those beliefs contradict the patient‘s. The charge nurse should counsel the new nurse about this practice. The patient may hold deep convictions about being a vegetarian and may feel disapproval from the nurse, which will impact the nurse–patient relationship. The other options are not appropriate, although the charge nurse could suggest the new nurse collaborate with the dietitian and patient to determine high-protein foods the patient finds acceptable.

questions.stem: A home health care nurse has been working with a patient who has the Nursing diagnosis Spiritual Distress. After a few weeks of implementing the care plan, what method is best for the nurse to determine if goals have been met?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient to what extent he/she feels goals have been met.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient to rate the distress on a scale of 1 to 10.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess for objective data to support goal attainment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determine if the patient thinks the interventions are helpful.

questions.rationale: For a diagnosis with a large subjective component, getting the patient‘s feedback on goal attainment is best. There may be no objective data the nurse can use to rate goal attainment. Using a scale can be a part of the evaluation, but the patient‘s determination is best.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled to have an MRI and has a metal religious icon pinned to his gown, which can‘t go in the scanner. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Take the icon off the patient‘s gown until she returns.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give the icon to the patient‘s family for safekeeping.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pin the icon to the patient‘s pillow so it can go to radiology.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Explain the restriction and ask the patient‘s preference.

questions.rationale: The religious icon has profound significance for the patient and should not be removed by the nurse. Since the icon cannot go into the MRI scanner itself, the nurse should explain the situation to the patient and get the patient‘s opinion of various options. All other options are possibilities, but it should be the patient‘s determination. Needs CNateUgRorSy:IPNsyGchTo sBoi.c aCl OI nMt e g r i t y

questions.stem: The nursing student learns which facts about religion and spirituality? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spirituality focuses on the meaning of life to people.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Religion and spirituality are mutually exclusive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Religion implies an organized way of worship.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Religion provides the structure by which to understand spirituality.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Spirituality is an individual practice that does not include others.

questions.rationale: Spirituality focuses on the meanings of life, death, and existence. Religion is an organiz ed and structured method of practicing or expressing one‘s spirituality, so they are interconnected and not mutually exclusive. Religion provides the structure for expressing spiritua lity. Spirituality can be expressed through relationships with others.

questions.stem: The student nurse learns that spirituality consists of practices that lead to connection to which items? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Other people

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nature

choices.label: C
choices.text: Religious institutions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oneself

choices.label: E
choices.text: Higher power

questions.rationale: Spiritual practices generally promote three categories of activity: connection with oneself, with others, and with a higher power.

questions.stem: The nurse who is aware of spirituality practices of major religions knows that which religions view health and illness as a process of balance or imbalance? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Catholicism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Native American

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hinduism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Greek Orthodox

choices.label: E
choices.text: Buddhism

questions.rationale: Native American, Hindu, and Buddhist practitioners believe that health and illness are a matter of balance or imbalance in the body. M S C : N C L E X C l i e n t N e e d sC NategRor y:IPsyGchosBoi.caCl InMtegrity

questions.stem: Which actions by a nurse constitute spiritual care? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Baptizing a critically ill child per the parent‘s request

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leaving the room, giving the patient and family privacy for prayer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Considering developmental stage when planning care

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notifying the hospital chaplain of a patient‘s request

choices.label: E
choices.text: Praying with patients and families when requested

questions.rationale: Many activities fall into the realm of spiritual nursing care, including baptizing an infant in an emergency, notifying the chaplain or other religious leader of patient requests for service, and praying with the patient and family. The nurse always considers the patient‘s developmental level when planning or providing any type of care. The patient and/or family may or may not want privacy for prayer; the nurse should assess the situation and not just leave.

questions.stem: The student using the FICA Spiritual Health Assessment will consider which factors? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Faith and belief

choices.label: B
choices.text: Focused practices

choices.label: C
choices.text: Importance of faith

choices.label: D
choices.text: Faith community involvement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Address spirituality in care

questions.rationale: FICA stands for faith and belief, importance of faith, faith community involvement, and address spirituality in care.

questions.stem: The nurse assessing a patient using the SPIRIT framework would ask which questions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Do you follow a particular religion?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―How involved in your church are you?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Are there any practices I can help you with?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―How will your religion affect your care?‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―What gives you hope in bad situations?‖

questions.rationale: SPIRIT stands for Spiritual belief system, personal spirituality, integration and involvement in a spiritual community, ritualized practices and restrictions, implications for medical care, and terminal events planning. Hope is a good thing to assess but is more related to the HOPE framework.

questions.stem: When does the nurse assess patients‘ spirituality? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Upon admission

choices.label: B
choices.text: New diagnosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Life-changing diagnosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: When the chaplain makes rounds

choices.label: E
choices.text: When facing treatment decisions

questions.rationale: There are many times at which a spiritual assessment is necessary. All patients should have their spirituality assessed upon admission at a minimum. Other assessments should be conducted at times when the patient is at risk for spiritual distress. Assessment should be done based on patient need, not when the chaplain is available.

questions.stem: The nurse who incorporates the HOPE framework assesses a Native American patient for which of the following? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Desire for shaman to be present

choices.label: B
choices.text: Personal use of herbs and prayers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Desire to create a living will

choices.label: D
choices.text: Power of storytelling for healing

choices.label: E
choices.text: Involvement in church activities

questions.rationale: Native Americans often use shamans; prayers, songs, and dances; storytelling; and herbs in health care. The HOPE framework assesses sources of hope, meaning comfort, strength, peace, love, and connection; organized religion; personal spirituality and practice; and effects on medical care and end-of-life issues. The nurse who knows about both topics will assess this patient for the desire for a shaman to be present, the personal use of herbs and prayers, and storytelling. A living will is more accurately assessed with the SPIRIT framework. Involvement in church activities can be best assessed using either the SPIRIT or FICA framework. Analyzing 3rd Edition

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The student learns that which is the best definition of a public health nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Works with the public.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Works in public areas.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Works with the greater community.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Works with public funding.

questions.rationale: A public health nurse works with communities as a larger whole and is concerned with specific target or vulnerable groups within that community. The other options are inaccurate.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is discharging a patient and is planning on what material to give the patient to take home. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s ability to read and understand.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Determine if the patient wants to take written material home.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the patient the same material as other patients get.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient if he/she hNaUs Ra SneIedNGforTBw.ritCtenOmaterial.

questions.rationale: Health literacy in an important concept in health. If the patient cannot read or comprehend written material, it will be of limited use. The nurse first assesses the patient‘s ability to read and comprehend written material before choosing the material with which to send him/her home. Patients may or may not realize what they need for discharge, if anything. Giving the patient the same material other patients get does not acknowledge their need for holistic and individualized care.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is planning primary prevention activities. Which activity would the nurse include in this plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Safer sex education for teens

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mammogram screening

choices.label: C
choices.text: Medication compliance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Annual physical exams

questions.rationale: Primary prevention includes activities designed to prevent a disease or condition from occurring in the first place. Examples of primary prevention activities include vaccinations, wellness programs, good nutrition for health, and safer sex programs. Mammograms and physical exams are secondary prevention measures. Medication compliance would be tertiary prevention.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse wants to volunteer for a community group providing secondary prevention. What activity would the nurse attend?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stroke rehabilitation support group

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood pressure screening at the mall

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bicycle safety class at the elementary school

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drop by nutrition station at the grocery store

questions.rationale: Secondary prevention activities are aimed at early diagnosis and prompt intervention. Blood pressure screening events are a good example. Stroke rehabilitation is tertiary prevention. Bicycle safety classes and nutrition education are examples of primary prevention.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is orienting to a new job in a home health care agency and is t old that most of her patients need tertiary prevent NioUn.RWShIaNt aGctTivBit.y CdoOeMs the nurse plan to include in the daily routine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Household safety checks

choices.label: B
choices.text: Well-baby checkups

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibiotic administration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monthly blood pressure assessments

questions.rationale: Tertiary care is aimed at people who are already experiencing a health alteration, such as those with an infection who need antibiotics. The other options are secondary prevention.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is interested in epidemiology. What work activity would best fit this role?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Studying census data to determine common causes of death

choices.label: B
choices.text: Researching population variables that contribute to disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Developing sanitary measures to prevent foodborne illness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Designing research to determine the connection between pollution and cancer

questions.rationale: The epidemiologist works to develop programs to prevent the development and spread of disease. Studying census data, researching population variables, and designing studies do not fall in this field.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The student studying community health nursing learns that vulnerable populations can be best assisted by which activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Researching their genetic risk for health problems

choices.label: B
choices.text: Working with the community to decrease health risks

choices.label: C
choices.text: Studying vital statistics to determine their causes of death

choices.label: D
choices.text: Making sure the population maintains immunizations

questions.rationale: Vulnerable populations have some characteristic that puts them at higher risk for identified health problems. The nurse can best assist vulnerable populations by identifying and working with them to decrease their risks. Researching genetic risks, studying vital statistics, and improving immunizations are all part of the solution, but the overarching priority action is to help the community decrease its risks.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is completing an OASIS assessment on a patient. What data would be most important for the nurse to assess?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of grocery stores nearby

choices.label: B
choices.text: Safety concerns within the home

choices.label: C
choices.text: Number and kind of pets

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proximity to a health care facility

questions.rationale: OASIS (Outcomes and Assessment Information Set) is a data set of outcome measures for adult home health care clients that is used to track outcome-based quality improvement. Factors that could potentially affect patient safety in the home are particularly important. The other options are not included in this assessment.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A community was devastated by a tornado several months ago. What nursing diagnosis would be most appropriate for the nurse to consider?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Social isolation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deficient community resources

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ineffective community coping

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deficient community health

questions.rationale: This diagnosis considers those in a community who may be feeling helpless, hopeless, or frustrated because of an extraordinary event. Financial and physical resources may not be available for rebuilding. Social isolation refers to unacceptable social behavior. Deficient community resources is not an approved diagnosis. Deficient community health may become a problem if sanitary conditions lead to an outbreak of disease.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: When planning interventions for a community, what action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Involve community leaders in planning.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Create a plan of action addressing priorities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determine what resources are available.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attempt to find funding for the plan.

questions.rationale: Stakeholders need to be involved in planning to ensure buy-in from the community. The stakeholders could be community or business leaders. The other actions are important, but if the community leaders are not committed to the plan, the plan is unlikely to work.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse has referred a patient to a community agency. When talking to the patient later, he states that he did not find the agency helpful. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Determine what the patie NntUwRoSulId NfinGdThBe.l pfCuOl. M

choices.label: B
choices.text: Review the agency‘s mission and scope.
