choices.label: C
choices.text: Make another appointment with the agency.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Warn the patient that nonadherence affects payment.

questions.rationale: One of the most important aspects of a community health nurse‘s role is to be familiar with referral agencies. Awareness of the scope of an agency‘s influence and services helps the community nurse to pinpoint which agencies are best able to address specific needs. The nurse may have sent this patient to an agency that did not meet his needs. The nurse should ask the patient‘s opinion about what services are needed. Making another appointment without ensuring that this is the right agency for the patient will not solve the problem. Telling the patient that payment might not be ensured for nonadherence is not therapeutic communication.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse has assessed a community and has found many areas in which health can be improved. As a result, the nurse has multiple ideas for programming. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Determine what the community thinks is most important.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use vital statistics to determine which is most important.

choices.label: C
choices.text: See what other communities are focusing programming on.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Choose the easiest problem to address first.

questions.rationale: The nurse‘s priorities may be very different from the community‘s. For programming to be successful, there must be buy-in from members of the community. Unless programming addresses a need the community thinks is important, it is unlikely to be successful.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A home health care nurse is working with the family of a patient who has Alzheimer disease and requires 24-hour care. What assessment by the nurse indicates the family is meeting an important goal for caregiver role stress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Family eats dinner together every night.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Family uses respite care one night a week.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Family investigates research trials for patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Family verbalizes exhaustion from caregiving.

questions.rationale: Caregiver role stress can occur when the caregiver(s) is unable to meet obligations or unable to take care of personal needs. Using a respite caregiver once a week gives the family a little time off to accomplish needed tasks. The other observations are not tied to this diagnosis.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The nurse has implemented a community-wide immunization program for seasonal influenza. Once the program has ended, what action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Begin planning for next year‘s program.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Send mail surveys to participants.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determine financial gains or losses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evaluate the program and outcomes.

questions.rationale: The last step of the nursing process is evaluation. The nurse should evaluate the program to see if interventions had the desired effect. Evaluation could include surveys or looking at financial outcomes, but those are only limited aspects of the process. Planning for next year‘s event should not occur until after evaluation has been completed.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is wondering if home health care nursing is a good fit. What characteristic or ability does the experienced home health care nurse suggest is most important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clinical reasoning

choices.label: B
choices.text: Organization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessment skills

choices.label: D
choices.text: Time management U S

questions.rationale: The role of the registered nurse in home health care is essentially autonomous in that the nurse must be highly proficient in health assessment (physical and psychosocial), be well versed in complex technical and clinical skills, possess strong critical-thinking and clinical reasoning abilities, and demonstrate excellent organizational skills. All choices are important characteristics or abilities of home health care nurses. How ever, since the nurse working out in the community may not have the resources (personnel or materiel) av ailable in an acute care facility and often must improvise, clinical reasoning would be the most important of the choices provided.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is a case manager for a home health care agency. The nurse often orders supplies for patients seen by the agency. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Negotiate for cheaper prices from suppliers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Investigate what each patient‘s insurance will cover.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refer the patient to the closest supply source.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use the same supplier for all patients‘ needs.

questions.rationale: The case manager in home health care must be a well-versed financial steward and understand what each patient‘s insurance will cover to maximize the patient‘s benefit. The home health care nurse serves as a case manager (coordinator) of client care, needed services, and needed supplies in the home setting. NTheRn uIrse mGusBt b.eCweMl l versed as a financial resource manager, who needs to be aware of what is or is not covered on the client‘s insurance plan.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The public health nurse volunteers for a missionary group caring for Ebola patients in Africa. The nurse is reviewing the data using analytic epidemiology methods. What information does the nurse collect as the priority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cultural norms in burial practices

choices.label: B
choices.text: Genetic variables in disease acquisition

choices.label: C
choices.text: Statistics related to incidence and prevalence

choices.label: D
choices.text: Autopsy data on direct cause of death

questions.rationale: Analytic epidemiology hypothesizes why a disease is occurring in a community and looks at cultural practices, nutrition, and extrinsic factors such as the environment for links. Genetic variables and direct cause of death data are more related to epidemiology.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The student nurse learns the ANA‘s Scope and Standards of Practice for public health nursing include components? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Team membership

choices.label: B
choices.text: Developing research

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ethical behavior

choices.label: D
choices.text: Responsible resource use

choices.label: E
choices.text: Advocacy

questions.rationale: The ANA‘s Scope and Standards of Practice for public health nursing requires participation in research, responsible resource utilization, ethical behavior, leadership, and advocacy like the standards of practice for all nurses. Team membership and developing one‘s own research are not included.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is assessing social determinants of health. Which does the nurse include in the assessment? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vaccination compliance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Family structure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Communication patterns

choices.label: D
choices.text: Roles for women

choices.label: E
choices.text: Education

questions.rationale: Income, education, health literacy, where people live or work, early childhood development, social exclusion, family structure, the status and role of women, and vaccination adherence are just some of the social determinants of health recognized worldwide. Communication patterns often are important to assess in culturally diverse individuals, families, and communities, but this is not considered a social determinant of health care.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse is studying intrinsic factors that influence the development of asthma in a community. What factors does the nurse assess? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Socioeconomic status

choices.label: B
choices.text: Genetics

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pollution in the area

choices.label: D
choices.text: Water cleanliness

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immunization status

questions.rationale: Host, or intrinsic factors are individual variables such as genetics, age, gender, ethnic group, immunization status, and human behavior that impact a person‘s health. The other options are all extrinsic factors, which pertain to environmental characteristics.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The nurse is conducting a windshield survey. What items does the nurse assess? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Types of housing available

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cars seen in parking lots

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recreational facilities

choices.label: D
choices.text: Health care facilities

choices.label: E
choices.text: Places of worship

questions.rationale: A windshield survey is a type of community health assessment. The nurse walks or drives through a neighborhood and notes the type of housing available, the presence and condition of recreational facilities, the presence of health care facilities, and places of worship among other items. Types of cars noted in the neighborhood are not one of the assessments.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The community health nurse knows that which are standards of professional performance for home care nurses according to the ANA? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Collegiality

choices.label: B
choices.text: Performance appraisal

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ethical behavior

choices.label: D
choices.text: Outcome identification

choices.label: E
choices.text: Resource utilization

questions.rationale: The ANA‘s Public Health Nursing: Scope and Standards of Practice (2013) requires participation in research, responsible resource utilization, ethical behavior, leadership, and advocacy similar to the standards of practice for all nurses.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: A nurse wants to create a community action plan for health problems related to ai r pollution from a nearby factory. Which stakeholders does the nurse consult as the priority? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Factory owners

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stock shareholders

choices.label: C
choices.text: Community residents

choices.label: D
choices.text: Local health care providers

choices.label: E
choices.text: Factory employees

questions.rationale: Stakeholders have a significant interest in a topic. The priority stakeholders the nurse would want to consult for this project include the factory owners, community residents, and health care providers. The stockholders would probably not be consulted. The employees could be a significant stakeholder if the action plan affected employment.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The nurse explains to the patient that which services will be covered under Medicare? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Infusion therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ostomy management

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal dialysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chemotherapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Grocery shopping

questions.rationale: Medicare will reimburse for professionally rendered services provided by a licensed health care provider. Grocery shopping would not be covered. If homemaker services are provided to a patient also receiving skilled care, then they too are reimbursed.

questions.chapter_title: Public Health, Community-Based, and Home Health Care
questions.stem: The home health care nurse eNd u cRa tUe sIpSa tGiNe n tBs .o nCw hMi c h goals of hospice care? (Select all thatapply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relieve suffering.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Support the patient and family.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provide grief support.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep patients out of the hospital.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lower medical expenses.

questions.rationale: The goals of hospice care include relief of suffering, supporting the family and patient, and providing grief support after the patient dies. Goals do not include keeping patients out of the hospital or lowering medical costs. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: A parent confides to the nurse that the parent‘s 3-year-old son seems to be touching his genitals frequently. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―This is normal behavior at his age.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Why do you think he is doing that?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Does he complain of burning with urination?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I‘d ignore that behavior; it‘s attention-seeking.‖

questions.rationale: Self-exploration of the body is a normal behavior at this age. The other responses are not necessary.

questions.stem: The nurse learns that spermatozoa are produced in which sexual organ?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Scrotum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Testes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glans

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prostate

questions.rationale: The male testes produce spermatozoa and the male hormone testosterone.

questions.stem: The nursing student learns that the function of the hypothalamus is to do which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cause lactation to begin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Produce spermatozoa.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Release follicle-stimulating hormone.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Release gonadotropin-releasing hormone.

questions.rationale: The menstrual cycle is under the influence of the hypothalamus and gonadotropin-releasing hormone.

questions.stem: A patient states, ―I just don‘t conform to my gender role.‖ What does the nurse understand about this statement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is a homosexual.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s behaviors are abnormal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s actions differ from what is expected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient is having a gender crisis.

questions.rationale: Gender roles are socially imposed ―rules‖ about the behavior appropriate for men and women. When someone does not conform to gender role expectations, his/her behaviors are at odds with those expected by society. The patient is not necessarily homosexual, abnormal, or having a crisis.

questions.stem: A patient asks the nurse to recommend a nonprescription contraceptive. What options does the nurse discuss?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diaphragm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cervical cap

choices.label: C
choices.text: Condom

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intrauterine device

questions.rationale: The condom is the only option listed that is nonprescription.

questions.stem: A patient has been diagnosed with a sexually transmitted disease (STD) and the patient‘s partner is angry, saying, ―She must have cheated on me.‖ What response by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―This infection may have been present for a long time.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―You need to be tested for this disease too.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Yes, you‘re right; if you don‘t have the STD, she cheated.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Now, now, getting angry will not help anything.‖

questions.rationale: Some STD symptoms may go unnoticed for a long time. Telling the partner to get tested as well without further explanation is likely to cause defensiveness. The nurse has no way of knowing if the patient ―cheated‖ on the partner or not. Telling the partner not to get angry is dismissive of his/her concerns.

questions.stem: A nurse is working with a patient using the PLISSIT model. In the LI phase, what is an appropriate activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Educate the patient on water-based lubricants.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient for permission to discuss sexuality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Instruct the patient on positions acceptable after knee replacement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Refer the patient and partner to a licensed therapist.

questions.rationale: The LI phase of the PLISSIT model stands for limited information, which would be information the patient needs to function sexually. Asking permission is P. Discussing specific concerns related to a specific medical condition is SS (specific suggestions). Referral to a therapist is IT (intensive therapy).

questions.stem: A nurse in the emergency department wants to screen a patient for domestic violence, but the woman‘s partner won‘t leave. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the questions anyway.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tell the partner to leave.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Go with the patient to the bathroom.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Skip the abuse assessment.

questions.rationale: Nurses are required to screen for domestic abuse. The nurse needs to provide complete privacy during this assessment. If the partner won‘t leave, the nurse can go with the patient to the bathroom under the guise of obtaining a urine sample and ask the questions there. Telling the partner to leave will most likely increase the partner‘s vigilance. Skipping the assessment is not an option.

questions.stem: A woman complains that her partner threatens her and berates her in front of the children. She denies being in an abusive relationship or being the victim of physical violence. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the woman to leave the abusive partner.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Educate the woman on forms of domestic abuse.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Help the woman work on a physical safety plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insist the woman take written information.

questions.rationale: This woman first needs to understand she is indeed in an abusive relationship. The nurse gently educates her on the type of abuse that is possible. Telling the woman what to do is likely to be met with resistance, plus the time of leaving is the most dangerous part of the relationship. The woman may not be accepting of a physical safety plan, since she states there is no physical violence. The woman may be fearful of taking written information because the abuser may find it.

questions.stem: The nurse is working with a patient who has a sexual dysfunction. What statement by the patient indicates progress toward an important goal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I am beginning to enjoy sex more these days.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I‘m glad my partner is understanding of the lack of sex.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I wish I didn‘t need these pills but I know they are important.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I hope one day to have a sexual partner again.‖

questions.rationale: According to NANDA, sexual dysfunction occurs when a person has a change in sexual function that the person finds ―unsatisfying, unrewarding, or inadequate.‖ To show that a goal has been met, the patient would state that sexual activity is more satisfying, rewarding, or adequate. The patient stating that he/she is beginning to enjoy sex more shows progress toward that goal.

questions.stem: A nurse is working with a patient after the patient had a heart attack and is using the PLISSIT model to address sexuality needs. For the SS phase, what action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the patient if he wants to discuss sexuality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Teach the patient positions that require less stress.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offer the patient a referral to a sex therapist.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct the patient to speak with the doctor about sex.

questions.rationale: The PLISSIT model is a framework for addressing sexuality. In the SS (specific suggestions) phase, the nurse provides information that allows the patient to proceed with sexual relation s. Informing the patient about sNexUuRalSpIosNitiGoTnsBth.aCt aOr eMl e s s stressful on the heart is an example. P stands for permission. LI stands for basic, limited information, and IT stands for intensive therapy. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Psychosocial Integrity

questions.stem: A nurse wishes to incorporate an assessment of patient sexuality into all patient encounters but is concerned about appearing inappropriate. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: State, ―I always ask my patients permission to discuss sexuality. Is this alright?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wait for the patient to bring the subject of sexuality up to the nurse.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the patient written material on sexuality, then ask if he/she has questions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell patients that if they have any sexual concerns, you would be happy to discuss them.

questions.rationale: A matter-of-fact, organized approach to sexuality will decrease anxiety in both patient and nurse. Stating that all patients are asked about this topic makes it appear to be a normal part of the assessment. The other options put the responsibility for discussing sexuality on the patient when the nurse should be directing the process.

questions.stem: An emergency department (ED) manager wants to improve care for victims of sexual assault. What action by the manager is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Designate a private area of the ED for examinations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Establish a SART team for the department.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask nurses to volunteer to be advocates for these patients.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have victims examined immediately, rather than waiting their turn.

questions.rationale: Although all options would improve care for these patients, a SART (Sexual Abuse Response Team) approach offers comprehensive, evidence-based practice for these patients.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a victim of domestic violence. What charting by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient allegedly beat up by her boyfriend.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient has several bruises on the legs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient states, ―My boyfriend hit me with a hammer.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient claims she was assaulted last night.

questions.rationale: Good charting is objective and detailed. Using the patient‘s own words, in quotation marks, is the most accurate example of documentation. The nurse should not use words like ―allegedly‖ or ―claims‖ because they seem to cast doubt on the patient‘s story. The bruises on the legs need to be measured and described more fully.

questions.stem: A patient is recovering from colostomy surgery and states, ―I guess I‘ll never be able to have sex again … who would want me?‖ What Nursing diagnosis is most important for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sexual dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ineffective sexuality pattern

choices.label: C
choices.text: Knowledge deficit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ineffective coping

questions.rationale: Ineffective sexuality patterns refer to a patient who expresses concern about his/her own sexuality. This patient is concerned about the effect of this surgery on his/her attractiveness and desirability. Sexual dysfunction relates more to the physical problems. The patient may have a knowledge deficit or ineffective coping, but these are not apparent from the question. Analyzing

questions.stem: A male patient takes a medication known to cause erectile dysfunction. What action by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: State, ―If this medication has bad side effects, talk to your doctor.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask, ―Are you having any sexual problems in your life right now?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the patient written information on the side effects of the drug.

choices.label: D
choices.text: State, ―Many men have erectile dysfunction on this drug.‖

questions.rationale: Giving the patient factual information is best. The nurse can follow up on this statement by asking the patient if the medication is affecting his sexuality. ―Bad side effects‖ is vague, as is ―any sexual problems.‖ Written information may be helpful, but the patient may not be literate and this does not allow the nurse to be engaged with the patient.

questions.stem: A school nurse is planning a sex education activity. What information from research does the nurse apply to this education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sex education should be taught in high school.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The school nurse should be the primary source of sex education.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The method of birth control that should be presented is abstinence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Parents‘ open communication regarding sex education has a positive impact on their children.

questions.rationale: According to research, parents should be encouraged to have open communication with their children regarding sex educations. The study noted sex education initiated in the sixth grade had an overall positive effectNin dRe l aUIyi n gGs e xBu.a lCa ctMi v i t y. The school-based program issecondary to parental input and supports that input by providing knowledge and skill building. Open communication would encourage discussion of a variety of birth control methods.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching patients about their medications and implications for sexuality. Which combinations are correct? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antipsychotics: erectile dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phenytoin: decreased desire

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antihistamines: increased vaginal lubrication

choices.label: D
choices.text: SSRIs: prolonged orgasm

choices.label: E
choices.text: Marijuana: chronic use—reduced inhibitions

questions.rationale: Antipsychotics can lead to erectile dysfunction. Phenytoin can lead to decreased desire and function. Antihistamines can cause decreased vaginal lubrication. SSRIs can lead to absent or delayed orgasm. Chronic marijuana used can lead to decreased desire. Therapies

questions.stem: A nurse is planning sexuality education programs. Which topics are important to each age- group? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adolescents: contraception

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adolescents: infertility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Young adults: conception

choices.label: D
choices.text: Middle adulthood: sexual dysfunction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Old age: decreased sexuality

questions.rationale: Adolescents need education on contraception and avoidance of unwanted pregnancy, STDs, HIV infection, sexual abuse, sexual orientation, and good decision making. Young adults particularly need information on conception and infertility. Middle adults need education on emotional and physical changes that occur during this age span including the empty nest syndrome and sexual dysfunction. Older adults need education on physical changes brought by age and encouragement that sexuality normally continues throughout life.

questions.stem: The student learns that which are cycles in the female sexual response cycle? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excitement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Orgasm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: Detumescence

choices.label: E
choices.text: Plateau

questions.rationale: The female sexual response cycle includes the phases of excitement, plateau, orgasm, and resolution. Detumescence is when the penis is no longer erect after orgasm.

questions.stem: A nurse is uncomfortable with a patient‘s comments, which are sexual in nature. Which actions by the nurse are most appropriate? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the patient to stop making sexual comments.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Try joking with the patient to establish rapport.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tell the patient you are leaving and will return in a few minutes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inform the patient he/she can be sued for this behavior.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Explain to the patient how you feel about the comments.

questions.rationale: There are several steps a nurse can take when patients are displaying inappropriate sexual behavior. First, the nurse should tell the patient to stop what he/she is doing. The nurse can also tell the patient that he/she is leaving for a few minutes and will return when the patient can control this behavior. The nurse can also tell the patient how he/she feels about the comments or actions. The nurse should not joke with the patient or in any way participate in this behavior. The nurse should not threaten a patient with a lawsuit.

questions.stem: The nurse has assessed a patient and determined that the patient has a sexual issue that needs to be addressed. What actions by the nurse are most appropriate? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use information from multiple sources to help plan care.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Collaborate with other health professionals to develop the plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Involve the patient and significant other in the process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use standard care plans to limit patient embarrassment.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Examine one‘s own biases before implementing the plan.

questions.rationale: A good care plan uses information from multiple sources, involves other disciplines as appropriate, and involves the patient and significant other. In matters related to sexuality, the nurse also must examine his/her own biases so they do not limit the ability of the nurse to work with the patient. Using standard care plans does not address the patient‘s unique needs.

questions.stem: A nurse is planning an educational event on safer sex. What topics does the nurse include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proper use of condoms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoidance of risky behaviors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Need for routine examinations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoidance of homosexual activity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Symptoms of common STDs

questions.rationale: Safe sex education includes proper use of condoms, avoidance of risky behaviors (and what those are), the need for routine examinations, and symptoms of common STDs. The nurse should not include judgmental comments about sexual practices; all people need information on safer sex practices.

questions.stem: A nurse understands that which characteristics of family dynamics impact a patient‘s sexuality? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Religion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Age

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ethnicity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Culture

choices.label: E
choices.text: Geographic location

questions.rationale: Religion, values, age, ethnicity, and culture all impact family dynamics, which in turn affect expressions of sexuality. Although geographic location can influence culture, it is not a specific family dynamic important to sexuality.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing factors that affect sexual function in patients with chronic diseases. What topics does the nurse include in the assessment? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fatigue

choices.label: B
choices.text: Medications

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Occupation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Physical impairment

questions.rationale: Fatigue, medications, pain, and impairments all can have direct effects on sexuality. Lifestyle is another factor, but occupation does not in itself influence sexuality. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nursing instructor asks the student nurse to identify what Robert Wood Johnson Foundation funded project that focuses on nurses‘ increased attention to patient safety?

choices.label: A
choices.text: OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Agency)

choices.label: B
choices.text: MSDS (material safety data sheets)

choices.label: C
choices.text: QSEN (Quality and Safety Education for Nurses)

choices.label: D
choices.text: ADA (Americans with Disability Act)

questions.rationale: QSEN, or the Quality and Safety Education for Nurses, was funded by the R WJ to focus on preparing nurses of the future with the knowledge, skills, and attitudes to advance quality and safety on the job. MSDS are material safety data sheets, OSHA is the Occupational Safety and Health Agency, and ADA is the Americans with Disability Act.

questions.stem: The nurse knows changes in which body system affect overall mobility increasing the propensity of falling?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neurologic

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hepatic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cardiopulmonary

choices.label: D
choices.text: Musculoskeletal

questions.rationale: Impairments in the musculoskeletal system can impact mobility through restrictions of range of motion and strength, increasing the chances of falling. Changes to the neurologic system can impair cognitive functioning, changes to the hepatic system can affect mental status, and changes to the cardiopulmonary system can affect activity tolerance. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Safe and Effective Care

questions.stem: The nurse is visiting a patient with cardiac disease who has been experiencing increased episodes of shortness of breath when exercise is attempted. The nurse is concerned that the patient‘s decrease in activity may lead to which outcome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Orthostatic hypotension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase risk of heart disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Loss of short-term memory

choices.label: D
choices.text: Worsening shortness of breath

questions.rationale: Inactivity in patients with cardiopulmonary disease can lead to an unsafe drop in blood pressure with position changes, or orthostatic hypotension. The patient already has heart disease. Loss of short-term memory is not related to the shortness of breath. The lack of activity is not likely to worsen the shortness of breath; improving activity level may help things eventually.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes conversations about safe sexual practices, including the consequen ces of unprotected sex such as pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections, are import ant to begin in what patient population?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adults

choices.label: B
choices.text: School-aged children

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adolescents

choices.label: D
choices.text: Older adults

questions.rationale: Sexual curiosity and experimentation occur in the adolescent patient population. Conversations about safe sexual practices, including the consequences of unprotected sex, such as pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections, are important. These conversations are also important for adults and older adults but are handled differently in context with their age-related needs. School-aged children may be too young depending on their age and their environment. The nurse must use judgment on when to have the conversation.

questions.stem: The nurse manager is developing a training guide and identifies which organization that is the best for resources to help develop guidelines to prevent exposure to hazardous situations and decrease the risk of injury in the workplace?

choices.label: A
choices.text: OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration)

choices.label: B
choices.text: CDC (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention)

choices.label: C
choices.text: QSEN (Quality and Safety Education for Nurses)

choices.label: D
choices.text: NIOSH (National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health)

questions.rationale: Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) was established in 1970 to provide employers with guidelines for preventing exposure to hazardous chemicals and hazardous situations and reducing the risk of injury in the workplace. The CDC is the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and provides information to address exposure to infectious diseases. QSEN, or the Quality and Safety Education for Nurses, was funded by the RWJ to focus on preparing nurses of the future with the knowledge, skills, and attitudes to advance quality and safety on the job. NIOSH, or the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health, is a federal agency within the CDC that was established to conduct research and recommend interventions for the prevention of work-related injury and illness.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating parents about firearm safety. Which parent statement indicates to the nurse a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should make sure I obtain the proper permits.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―It is okay to store firearms with ammunition loaded.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should store all firearms without ammunition.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should make sure all firearms have trigger locks in place.‖

questions.rationale: Firearms should be stored in a secure location with trigger locks in place. Ammunition should  be stored in a separate location also locked. Proper permits should be obtained a s appropriate. Loaded firearms should never be stored where children can access them.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes that a patient is using a portable generator in the house as a power source. What source of poisoning does the nurse appropriately identify?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lead

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carbon monoxide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antifreeze

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pesticide

questions.rationale: Sources of carbon monoxide include automobiles, stoves, gas ranges, portable generators, lanterns, the burning of charcoal and wood, and heating systems. Lead is found in lead-based paints in toys, buildings, and ceramic dishes; sources of lead include water from lead pipes or pipes soldered with lead, gasoline or soil contaminated by gasoline, and household dust that may contain paint chips or soNilU. ARnStiIfrNeeGzeTaBn.d pCeOstMicides are liquids.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the patient about the proper disposal of medications in the home. Which statement by the patient indicates a good understanding of the information?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Remove the label from the bottle and throw in the trash.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Flush the medication down the disposal.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Mix the medications with kitty litter, place the mixture in a jar, and put the jar in the trash.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Dissolve the medication in water and pour down the drain.‖

questions.rationale: Flushing or pouring the medication down the drain can contaminate the water system. Throwing the medication in the trash poses potential for someone to remove the medication and use it. This can be avoided by mixing it with an undesirable substance like kitty litter or coffee grounds.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that which patient has a teaching need based on statements by the patient‘s parents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―My 6-month-old daughter only sleeps with me when she‘s ill.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I do not put pillows in the bed with my 3-month-old son.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I do not feed popcorn to my 2-year-old.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I have discussed the risks of the ‗choking game‘ with my 16-year-old.‖

questions.rationale: Small children should never sleep in the bed with others because of the risk of suffocation. The rest of the statements are appropriate. Pillows do present a hazard to a 3-month-old, and popcorn is a choking risk for a 2-year-old. The choking game is a risk to any adolescent.

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching a student nurse about restraint use in patients. Which statement by the student nurse indicates a learning need regarding restraints?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Having all four side rails up on the bed is considered a restraint.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―The use of restraints has been shown to decrease fall-related injuries.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Death has been associated with the use of restraints.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Medications administered to control behavior are considered a chemical restraint.‖

questions.rationale: Restraints may be physical or chemical. A physical restraint is a mechanical or physical device, such as material or equipment attached or adjacent to the patient‘s body, used to restrict movement. Examples of physical restraints are wrist or ankle restraints, a jacket or vest, and side rails. A medication that is administered to a patient to control behavior is a chemical restraint. The use of restraints has been associated with patient injury including death and does not prevent patient falls.

questions.stem: The nurse displays an understanding of high-risk populations for MRSA when identifying which group as the lowest risk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prison inmates

choices.label: B
choices.text: College dorm residents

choices.label: C
choices.text: Team athletes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Food service workers

questions.rationale: High-risk populations for MRSA include those living in close quarters or those who have frequent skin-to-skin contact, including prison inmates, college dorm residents, and team athletes. Food service workers work together but do not generally live in close quarters or have skin-to-skin contact frequently.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that which assessment tool is not used to assess fall risk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glasgow Falls Scale

choices.label: B
choices.text: Johns Hopkins Hospital Fall Assessment Tool

choices.label: C
choices.text: Morse Fall Scale

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hendrich II Fall Risk Model

questions.rationale: The Glasgow is a coma scale used to measure level of consciousness, not falls. The rest are scales used to assess the risk for falls in patients. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Safe and Effective Care

questions.stem: The patient has a nursing diagnosis of Risk for Falls. The nurse identifies which goal to be most important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient will ambulate twice a day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will have no symptoms of infection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will perform activities of daily living.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will have no injuries during hospital stay.

questions.rationale: All the goals except lack of infection are appropriate for a patient with a Risk for Falls diagnosis; however, the most important goal is for the patient to have no injuries during the hospitalization.

questions.stem: Which collaborative team member would be most effective in assisting the nurse to identify medication alternatives that are less likely to cause drowsiness and dizziness to reduce the risk of falls in the elderly patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nursing case manager b. Charge nurse

choices.label: C
choices.text: Physical therapist

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pharmacist

questions.rationale: The nurse collaborates with the pharmacist and health care provider to identify and implement safe medication alternatives for older adults to minimize side effects such as drowsiness, dizziness, and orthostatic hypotension, which can increase fall risk. Although case managers and charge nurses might have some experience in this area, pharmacists are educated to focus on medication. Physical therapists evaluate the patient‘s ability to perform and maintain balance during routine activities such as sitting, standing, and walking.

questions.stem: The nurse is concerned about helping the patient find resources to obtain assistive equipment to be used in the home. Which team member should the nurse contact first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Occupational therapist

choices.label: B
choices.text: Physical therapist

choices.label: C
choices.text: Health care provider

choices.label: D
choices.text: Social worker

questions.rationale: The nurse should collaborate with the social worker to identify community resources for obtaining assistive equipment. The social worker facilitates contact with insurance companies or other agencies to assist with the financing of recommended therapeutic assistive and specialty devices. Occupational therapists evaluate the patient for safe performance of activities of daily living (ADLs) such as bathing, dressing, and grooming, and they make recommendations to enhance safe performance of these activities, such as the use of specialty equipment (e.g., grippers for pants, oversized shoehorns). Physical therapists evaluate the patient‘s ability to perform and maintain balance during routine activities such as sitting, standing, and walking. They make recommendations for assistive devices such as canes and walkers to promote safe performance of these activities. Health care providers order the equipment.

questions.stem: Which statement by the patient indicates to the nurse a teaching need regarding safety in the home?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I will put a night-light in every room.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I will not use an extension cord to plug in multiple items.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I will wash my throw rugs in the bathroom regularly.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I will keep all cleaning supplies out of reach of children.‖

questions.rationale: Throw rugs present a fall or tripping hazard. Night-lights help light halls to prevent falls, extension cords can present a trip hazard, and cleaning supplies can contain poisonous materials.

questions.stem: The ER nurse is triaging a patient with suspected poisoning. Who should the nurse anticipate contacting first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Family services

choices.label: B
choices.text: Radiology

choices.label: C
choices.text: Poison Control Center

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respiratory

questions.rationale: If poisoning is suspected, the National Poison Control Center should be contacted immediately. This information will be needed to determine treatment. Respiratory may be needed, and radiology and family services may also be needed, but that will be determined after the treatment plan is determined.

questions.stem: The staff nurse knows that many health care facilities use the fire emergency response defined by which acronym?

choices.label: A
choices.text: RACE

choices.label: B
choices.text: PASS

choices.label: C
choices.text: PACE

choices.label: D
choices.text: QSEN

questions.rationale: RACE stands for rescue, alarm, contain, and extinguish. QSEN is the Quality and Safety Education for Nurses. PASS is pull, aim, squeeze, and sweep for fire extinguishers. PACE is not a health care acronym.

questions.stem: The nurse is ambulating a patient back from the bathroom when the patient begins to have a seizure. Which action should the nurse do first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lower the patient to the floor if standing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Move sharp or hard objects away from the patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turn the patient‘s head to the side to prevent aspiration.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attempt to place a tongue blade to prevent choking.

questions.rationale: During a seizure, a patient should be protected from injury by first lowering the patient to the ground if standing. The nurse should then place the head on a soft surface and turn it to the side to prevent aspiration and move sharp or hard objects out of the way. The nurse should never attempt to force any object into a seizing patient‘s mouth.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a confused, combative patient. Which action would be considered last by the nurse to control behavNior Rof thIe clGi entB? .C M

choices.label: A
choices.text: Orient the patient frequentlUy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply restraints. S N T O

choices.label: C
choices.text: Move the patient to a room close to the nurse‘s station.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage the family to spend time with the patient.

questions.rationale: All alternatives to physical restraints should be considered prior to their use.

questions.stem: The nurse knows which method to be an appropriate way to tie restraints?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Knot tied to the bed frame

choices.label: B
choices.text: Quick-release knot tied to the side rail

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bow tied to the bed rail

choices.label: D
choices.text: Quick-release ties attached to the bed frame

questions.rationale: Restraints should never be tied in a knot because the knot may prohibit a quick exit in the event of an emergency requiring evacuation. Instead, use quick-release ties or mechanisms such as buckles. Restraints are never be tied to side rails because injuries may result when they are raised or lowered. They should be tied to a stable part of the bed such as the frame.

questions.stem: Which statement by the nurse correctly identifies the UAP role in patient restraint use?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―The UAP can perform initial assessment.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―The UAP can apply a restraint.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―The UAP can assist with applying and monitoring of a physical restraint.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―The UAP can contact the health care provider and request an order for restraints.‖

questions.rationale: The UAP cannot perform the initial assessment, and most facilities require that a registered nurse or licensed practical nurse. Applying a restraint. The health care provider should be contacted by the nurse, not the UAP. The UAP can assist with applying the restraint and can perform monitoring checks under the direction of a Registered Nursing.

questions.stem: The nurse is explaining the National Patient Safety Goals (NPSG) to the student nurse. Which answers indicate that the student has a good understanding of these goals? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The NPSG‘s focus on treating chronic infections quickly

choices.label: B
choices.text: The NPGS‘s focus on improving staff communication

choices.label: C
choices.text: The NPGS‘s focus on using medications safely

choices.label: D
choices.text: The NPGS‘s focus on identifying patients correctly

questions.rationale: The NPSG focus on specific goals each year. The goals for 2018 included: identify patients correctly, improve staff communication, improve the safety of using medications, reduce harm associated with clinical alarm systems, reduce risk of health care–associated infections and the hospital identifies safety risks inherent in its patient population. Although treating chronic infections quickly is important, it is not an NPSG.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing education to a cardiac patient who has multiple life stressors that are impacting the patient‘s health. Which statements by the patient indicate a good understanding of actions that can be taken to reduce stressors? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should change my job.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should plan some downtime.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should meet with a financial counselor.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should talk with my family about my situation.‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―I should make my family go to counseling with me.‖

questions.rationale: In adulthood, life stressors such as financial concerns, work-related demands, and efforts to balance work with family life are common challenges that can take a physical toll on the body. Individuals should plan relaxation periods or vacations. Meeting with financial counselors and talking with family can help to achieve that balance. Changing jobs may be beneficial but could also create more stress and forcing family to go to counseling may also not be a wise choice.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing education to a community group on environmental safety. Which safety measures are effective in improving their environmental safety? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use of night-lights throughout the home

choices.label: B
choices.text: Illumination of stairwells and pathways

choices.label: C
choices.text: Installation of motion-activated lighting on the exterior of the home

choices.label: D
choices.text: Application of wax to all floors to increase shine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Staying indoors when air pollution is high

questions.rationale: Inadequate lighting presents safety concerns in home, work, community, and health care environments. For an individual to safely and successfully navigate pathways and perform various activities while avoiding potential obstacles and hazards, the environment must be well illuminated. Well-lit, glare-free halls, stairways, rooms, and work spaces help to reduce the risk of tripping, slipping, and falling. Night-lights reduce the risk of injuries to children, guests, and older adults. Lighting the exterior of the house will also reduce the risk of falling. Staying indoors during episodes when air pollution is high can help prevent chronic lung disease. Waxed floors are s l i pNp e rRy . IGB.CM  U S N T O 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which term to identify the second line of defense that leads to local capillary dilation and leukocyte infiltration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal flora

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inflammatory response

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immune response

choices.label: D
choices.text: Humoral immunity

questions.rationale: The second line of defense is the inflammatory response. Inflammation is a local response to cellular injury or infection that includes capillary dilation and leukocyte infiltration. Normal flora is the body‘s first line of defense. The immune response is the body‘s attempt to protect itself from foreign and harmful substances. Humoral immunity is a defense system that involves white blood cells (B lymphocytes) that produce antibodies in response to antigens or pathogens circulating in the lymph and blood. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse knows that the a n tNi ge nR- a nIt iboGd y rBea.ctCionMis an example of what type of immunity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Humoral

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cellular

choices.label: C
choices.text: Innate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Passive

questions.rationale: U S N T O Humoral immunity is a defense system that involves antibodies and white blood cells that are produced to fight antigens. Cellular immunity involves defense by white blood cells against any microorganisms that the body does not recognize as its own. The innate (nonspecific) immune system provides immediate defense against foreign antigens. Passive immunity occurs when a person receives an antibody produced in another body. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse uses what term to identify a disease-causing organism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pathogen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normal flora

choices.label: C
choices.text: Germ

choices.label: D
choices.text: Microorganism

questions.rationale: Infectious agents include any disease-causing agent and are called pathogens. They include bacteria, fungi, viruses, and parasites. Normal flora is a group of non–disease-causing microorganisms that live in or on the body. Germ is a term used for microorganism. A microorganism is bacteria, fungi, or protozoa. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is explaining to the patient why antibiotics are being administered. The answer would be correct if the nurse stated antibiotics are effective against which microorganism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Viruses

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fungi

choices.label: C
choices.text: Parasites

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bacteria

questions.rationale: Antibiotics are effective against bacteria, and exact antibiotic sensitivity is tested so that appropriate antibiotics are prescribed. Infections that are caused by fungi are treated with antifungal medications. Certain antiviral medications are used to manage the symptoms of a viral infection. These medications, if given during the early phases of illness, can decrease the amount of time that the patient has viral symptoms. Treatment for parasitic infections varies depending on type of parasite.

questions.stem: The nurse anticipates correctly that what medication category would be ordered to treat athlete‘s foot?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antiviral

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antibiotic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antihelminth

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antifungal

questions.rationale: The nurse would expect to treat athlete‘s foot with an antifungal because it is a fungal infection. An antibiotic treats bacterial infections, antivirals treat viral infect ions, and antihelminth treats parasitic worms.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes that the stethoscope most correctly represents which possible link in the chain of infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Source

choices.label: B
choices.text: Portal of exit

choices.label: C
choices.text: Portal of entry

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mode of transmission

questions.rationale: The stethoscope would be a means for the pathogen to travel from source t o host. The source is the reservoir or host. The portal of exit is where the pathogen escapes  from the reservoir of infection, and the portal of entry is where the microorganism enters the susceptible host.

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching a group of patients about diseases that are transmitted by ticks. Which term would the nurse use when identifying the function of a tick in spreading disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vectors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bacteria

choices.label: C
choices.text: Viruses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fungi

questions.rationale: Vectors carry pathogens from one host to another. Bacteria are single-cell organisms. Viruses are the smallest organisms. Fungi are single-cell organisms that can cause infection.

questions.stem: What response would the nurse provide to correctly identify the most effective method to prevent hospital-acquired infections?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use of sterile technique

choices.label: B
choices.text: Isolation protocols

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibiotic use

choices.label: D
choices.text: Handwashing

questions.rationale: Handwashing is the most effective method to prevent hospital-acquired infections. Sterile technique is only used for certain procedures and isolation protocols are used for patients already infected or for protective isolation in immune-compromised patients and are not used for every patient. Antibiotics are used to treat infections.

questions.stem: The nurse correctly identifies which patient as having the greatest risk for infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An 80-year-old male with an enlarged prostate

choices.label: B
choices.text: A 24-year-old female long-distance runner

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 50-year-old obese male

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 40-year-old sexually active female

questions.rationale: The 80-year-old male has more risk factors because he is elderly and has increased risk of urinary tract infection related to prostate enlargement, so he has two risk factors. A 24-year-old female runner is likely healthy with no additional risk factors. The 50-year-old obese male has one additional risk factor. The 40-year-old sexually active female may not have additional risk factors if she is using protection and does not have multiple partners. U S N T O Analyzing

questions.stem: The nurse understands that which set of vital signs most likely indicates infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: T: 98.6 °F (37.0 °C), P: 75 beats/min, R: 18 breaths/min, BP 120/80 mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: T: 99 °F (37.2 °C), P: 80 beats/min, R: 18 breaths/min, BP: 110/70 mm Hg

choices.label: C
choices.text: T: 100.5 °F (38 °C), P: 96 beats/min, R: 22 breaths/min, BP: 150/100 mm Hg

choices.label: D
choices.text: T: 98.9 °F (37.1 °C), P: 66 beats/min, R: 18 breaths/min, BP: 98/62 mm Hg

questions.rationale: With infection, temperature will rise and blood pressure will increase along with pulse and respiratory rate.

questions.stem: A patient admitted after abdominal surgery has a Nursing diagnosis of risk for infection. The nurse identifies which goal to be most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient will ambulate length of hallway this shift.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will consume 20% of meals by the end of the week.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient‘s incision will be without signs or symptoms of infection at discharge.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will verbalize need to stop antibiotics medication when symptom free.

questions.rationale: Maintaining skin integrity is anNappRropIriatGe goBal.foCr thMis patient to ensure the patient does not develop a wound infection. A m bu l a ti n g w i l l assis t in preventing skin breakdown be getting the patient out of bed, but it is not the priority goal for a patient with an incision. Consuming only 20% of meals will not ensure adequate nutrition and verbalizing the end of antibiotic administration to be when symptoms end is inappropriate. Antibiotics should be taken until the prescription is complete.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is comatose. When preforming oral hygiene, which interval is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every shift

choices.label: B
choices.text: Twice daily

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every 4 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Daily

questions.rationale: Oral care should be performed every 4 hours to prevent the colonization of bacteria. Less often than every 4 hours is not effective.

questions.stem: The nurse knows which skill does not require the use of sterile technique?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NG tube insertion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Foley catheterization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tracheostomy care

choices.label: D
choices.text: PICC line insertion

questions.rationale: NG tube insertion requires a clean, not sterile, technique as the gastrointestinal tract is not sterile. Use strict aseptic technique when inserting an intravenous (IV) or Foley catheter and when performing suctioning of the lower airway.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which situation to be inappropriate to use alcohol-based hand sanitizer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient with pneumonia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient with Clostridium difficile

choices.label: C
choices.text: Status post-appendectomy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient with HIV

questions.rationale: Soap and water must be used to thoroughly clean hands if there is any visible soiling or dirt and with certain infections such as Clostridium difficile and vancomycin-resistant enterococci when preparing for a sterile or surgical procedure, before and after eating, and after using the restroom. In the other situations, a hand sanitizer is as effective as soap and water. U NSR ING BT.C MO

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing to perform suctioning on a new tracheostomy with the potential for forceful expulsion of secretions and identifies what PPE (personal protective equipment) should be worn?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gloves and eyewear

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gloves, gown, and mask

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eyewear and gown

choices.label: D
choices.text: Eyewear, mask, gown, and gloves

questions.rationale: Use gloves routinely when blood or body fluid might be present. If splashing is possible, use your nursing judgment about what other PPE might be necessary. Forceful expulsion of secretions would require all PPE—gown, mask, eyewear, and gloves—to provide adequate protection.

questions.stem: Which isolation precaution should the nurse implement for the patient who has been diagnosed with hepatitis A?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Airborne

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contact

choices.label: C
choices.text: Droplet

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protective

questions.rationale: Contact precautions are used when a known or suspected contagious disease may be present and is transmitted through direct contact with the patient or indirect contact with items in the patient‘s environment. Airborne precautions are used when known or suspected contagious diseases can be transmitted by means of small droplets or particles that can remain suspended in the air for prolonged periods. Droplet precautions are used when known or suspected contagious diseases can be transmitted through large droplets suspended in the air. Protective isolation is used for patients who have compromised immune systems.

questions.stem: When the patient is diagnosed with pertussis, which isolation precaution should the nurse implement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Droplet

choices.label: B
choices.text: Airborne

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contact

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protective

questions.rationale: Droplet precautions are used when known or suspected contagious diseases can be transmitted through large droplets suspenNdedRin It h e GaUi r. SBCo.nCtNactMprecaution s are used when a known orsuspected contagious disease may be present and is transmitted through direct contact with the patient or indirect contact with items in the patient‘s environment. Airborne precautions are used when known or suspected contagious diseases can be transmitted by means of small droplets or particles that can remain suspended in the air for prolonged periods.

questions.stem: When teaching a student nurse about removing PPE, the nurse would include which correct order of equipment removal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gloves, eyewear, gown, and mask

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mask, eyewear, gown, and gloves

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gown, mask, eyewear, and gloves

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gloves, gown, mask, and eyewear

questions.rationale: When removing PPE, gloves, which are contaminated, are removed first to prevent contamination of the face and eyes during removal of the mask and to prevent spread of microorganisms. Eyewear should then be removed, followed by the gown and finally the mask. Control

questions.stem: When the nurse is wearing sterile gloves, which action would result in the gloves becoming nonsterile?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fold gloved hands until procedure begins.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Change a dressing using aseptic technique.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place sterile gloved hands below waist.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use correct protocol when donning sterile gloves.

questions.rationale: Once the hands have been placed below the waist, they can longer be considered sterile or free from organisms. Asepsis refers to freedom from disease-causing contamination. All other choices maintain asepsis.

questions.stem: The nurse is planning care for an elderly patient. The nurse recognizes the patient is at risk for respiratory infections based on which factors? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased cough reflex

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased lung elasticity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased activity of the cilia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Abnormal swallowing reflex

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased  sputum  produc NtioUnRSINGTB.COM

questions.rationale: The elderly are at an increased risk for respiratory infections because of d ecreased cough reflex, decreased elastic recoil of the lungs, decreased activity of the cilia, and  abnormal swallowing reflex. They do not generally have increased sputum production.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing education to a patient who is being discharged home on antibiotic therapy. Which statement(s) by the patient indicates further education is needed? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should take antibiotics every time I am sick.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should take all antibiotics as prescribed.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should save all unused antibiotics.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should stop taking antibiotics when I feel better.‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―If I develop a rash while taking these I will call the provider.‖

questions.rationale: The overuse of antibiotics and inappropriate use, such as not completing prescriptions and sharing antibiotics, has led to increased resistance. Taking antibiotics as prescribed helps to ensure the infection will be treated correctly. A rash may indicate an allergic reaction and the patient needs to report this to the provider. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which statements by the student nurse regarding handwashing indicate a need for further education? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wash hands first, then wrists.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rinse from fingertips to wrists.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dry using a scrubbing motion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turn off faucet with clean, dry paper towel.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dry the hands in the same order as washing them.

questions.rationale: When washing hands, first wet the wrists and hands; with fingers pointing downward, first wash the wrists and then the hands below the wrists. Then apply soap, lather, and rub using a circular motion for 15 to 20 seconds. When rinsing, rinse from wrist to fingertips, keeping hands with fingers pointing downward. Using clean paper towels, dry thoroughly in the same order (from wrists to fingers) using a patting motion. Turn off the faucet with a clean, dry paper towel.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that standard precautions are indicated for which group(s) of patients? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: All patients

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patients with HIV

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patients with MRSA

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patients with tuberculosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patients who are bleeding

questions.rationale: The nurse can take steps at any link in the chain to halt the spread of infection. Standard precautions are used with all patients to limit direct exposure to blood and body fluids. The other choices are additional precautions such as airborne precautions are used with patients who have diseases such as tuberculosis and contact precautions with patients who have MRSA.

questions.stem: The patient is on protective precautions. The nurse knows which statements are true regarding these precautions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: A positive-pressure room with a HEPA filtration system is required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Special respirator masks should be available and one size fits all.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No live plants are allowed in the room.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient may eat any foods desired.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Everyone entering the room wears a mask.

questions.rationale: Protective precautions may require a positive-pressure room. No live plants, fresh flowers, fresh raw fruit or vegetables, sushi, or blue cheese may be brought into the room because they may harbor bacteria and fungi. The patient cannot eat just any foods because some are restricted. A mask is required for anyone entering the room and for the patient if leaving the room. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse knows that which statement is true regarding the importance of hygiene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse can assess other body systems during the bath.

choices.label: B
choices.text: UAPs perform hygiene because there is no benefit of nurses doing this care.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The mucous membranes of the lips, nostrils, anus, vagina, and urethra are not a part of the integumentary system when providing hygiene.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The main purpose of bathing is to decrease the patient‘s body odor.

questions.rationale: The bath is an excellent opportunity for the nurse to assess multiple body systems. Although the UAP can perform hygiene, there is benefit to the nurse doing it because of the ability to assess the patient. The mucous membranes are a part of the integumentary system, and bathing cleanses the skin, reduces odor, provides comfort, and contributes to the patient‘s health and well-being.

questions.stem: Excessively dry skin can lead to cracks and openings in the integumentary system. Based on this, what is the most applicable Nursing diagnosis for a patient with excessively dry skin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired Health M ai n t e nNa nUc Re SINGTB.CO

choices.label: B
choices.text: Risk for Injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk for infection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acute pain

questions.rationale: Any interruption in the skin, which is the body‘s first line of defense, can potentially lead to infection. Impaired health maintenance could have dry skin as a symptom. Acute pain and risk for injury are not appropriate.

questions.stem: The nurse correctly identifies which patient as having the highest risk for injury related to temperature of water when bathing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient with asthma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient with a stroke

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient with diabetes

questions.rationale: Patients with neurologic deficits such as peripheral neuropathy resulting from diabetes may not be able to identify extremes of hot and cold. Patients with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder and asthma are not likely to be injured by temperature extremes. Patients with a stroke may have some alteration in sensation on one side of their body but can compensate by using the other side, and they are at less risk than a patient with diabetes.

questions.stem: Which tool is used by the nurse to determine risk for impaired skin integrity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Braden scale

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glasgow scale

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vanderbilt scale

choices.label: D
choices.text: MMSE scale

questions.rationale: The Braden scale is used to determine risk for impaired skin integrity: The Glasgow is a coma scale, the Vanderbilt is a behavior scale, and the MMSE is the mini-mental ex am to determine cognitive status.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing care to a post-stroke patient on the rehabilitation floor with a nursing diagnosis of Impaired health maintenance. Which goal is most appropriate on day one?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient will ambulate indNepUenRdSenItlNyGtwTicBe.aCdOayM.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will perform all own ADLs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will consume 75% of all meals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will begin to perform 25% of own ADLs.

questions.rationale: The patient needs to work toward achieving as much independence in self-care as possible; starting with 25% in a post-stroke patient on day one is more achievable than 100%. Ambulating and eating meals are not goals for a problem with self-care.

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing to give a patient a complete bed bath. What area of the body should be bathed first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hands

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eyes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Face

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arms

questions.rationale: The nurse should start washing the patient‘s eye area, using a washcloth without soap, followed by the patient‘s face, hands, and arms.

questions.stem: The UAP asks why the arms are washed from distal to proximal. Which response by the nurse is appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To promote circulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: To maintain asepsis

choices.label: C
choices.text: To maintain comfort

choices.label: D
choices.text: To maintain tradition

questions.rationale: Washing from distal to proximal promotes circulation and blood return. Asepsis i s the state of being free from disease-causing contaminates. There is no difference in comfort. Tradition i s a custom.

questions.stem: The nurse has assisted the patient to wash the hands, face, axillae, and perineal area. What type of bath does the nurse chart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sink bath

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete bed bath

choices.label: C
choices.text: Partial bed bath

choices.label: D
choices.text: Shower

questions.rationale: A partial bed bath is performed when only part of the body is washed. A complete bed bath is for patients who are completely bedridden or are totally dependent on others for care. A shower is usually for patients who are strong enough to shower independently. A sink bath is when the patient washes while standing or sitting in front of a bath basin or sink.

questions.stem: The nurse is performing perineal care for the uncircumcised patient. Which action does the nurse take?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Does not move the foreskin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Retracts the foreskin, pulling it away from the body.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leaves the foreskin retracted, allowing it to return to position naturally after care.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Retracts the foreskin and returns it to its natural position after cleaning, rinsing, and drying.

questions.rationale: The foreskin must be returned to its normal position after cleaning to prevent contraction and swelling. It is okay to move the foreskin to clean the penis. To retract the foreskin, gently push it toward the body. It should be returned to its position by the nurse, not left to return on its own.

questions.stem: Which member of the collaborative team is most appropriate to cut the toenails of a diabetic patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nurse

choices.label: B
choices.text: Physical therapist

choices.label: C
choices.text: Occupational therapist

choices.label: D
choices.text: Podiatrist

questions.rationale: Patients with diabetes are usually seen by a podiatrist or diabetic specialist for foot care needs. Nurses can trim toenails of patients not at risk for infection.
