Physical therapists provide services that restore function and mobility. Occupational therapists use treatments to maintain or restore daily living and work skills.

questions.stem: When providing the patient with routine hygienic care, which action would the nurse omit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Massage the back with lotion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral care with a toothbrush

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shaving with a disposable razor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ear hygiene with cotton-tipped applicators

questions.rationale: Cotton-tipped swabs or appliNcatoRrs sIh o uGl d nBot.bCe u sMe d in the ears for cleaning because this can push wax farther into the ears. A backUmassage may be given as part of a complete bed bath. Oral care is an essential nursing intervention that provides patient comfort, removes plaque and bacteria, reduces the risk of tooth decay, and decreases halitosis. Oral care includes brushing the teeth and tongue, flossing, rinsing the mouth, and cleaning dentures. Shaving a patient may be part of hygienic care and can be done with a disposable or electric razor.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with swallowing concerns and decreased level of consciousness. The nurse knows to put the patient in what position for oral care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High Fowler‘s

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Side-lying

choices.label: D
choices.text: Low Fowler‘s

questions.rationale: The side-lying position should be used to prevent aspiration. The high Fowler‘s, low Fowler‘s, and prone position will not prevent aspiration.

questions.stem: What action by the nurse is inappropriate regarding denture care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carrying the dentures to the sink wrapped in a paper towel

choices.label: B
choices.text: Placing a towel in the sink and brushing the dentures over the towel

choices.label: C
choices.text: Brushing the dentures as the nurse would the teeth of a conscious patient

choices.label: D
choices.text: Applying adhesive, then inserting upper and then lower dentures

questions.rationale: Dentures should not be wrapped in a paper towel; they should be placed in the denture cup to carry them to the sink. The towel prevents the dentures from being damaged if the teeth are dropped. The nurse can brush the dentures as she would the teeth of a conscious patient. Apply denture adhesive (if used) and insert the dentures, inserting first the upper and then the lower plates, using 4 4 inch gauze.

questions.stem: What statement by the nurse is true regarding oral care of patients on anticoagulants?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use an electric toothbrush daily.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid oral care.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use mouthwash only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use a soft-bristled toothbrush.

questions.rationale: Oral care is important regardless of medication, but a soft-bristled toothbrush should be used related to increased risk of bleeding for any patient on an anticoagulant. An electric toothbrush is too aggressive,NanUdRmSoIutNhwGaTsBh .is Cn oOt Ma dequate.

questions.stem: The nurse is assisting a patient to insert contacts and a contact is dropped. What action should occur next?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Moisten the finger with lens solution and gently touch it to pick it up.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Moisten the contact lens with tap water and pick it up.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pick it up and insert the contact lens.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discard the contact lens.

questions.rationale: If a lens is dropped, do the following: (1) moisten a finger with the lens solution, and then gently touch the lens with the moistened finger to pick it up. (2) Clean, rinse, and disinfect the lens to avoid a potential eye infection from any microorganisms that might have adhered to the lens. The contact lens does not need to be discarded and tap water should not be used for contact lens.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which statement by the patient indicates a teaching need?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I use bobby pins to remove excessive ear wax.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I use soap and a warm cloth to clean the outside of my ear.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―My doctor sometimes gives me oil drops for my ears.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I never use Q-Tips.‖

questions.rationale: Washing the ear with a washcloth and soap is sufficient in most patients. If the patient has a buildup of wax, or cerumen, the health care provider may order special oil drops to soften the wax before irrigating the ear canal. Do not try to remove the wax using a cotton-tipped applicator because this can push the wax farther into the ear canal. Caution patients to never insert anything sharp into the ear, such as bobby pins. Sharp objects can rupture the tympanic membrane.

questions.stem: The nurse is asked to shave a patient who is taking warfarin (Coumadin). What is the most appropriate action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Refuse to shave the patient because he is on an anticoagulant.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shave as usual with a safety razor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offer to wax rather than shave the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use an electric razor.

questions.rationale: Patients on anticoagulants should use an electric razor for shaving to avoid bleeding complications. Patients should have the option of shaving if they would like to shave. Waxing may not be an option.

questions.stem: The nurse and UAP are making an occupied bed together. Which action by the nurse is incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse asks and assists the patient to turn toward the UAP and loosens the fitted sheet and rolls it in toward the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The nurse rolls dirty linens to the side then places the linens on the floor while finishing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse tucks the clean bottom sheet under the cleaner underside of the dirty linens.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse wears gloves to remove dirty linens.

questions.rationale: Bed linens should be placed in the linen hamper, not on the floor, after they are removed from the bed. The patient turns to each side while the bed linens are changed, and the nurse wears gloves.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that which areas of the patient‘s body are at increased risk of excoriation? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exposed areas such as the face

choices.label: B
choices.text: Areas exposed to stool

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skin on skin areas

choices.label: D
choices.text: Area under pendulous breasts

choices.label: E
choices.text: Under an abdominal fold

questions.rationale: Excoriation (red, scaly areas with surface loss of skin tissue) occurs in patients whose skin is exposed to bodily fluids such as stool, urine, or gastric juices. Excoriation also occurs in areas where skin rests on skin, such as in the axilla (armpit); under large, pendulous breasts; or in abdominal folds. Exposed areas are more likely to become sun burned or wind burned.

questions.stem: The nurse is demonstrating cultural sensitivity in performing perineal care when carrying out which actions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The male nurse delegates perineal care of a female patient to the female UAP.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The male nurse asks a female patient if she would prefer a female to perform care.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse approaches the care in a sensitive, professional manner.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse assesses culturNal pRr e f eIr en cGe s oBf.thCe paMtient prior to care.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The nurse provides care quickly and in a matter of fact manner.

questions.rationale: The nurse assesses patient backgrounds and provides hygienic care in a manner that is sensitive to the differences in habits and customs. This includes asking the patient about their preferences and not assuming what their preferences will be. A female patient may be comfortable with a male nurse performing perineal care. The nurse should not perform the care without asking first and should not preform the task quickly.

questions.stem: When the nurse is assisting patients with hygiene care, which tasks should be included? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bathing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral care

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perineal care

choices.label: D
choices.text: Foot care

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patient communication

questions.rationale: Hygienic practices include bathing, oral care, perineal care (cleansing of the genital area, urinary meatus, and anus), foot care, and shaving. During hygiene care the nurse communicates with the patient, assesses the skin, and observes for any abnormalities.

questions.stem: The nurse is bathing a patient and notes reddened skin above the coccyx. Which actions by the nurse are appropriate? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apply a barrier cream and massage the area.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the findings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Position the patient to relieve pressure on coccyx.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Report the area to the charge nurse.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Report the new finding to the provider.

questions.rationale: Gently wash any reddened or swollen areas and pat them dry. Use clean, nonsterile gloves as needed to comply with standard precautions. Document the findings from the assessment and report them to the provider, charge nurse, or other appropriate personnel per agency policies. Avoid massaging reddened areas on the skin during the bath. Further tissue breakdown can occur if reddened areas are massaged.

questions.stem: Regarding perineal care, whiNchUnRuSrsiInNg GacTtioBn.s CarOe Mappropriate? ( Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse applies gloves prior to performing perineal care.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The nurse ignores the erection of a male patient during perineal care.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse documents the perineal care.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse only completes perineal care with daily bathing.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The nurse can delegate perineal care.

questions.rationale: The nurse uses standard precautions (gloves) whenever contact with body fluids is expected. A male patient may have an erection during care, which is a normal response with tactile stimulation. The care provider can ignore the erection and continue with the procedure or return later to complete the care, depending on the comfort level and the situation. Documentation is part of hygienic care. Note any redness, drainage, odor, edema, or skin changes. Perineal care is provided during a bath or shower but may be necessary more frequently, especially in incontinent patients. Perineal care can be delegated.

questions.stem: The nurse should avoid soaking the feet of which patient population? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patients with peripheral vascular disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patients with a stroke

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patients with diabetes U S N

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patients with arthritis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patients who are malnourished

questions.rationale: Soaking the feet of patients with peripheral vascular disease, cardiovascular disease s uch as strokes and diabetes are contraindicated because it may cause skin breakdown or inf ection. Patient with arthritis or malnourished have no contraindications to having their feet soaked.

questions.stem: The nurse notes that a trauma patient has multiple tangles in the hair. Which actions taken by the nurse are appropriate? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Work the tangles to the ends of the hair, then trim with scissors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply warm water and conditioner.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply detangler as available.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use a comb or fingers to work through tangles.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cut the tangles out if working on them agitates the patient.

questions.rationale: Apply warm water and a conditioner or a detangler, if available, to release tangles and avoid injury to the scalp. Use a comb and/or fingers to work through the tangles individually before shampooing. The nurse avoids cutting the patient‘s hair unless first asking the patient‘s permission. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: What response would the nurse give the patient when questioned about the effect of rheumatoid arthritis on the musculoskeletal system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Muscle weakness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Muscle wasting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Joint inflammation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Joint spasticity

questions.rationale: Rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis cause inflammation of joints, resulting in pa in and limited joint mobility, not muscle mobility. Genetic disorders such as muscular d ystrophy result in muscle weakness and gradual muscle wasting. Spasticity (increased muscle tone) occurs in developmental disorders, such as cerebral palsy, and results in reduced range of motion (ROM) and abnormal movement patterns. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity

questions.stem: The nurse is implementing generalized falls precautions for patients who are at risk for falls. Which intervention indicates a lack of understanding of these precautions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The bed is placed in the lNo w Rp o s iIt io nG.      B.CM

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is wearing s oc kUs.  SNT  O

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s cell phone is by the bedside.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s call light is within reach.

questions.rationale: If the patient is ambulatory, require the use of nonskid footwear. Socks can be slippery unless they have a grip surface on them. Keep patient belongings (e.g., tissues, water, urinals, personal items) within the patient‘s reach. Keep the call light in reach and remind the patient to use it and keep the bed in the low position.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the family of a patient on falls risk precautions. Which statement by the family indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should keep the wheelchair locked unless using it to move Mom.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should leave the bathroom light on as she does at her home.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should leave her slippers by the wheelchair.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should keep her cell phone close to her bed.‖

questions.rationale: Leave lights on or off at night, depending on the patient‘s cognitive status and personal preference. Keep the wheels of any wheeled device (e.g., bed, wheelchair) in the locked position. Keep patient belongings (e.g., tissues, water, urinals, personal items) within the patient‘s reach. If the patient is ambulatory, require the use of nonskid footwear (socks or shoes).

questions.stem: The nurse is performing passive range-of-motion exercises on a patient when the patient begins to complain of pain. What is the first thing the nurse should do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the health care provider.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperextend the joint.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stop the range of motion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Switch to active range of motion.

questions.rationale: Stop range-of-motion exercises if the patient begins to complain of pain or if resistance to movement is experienced. Never hyperextend or flex a patient‘s joints beyond the position of comfort. Active range of motion is when the patient moves the joint. Notifying the health care provider would happen later.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which goal to be appropriate for the patient who is postoperative day one from a hip fracture with NthUe nRuSrsIinNgGdTiaBgn.oCsisOIMmpaired mobility? a. Patient will interact with others.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will ambulate to the bathroom with assistance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will have no skin breakdown.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will have a physical therapy consult.

questions.rationale: Patients with a diagnosis of Impaired mobility should have a goal aimed at improving their mobility. Although immobility can impact social isolation and skin breakdown, those goals are not appropriate for this diagnosis. Have a physical therapy consult is not a goal but an intervention.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which goal to be appropriate for the patient who is postoperative day one from abdominal surgery and on bed rest with the nursing diagnosis impaired skin integrity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient will ambulate twice a day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will eat 50% of meals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will have no further skin breakdown.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will interact with others.

questions.rationale: The patient already has a wound, so the goal is focused on no further skin breakdown as a result of the bed rest and immobility. Although nutrition is important to wound healing, it is not the focus of this Nursing diagnosis. Ambulating and interacting with others are not goals for this diagnosis.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing education to the patient about isometric exercises. Which statement by the patient indicates a good understanding of these exercises?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―An example of this type of exercise is walking.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―An example of this type of exercise is running.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―An example of this type of exercise is Kegels.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―An example of this type of exercise is weight lifting.‖

questions.rationale: Isometric exercise requires tension and relaxation of muscles without joint movement. An example is tension and relaxation of pelvic floor muscles (i.e., Kegel exercise). Isotonic exercise involves active movement with constant muscle contraction, such as walking, turning in bed, and self- feeding. Aerobic exercise requires oxygen metabolism to produce energy. Patients may engage in rigorous walking or repeated stair climbing to achieve the positive effects of aerobic exercise. Anaerobic exercise builds power and body mass. Without oxygen to produce energy for activity, anaerobic exercise takes place, such as heavy weight lifting.

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing to assiNstUthReSpIatNieGntTtoBw.aClkOtMo the bathroom after medicating the patient with a narcotic for pain management. What possible adverse effect should the nurse be immediately aware?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Constipation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Depression

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dizziness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pain relief

questions.rationale: Potential adverse side effects of narcotics include respiratory depression, hypotension, confusion, sedation, constipation, and dizziness. The nurse should be immediately aware of dizziness during ambulation because of the safety risks. Pain relief is expected. Depression is not an immediate adverse side effect. Constipation will not impact the nurse‘s ability to safely ambulate the patient.

questions.stem: The nurse correctly selects which intervention to avoid causing shear or friction when moving a patient in bed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using an airflow bed

choices.label: B
choices.text: Using a slide board

choices.label: C
choices.text: Using a trochanter roll

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using a gel mattress

questions.rationale: A transfer or slide board is made of plastic-like material that reduces friction. Linens easily slide over the board, facilitating bed linen changes. Patients can be repositioned or transferred with a minimum of force required. A trochanter roll prevents outward rolling of the hip when a patient is lying on his/her back. An air-fluidized bed uses airflow to move silicone particles in the bed, creating a watery, fluid-like movement and resulting in lower pressure to avoid or alleviate decubitus ulcers. A foam or gel combination mattress reduces pressure.

questions.stem: Which explanation by the nurse best describes active assistive range of motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient independently moves all joints.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient to partially moves all joints.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The caregiver must move the patient‘s joints.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient performs isotonic exercises.

questions.rationale: Active assistive range of motion occurs when the caregiver minimally assists the patient, or the patient minimally assists himself/herself in the movement of joints through a full motion. Active range of motion occurs when the patient has full independent movement of all joints; this is also known as isotonic exercise. Passive range of motion occurs when the caregiver moves the patient‘s joints through a full motion. This exercise does not maintain or improve strength but maintains flexibility and prevents contractures and atrophy.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which instruction to be appropriate to delegate to the UAP (Unlicensed assistive personnel)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s skin during a bath.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reposition the patient using the trapeze.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s ability to perform range-of-motion exercises.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the health care provider of any changes.

questions.rationale: Repositioning a patient can be delegated to unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP); the nurse should provide proper instruction regarding specific positioning techniques, individualized patient concerns, and circumstances that require notifying a nurse. UAP may not perform assessments or evaluations but should notify the nurse about any skin or musculoskeletal issues (not the health care provider).

questions.stem: The nurse knows that manual lifting should only be done in which situation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patients who are less than 150 lb

choices.label: B
choices.text: Life-threatening situations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Postsurgical patients

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patients who are less than 200 lb

questions.rationale: Many manual patient handling tasks are unsafe, because the weights lifted and movements required are beyond the ability of most caregivers. The key is to identify the task to be accomplished, and then use the required equipment and personnel so that the task fits the capabilities of the staff (U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, 2016). The patient‘s level of cooperation is taken into consideration when using the safe patient-handling and mobility (SPHM) algorithms to decide the best method of moving the patient. The patient‘s weight, medical conditions, and ability to assist are also considered (U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, 2016). Postsurgical patients as well as patients less than 150 or 200 lb may not fit the criteria.

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing to reposition the patient in bed. What is the first step in this process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Position the patient‘s arms across his/her chest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lower the side rails.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Grasp the draw sheet.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Raise the bed to a working height.

questions.rationale: Raising the bed to a working height is the first step before beginning the procedure. Proper positioning of equipment preNveUnRtsSprIoNviGdeTr Bdi.scCoOmMfort and reduces the chance of possible injury. Then lower the side rails as appropriate and safe and ensure that the bed wheels are locked. Then you can have the patient position his/her arms and/or grasp the draw sheet.

questions.stem: The nurse has delegated to the UAP to assist a patient with ambulating in the hallway with a cane. Which statement by the UAP indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should report any complaints of soreness to the nurse.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should watch for indications that the patient has difficulties using the cane.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should let the nurse or PT know if the cane doesn‘t seem to fit correctly.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should teach the patient how to walk with the cane.‖

questions.rationale: Educating patients on how to walk with assistive devices may not be delegated to unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP). UAP should report any of the following: noticeable incorrect usage or fit of assistive devices, complaints of soreness or weakness, difficulties involving balance or strength, or difficulties in performing the procedure or other concerns verbalized by the patient.

questions.stem: The nurse correctly teaches the patient to rise from a chair using crutches when which intervention is used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient starts from the back of the chair.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The weak leg is closest to the chair.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The hand on the strong side holds the hand bar of the crutch.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The strong leg is closest to the chair.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s strongest leg needs to be closest to the chair. The patient‘s hand on the weak side holds the hand bar of the crutches, and the hand on the patient‘s strong side holds onto the armrest of the chair. The patient moves to the front edge of the chair.

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching a patient about ways to decrease risk of bone fractures. Which statements by the patient indicate a good understanding of decreasing this risk? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should do weight-bearing exercises.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should get adequate intake of calcium and vitamin D.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should exercise regularly.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I need to do yoga exercises.‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―I wish I could reduce myNrUisRkSbuItNI GcaTn‘Bt .d oCaOn Myt h i n g.‖

questions.rationale: Inadequate dietary intake of calcium and vitamin D or impaired calcium metabolism may result in osteoporosis, which increases bone fragility and may lead to fractures. Decreased physical exercise and lack of weight-bearing exercise also contribute to bone fragility, deterioration, and loss of strength. Any type of exercise will help; it does not need to be yoga, but it does need to include weight-bearing exercise.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that a patient with a compromised cardiopulmonary system has a diminished capacity for exercise because of which conditions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased tissue perfusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of sensation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemiparesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diminished respiratory capacity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Muscle weakness

questions.rationale: Compromised cardiac function, decreased tissue perfusion, and diminished respiratory capacity directly affect a person‘s ability to perform activities of daily living (ADLs) and exercise. Hemiparesis and loss of sensation are associated with nervous system d isorders. Muscle weakness can be from a number of causes.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the patient about the effects of immobility on the body. Which statements by the patient indicate a need for further education? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I can become very weak.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I will gain weight.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I will lose muscle tone.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I can get bed sores.‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―I won‘t have any lung problems.‖

questions.rationale: Immobility may cause weakness, instability, anorexia, elimination alterations, decreased muscle tone, circulatory stasis, DVTs, pulmonary embolism, and skin breakdown. Knowing the effects of immobility on various body systems allows the nurse to quickly assess a patient‘s risk and recognize signs of impending complications.

questions.stem: The nurse knows which items are included in the documentation for a patient on fall precautions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: History of any falls

choices.label: B
choices.text: Falls risk assessment scores

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient and family education

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use of assist devices

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anyfall or reported fall

questions.rationale: The nurse should document the general assessment, include the patient‘s medical history, subjective and objective data, medication review, musculoskeletal status, and history of falls. Falls assessment and reassessment, patient family education and use of assist devices are also documented. Thoroughly document a fall or reported fall.

questions.stem: The nurse knows which findings indicate orthostatic hypotension? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: A decrease in systolic blood pressure by 30 mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: A decrease in diastolic blood pressure by 10 mm Hg

choices.label: C
choices.text: An increase in heart rate by 30 beats/min

choices.label: D
choices.text: An increase in systolic blood pressure by 20 mm Hg

choices.label: E
choices.text: A decrease in heart rate by 20 beats/min

questions.rationale: A drop in systolic blood pressure of 20 mm Hg, an increase in heart rate of 20 beats/min, or a drop of diastolic blood pressure of 10 mm Hg when a patient stands is classified a s orthostatic hypotension.

questions.stem: The nurse appropriately delegates care of the unit‘s patients to the properly trained UAP when that UAP is assigned which tasks? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: UAP assigned to reposition the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: UAP assigned to complete the MORSE falls risk scale.

choices.label: C
choices.text: UAP assigned to provide range-of-motion exercises.

choices.label: D
choices.text: UAP assigned to ambulate the patient in the hallway.

choices.label: E
choices.text: UAP assigned to time the patient on a TUG test.

questions.rationale: UAPs provide hands-on care for immobilized patients under the direct supervision of registered nurses. Turning and positioning of patients, range-of-motion exercises, transfers, and assistance with ambulation may be delegated to properly trained UAP. UAPs may not assess patients because that is a nursing responsibility. The MORSE falls risk scale is a risk assessment as is the Timed Up and Go (TUG) test.

questions.stem: The nurse is correctly demonstrating the use of a transfer belt when engaging in which actions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The belt is placed around the patient‘s hips.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The belt is secure, leaving only enough room for the nurse to grasp the belt.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse stands on the weaker side.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse holds the belt on the side of the patient.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The nurse stands behind tNheUpRaStinIeNt wGhTilBe .a mCbOuMlat ing.

questions.rationale: Transfer belts are used for patients with an unsteady gait or generalized weakness. Canvas transfer or gait belts are applied snugly around the patient‘s waist, leaving only enough room for the nurse to grasp the belt firmly during ambulation. Some belts may have handles. If the patient has a weaker side, the nurse should stand on that side and hold the gait belt firmly at the back of the patient‘s waist while ambulating.

questions.stem: The nurse is correctly assisting the patient in using a cane when the patient demonstrates which activities? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The top of the cane is level with the patient‘s bent elbow.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient holds the cane on his/her weaker side.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient moves the cane forward first.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s arm is comfortably bent when walking.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient moves the strong leg forward first.

questions.rationale: The top of the cane should be level with the hip joint, and the patient‘s arm should be comfortably bent when the patient is walking. The patient should hold the cane on his/her stronger side and move the cane forward first, followed by the weaker leg and then the stronger leg. This ensures that another point of support is always on the ground when the weaker leg is bearing weight and gives the patient a wide base of support. A patient using a cane should be encouraged to stand up straight and look forward. Leaning to one side or looking down can jeopardize safety and cause poor posture.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge education for the patient who is going home wi th a walker. Which statements by the patient indicate a good level of understanding of safety in the home? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I need to remove the throw rugs.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should make sure I only take a bath.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I cannot use the stairs.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I need to place a nonskid mat in front of the kitchen sink.‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―I wish I had two ways of leaving the house.‖

questions.rationale: To ensure patients do not have hazards that can cause falls at home, the nurse should evaluate where the living quarters are. If the patient has stairs, they need to be able to safely learn how to use the stairs. They need to remove throw rugs that are a trip hazard and place nonskid mats in front of sinks, tubs, and showers. They can shower with a bench or chair in the shower for sitting. Patients need a clear the exit so they can get out of the house quickly in case of an emergency, but do not specifNicUalRlySnIeeNd GtwToBd.iffCeOrenMt exits because of the walker.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies that knee-high SCD (Sequential Compression Device) sleeves are correctly placed on the patient when which conditions are met? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both sleeves are connected to the SCD device.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two fingers fit inside when the SCDs are inflated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There are no kinks in the tubing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The ankle pressure is 55 to 65 mm Hg.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The cooling control is on.

questions.rationale: Proper positioning of the SCD sleeve allows proper fit and application, which decreases the risk of constricting the blood flow or diminishing optimal outcomes. Wrap the sleeve around the leg and fasten it with Velcro straps. Verify that two fingers fit between the leg and the sleeve when the sleeve is not inflated. Connect the sleeves to the device, ensure that there are no kinks in the tubing, and turn on the cooling and set it to 35 to 55 mm Hg. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse knows which description would be classified as a closed wound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A large bruise on the side of the face

choices.label: B
choices.text: A surgical incision that is sutured closed

choices.label: C
choices.text: A puncture wound that is healing

choices.label: D
choices.text: An abrasion on the leg

questions.rationale: In a closed wound, as seen with bruising, the skin is still intact. An open wound is characterized by an actual break in the skin‘s surface. For example, an abrasion, a puncture wound, and a surgical incision are types of open wounds.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the patient about the signs and symptoms of a wound infection. Which statement indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―The wound will be red.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―The wound will have pus.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―The wound will be war mN.‖ RIGB.CM

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―The wound will need to b eUt r eSa t e dN. ‖ T O

questions.rationale: An infected wound shows clinical signs of infection, including redness, warmth, and increased drainage that may or may not be purulent (contain pus), and has a bacterial count in the tissue of at least 105/g of tissue sampled when cultured. The wound will need to be treated for the infection.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which type of wounds heal by tertiary intention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An acute wound in which the patient has sutures placed when it happened.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A pressure ulcer that was treated with dressing changes and is healed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An acute wound in which surgical glue was used to close the wound.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A wound that was left open initially and closed later with sutures.

questions.rationale: When a delay occurs between injury and closure, the wound healing is said to happen by tertiary intention. Wounds such as surgical incisions or traumatic wounds in which the edges of the wound can be approximated (brought together) to heal are examples of acute wounds. This type of wound is said to heal by primary intention. When a wound heals by secondary intention, new tissue must fill in from the bottom and sides of the wound until the wound bed is filled with new tissue such as a pressure ulcer.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is postoperative day one from an abdominal surgery. When the patient complains of a ―popping sensation‖ and a wetness in the dressing, the nurse immediately suspects which complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A wound infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: The stitches came loose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wound dehiscence

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wound crepitus

questions.rationale: Wound dehiscence, which usually occurs in connection with surgical incisions, is the partial or complete separation of the tissue layers during the healing process. This is an emergency situation. Stitches can come loose, but there is no popping sensation. Wound infections are characterized by redness, warmth, and drainage, and crepitus is air trapped under the skin.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient who has had abdominal surgery and whose wound has completely eviscerated when the nurse walks into the room. In addition to notifying the surgeon, what should the nurse do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cover the wound with a sterile gauze pad.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cover the wound with a transparent dressing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Put pressure on the wound with a sterile gauze pad.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cover the wound with gauze soaked with normal saline.

questions.rationale: If dehiscence or evisceration occurs, cover the wound with gauze moistened with a sterile normal saline, and notify the surgeon immediately. Putting pressure on the wound could cause further complications. Transparent films are used for autolytic debridement. A gauze pad will allow the wound to become dry and cause further complications.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies what goal to be the most appropriate goal for a patient with a stage 3 pressure ulcer who has a Nursing diagnosis of Impaired skin integrity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wound will be completely healed in 72 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wound will show signs of healing within 2 weeks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will develop no new pressure ulcers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will ambulate twice a day.

questions.rationale: A stage 3 pressure ulcer is a more extensive wound and will take time to heal, so the most appropriate goal will be to show signs of healing in 2 weeks. It will not heal in 72 hours. The goal of no new pressure ulcers is good, but not the most appropriate, and ambulating twice a day is more of an intervention.

questions.stem: A new nurse is delegating care of a chronic, nonsterile wound to a UAP. What action by the new nurse causes the preceptor to intervene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse asks the UAP to assess the wound.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The nurse asks the UAP to report increased wound drainage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse asks the UAP to observe changes in dietary intake.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse asks the UAP to change the dressing.

questions.rationale: Assessment and evaluation of a patient‘s skin and wounds, and the effectiveness of the treatment plan, are a nurse‘s responsibility and cannot be delegated to unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP). UAP should report to the nurse any changes in skin condition or integrity; elevation in temperature; complaints of pain; increased wound drainage or incontinence; and observed changes in dietary intake. Some dressing changes can be performed by UAP in some situations.

questions.stem: The nurse is repositioning the patient in the side-lying position. To avoid putting the patient at risk for pressure ulcers, the nurse should place the head of the bed in which position?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Flat

choices.label: B
choices.text: 90 degrees

choices.label: C
choices.text: 30 degrees

choices.label: D
choices.text: 45 degrees

questions.rationale: When side-lying, patients should be positioned at 30 degrees, as opposed to 90 degrees, to avoid positioning the patient directly on bony prominences such as the head of the trochanter.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which intervention is not a form of mechanical debridement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wet to dry dressings

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whirlpool baths

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wet to damp dressing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enzymatic dressing

questions.rationale: Enzymatic debridement is achieved through the application of topical agents containing enzymes that work by breaking down the fibrin, collagen, or elastin present in devitalized tissue, thus allowing for its removal. Mechanical debridement is a nonselective form of debridement because it not only removes the necrotic tissue, but also can remove or disturb exposed viable tissue that may be in the wound. The main forms of mechanical debridement are wet/damp-to-dry dressings and whirlpools.

questions.stem: The nurse is explaining the purpose of occlusive dressings to the student nurse. Which statement by the student nurse indicates a lack of understanding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Occlusive dressings are used for autolytic debridement.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Hydrocolloids are a type of occlusive dressing.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Occlusive dressings can be used on infected wounds.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Occlusive dressings support the most comfortable form of debridement.‖

questions.rationale: Occlusive dressings such as hydrocolloids and transparent films are used for autolytic debridement and are contraindicated in infected wounds. It is the most comfortable form of debridement for the patient. Nty  R IGB.CM  U S N T O

questions.stem: The nurse knows that a hydrocolloid dressing is appropriate for use on which type of wound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A wound with a large amount of drainage

choices.label: B
choices.text: A wound that is tunneling

choices.label: C
choices.text: A postsurgical incision with staples

choices.label: D
choices.text: A wound with a moderate amount of drainage

questions.rationale: Hydrocolloids are occlusive, adhesive dressings composed of gelling agents and carboxymethylcellulose. They absorb a small to moderate amount of drainage over a 3- to 7-day period, forming a gel as drainage is absorbed. A wound with a large amount of drainage would require a foam or alginate dressing, a postsurgical incision with staples could use Steri-Strips or gauze, and a wound that is tunneling may require packing.

questions.stem: When the nurse is caring for a patient with a Penrose drain, what care needs to be carried out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The drain must be compressed after emptying to work properly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The drain must be connected to suction if ordered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The drain is not sutured in place so care is taken to not dislodge it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The suction pulls drainage away from the wound as it re-expands.

questions.rationale: The Penrose drain, an open drain that is a flexible piece of tubing, is usually not sutured into place and is not connected to suction. Closed drains are compressed or connected to suction if ordered and pull drainage away as they expand.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the patient about the use of heat/cold therapy at home. Which statement by the patient indicates the need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should fill my ice bag 2/3 full of ice.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should use distilled water in my Aqua-K pad.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I can warm up my hot pack in the microwave.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should check the order for how long to leave the compress on.‖

questions.rationale: Warm compresses and water for soaks should not be heated in the microwave unless the product and microwave are specifically designed for this type of heating. Ice bags are filled two- thirds full, distilled water is used in Aqua-K pads, and application time for heat is as stated in the PCP order (for cold, it is a maximum of 20 to 30 minutes).

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which syringe to use when irrigating a patient‘s deep wound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 5-mL syringe

choices.label: B
choices.text: 10-mL syringe

choices.label: C
choices.text: 3-mL syringe

choices.label: D
choices.text: 30-mL syringe

questions.rationale: A deep wound is irrigated with a 30- to 50-mL piston syringe with an 18-gauge angiocath. Unlike the 1 pound per square inch (psi) of pressure or less that is delivered by a standard bulb syringe, the use of a 30- to 50-mL syringe and 18-gauge catheter has been shown to achieve an irrigation force that falls within the recommended 4 to 15 psi.

questions.stem: The nurse understands which rationale to be appropriate for drying a wound after irrigation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure the new dressing adheres to the wound.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure the new dressing remains occlusive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prevent skin breakdown from moisture.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prevent infection from irrigate solution.

questions.rationale: Proper drying prevents further skin breakdown from moisture. Patting (rather than rubbing) prevents healthy tissue from being removed and reduces trauma to the wound. The type of dressing will determine how it lays in the wound and whether it is occlusive. The drying does not prevent infection.

questions.stem: The nurse is performing a wet/damp to dry dressing change when the patient begins to complain of severe pain. What does the nurse do first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the provider.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Notify the wound care nurse.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stop the procedure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give the patient pain medication.

questions.rationale: If the patient is complaining of severe pain, the nurse should first stop the procedure and then determine if the pain is new or preexisting. Then the nurse can determine what to do next based on the patient‘s response.

questions.stem: The nurse knows what goal to be appropriate for a patient with a stage 3 pressure ulcer with the nursing diagnosis impaired physical mobility?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient will remain free o Nf UwoRuSndIiNnGfeTctiBo.nsCdOurMing the hospitalization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will report pain management strategies and reduce pain to a tolerable level.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will be able to assist with position changes using over bed trapeze within 1 week.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will consume adequate nutrition to meet nutritional requirements within 1 week.

questions.rationale: Patient will be able to assist with position changes using over bed trapeze within 1 week is an appropriate goal for impaired mobility. The patient remaining free of wound infections during the hospitalization is an appropriate goal for impaired tissue integrity. The patient reporting pain management strategies to reduce pain to a tolerable level is an appropriate goal for acute pain. The patient consuming adequate nutrition to meet nutritional requirements within 1 week is an appropriate goal for Impaired nutritional status.

questions.stem: When discussing stage 3 pressure ulcers with the student nurse, which description would the staff nurse include?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A pressure ulcer that involves exposure of bone and connective tissue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A pressure ulcer that does not extend through the fascia.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A pressure ulcer that does not include tunneling.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A partial-thick wound that involves the epidermis.

questions.rationale: Stage 3 pressure ulcers are full-thickness wounds that extend into the subcutaneous tissue but do not extend through the fascia to muscle, bone, or connective tissue. There may be undermining or tunneling present in the wound. Stage 4 pressure ulcers involve exposure of muscle, bone, or connective tissue such as tendons or cartilage. Stage 2 pressure ulcers are partial-thickness wounds that involve the epidermis and/or dermis. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which skin layer that delivers the blood supply to the dermis, provides insulation, and has a cushioning effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stratum germinativum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Epidermis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subcutaneous layer

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stratum corneum

questions.rationale: The subcutaneous layer delivers the blood supply to the dermis, provides insulation, and has a cushioning effect. The stratum germinativum constantly produces new cells that are pushed upward through the other layers of the epidermis toward the stratum corneum, where they flatten, die, and are eventually sloughed off and replaced by new cells. The epidermis is the outermost layer of the skin and the thinnest of the layers. The stratum corneum is made up of flattened dead cells.

questions.stem: The nurse knows which factors contribute to the development of wounds and lead to delays in wound healing? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: A patient who has diabetes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A patient with COPD.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A patient with on bed rest who is repositioned.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A patient who is obese and sweats excessively.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A patient on long-term steroid therapy.

questions.rationale: Factors that contribute to the development of wounds and lead to delays in wound healing include comorbidities such as vascular disease, which impacts the skin‘s ability to obtain required oxygen and nutrients, or diabetes, which affects not only the microvasculature, but also the skin‘s normally acidic pH; malnutrition involving inadequate proteins, cholesterol and fatty acids, and vitamins and minerals; medications such as steroids, nonsteroidal, anti-inflammatories, and anticoagulants; excessive moisture from sweating; and ex ternal forces such as pressure, shear, and friction that occur when turning and repositioning t he patient in bed.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes that the cause of pressure ulcers includes which factors? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intensity of the pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Duration of the pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tissue‘s ability to tolerate the pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Person‘s age

choices.label: E
choices.text: Person‘s nutritional status

questions.rationale: The primary cause of pressure ulcers is, as the name suggests, pressure. However, it is more than just pressure; it is the intensity of the pressure, the length of time that the tissue is subjected to the pressure, and intrinsic and extrinsic factors that affect the tissue‘s ability to withstand or tolerate that pressure. Intrinsic and extrinsic factors can include nutrition st atus and age.

questions.stem: When the nurse is performing a focused wound assessment on a patient, what information should be included in the documentation? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Location and size

choices.label: B
choices.text: Characteristics of the woNunUdRbeSdINGTB.COM

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient‘s response to wound treatment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient‘s pain level

choices.label: E
choices.text: Presence of drainage

questions.rationale: A focused wound assessment includes an evaluation of the wound‘s location, size, and color; presence of drainage; condition of the wound edges; characteristics of the wound bed; and patient‘s response to the wound or wound treatment. The patient‘s pain level would be documented with his/her pain assessment.

questions.stem: The nurse is using the Braden scale to assess the patient‘s risk for a pressure ulcer. Which risk categories are associated with the Braden scale? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Friction and shear

choices.label: C
choices.text: Moisture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sensory perception

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cognition

questions.rationale: The Braden scale ranks the patient on the risk categories of sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition, and friction and shear. The scale does not include cognition. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative orthopedic patient who has two Hemovac drains in place. Which interventions will the nurse perform? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Measure the amount of drainage in the device prior to emptying.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Label each drain and record them separately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recompress the device after emptying.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secure the device to the patient‘s gown above the level of the wound.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Check for kinks in the tubing.

questions.rationale: Use a marked, graduated measuring device to collect the drainage when emptying the reservoir to facilitate accurate measurement of the drainage. After emptying, recompress the device to maintain suction. Secure the container(s) to the patient‘s hospital gown below the level of the wound, avoiding tension on the tubing and making sure there are no kinks. If there are multiple drains, label them and document observations by the drain label.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes that cold therapy is contraindicated in which conditions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Edema

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shivering

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bleeding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Circulatory problems

choices.label: E
choices.text: Advanced age

questions.rationale: Cold should not be used if any of the following is present: edema (cold application slows reabsorption of the fluid), circulatory pathophysiology (cold application causes vasoconstriction, further reducing circulation to the area), and shivering (this is a comfort concern). Bleeding is contraindicated in heat therapy. Advanced age would require frequent observation due to thin skin. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse is providing education to a patient about the difference between simple and complex carbohydrates. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Simple carbohydrates give me quick energy.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Complex carbohydrates come from fruit.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Complex carbohydrates take longer to break down.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Simple carbohydrates come from milk products.‖

questions.rationale: Simple carbohydrates are broken down and absorbed quickly, providing a quick source of energy. Examples are sugars such as those derived from fruit (fructose), table sugar (sucrose), milk products (lactose), and blood sugar (glucose). Complex carbohydrates are composed of starches, glycogen, and fiber. They take longer to break down prior to absorption and utilization by the body‘s cells.

questions.stem: The nurse teaches the family member to provide the patient with how much dietary fiber per day?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 25 to 35 g

choices.label: B
choices.text: 20 to 35 g

choices.label: C
choices.text: 25 to 40 g

choices.label: D
choices.text: 20 to 40 g

questions.rationale: Older children, adolescents, and adults should consume 20 to 35 g of fiber a day. Food sources include whole grains, wheat bran, cereals, fresh fruits, vegetables, and legumes.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing education to an older adult around a healthy diet to support the challenges related to aging. Which statement indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should choose foods that are nutrient dense.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―High-fiber foods minimize the risk of constipation.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should eat more calories to avoid malnutrition.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I can add spices to enhance the taste of food.‖

questions.rationale: Calorie needs change with aging because of more body fat and less lean muscle. Less activity further decreases calorie requirements. Eating whole-grain foods and a variety of fruits and vegetables and drinking water may minimize the risk of constipation. The challenge for older adults is to choose foods that are nutrient dense; these foods are high in nutrients in relation to their calories. Older adults may experience a decreased sense of smell or taste, so the addition of spices and herbs may enhance the taste of foods.

questions.stem: When caring for an adolescent patient with anorexia nervosa, the nurse knows what would be the best treatment option for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hospitalization with skill nursing care

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compulsory tube feedings

choices.label: C
choices.text: Individually determined by a collaborative team

choices.label: D
choices.text: Outpatient treatment

questions.rationale: Ultimately, the decision on how best to ethically treat an adolescent suffering from an eating disorder needs to be one of collaboration among the child‘s physician, nurse, counselor, spiritual adviser, parents, and other concerned adults. Highly skilled nursing care with hospitalization is preferred prior to a drop in BMI levels below 13 kg/m2. Compulsory tube feedings are not always the best option. Although most adolescents with eating disorders can be treated on an outpatient basis, those who exhibit severe depression, extreme physical complications resulting from electrolyte imbalances, or suicidal tendencies may require extensive inpatient treatment N. URSINGTB.COM

questions.stem: A new UAP is measuring a patient‘s height. Which step of the procedure indicates a need for the registered nurse to provide further education on this skill?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The UAP instructs the patient to remove shoes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The UAP measures from the top of the patient‘s head to the bottom of the patient‘s foot arch.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The UAP positions the head against the headboard or measuring device.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The UAP makes sure the patient is standing erect.

questions.rationale: Height is measured from the top of the head to the bottom of the heel. The patient is instructed to remove shoes, stand erect, and position the top of the patient‘s head against the headboard or measuring device for accuracy.

questions.stem: The nurse is performing an oral examination on a patient and notices a beefy-red tongue. The nurse identifies this as a characteristic finding for what condition? U S N

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anorexia nervosa

choices.label: B
choices.text: Malnutrition

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bulimia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pernicious anemia

questions.rationale: In conditions such as pernicious anemia, a characteristic finding is a sore, smooth-surfaced, beefy-red tongue, which may interfere with the person‘s ability to chew certain foods. Anorexia nervosa and bulimia are eating disorders. In malnutrition the oral mucosa may be a darker red than normal with oral lesions and/or the tongue may reveal white irregular areas. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse has delegated the feeding of a patient who has recently had a stroke to the UAP. Which procedure that the UAP performs would demonstrate a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Uses thickened liquids.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Puts the bed at 25 degrees.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encourages slow eating.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Has the patient alternate between food and sips of fluid.

questions.rationale: During feeding, the head of the bed needs to be elevated at 30 to 45 degrees or higher. Liquids are thickened, and patients are encouraged to use slow-eating habits and to alternate between bites of food and sips of fluids to facilitate swallowing. N. 6  RIGTOBP:.ECvalMuation Control

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which outcome statement to be appropriate for the nursing diagnosis Impaired swallowing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient will consume 50% of each meal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will gain 2 lb a week.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will not show any signs of aspiration during meals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will demonstrate using an assistive device to feed self.

questions.rationale: An appropriate goal statement for impaired swallowing is that the patient will not exhibit any signs or symptoms of aspiration during this hospitalization (e.g., lungs clear, respiratory rate within normal range for patient). Consuming 50% of meals and gaining weight are appropriate goals for Impaired nutritional intake. Using assistive devices is an appropriate goal for Impaired self-feeding.

questions.stem: The nurse is explaining to the UAP that the patient is on a full-liquid diet. Which statement by the UAP indicates a need for reorientation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I can give the patient orange juice.‖ U S N

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I can give the patient yogurt.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I can give the patient oatmeal.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I can give the patient milk.‖

questions.rationale: Full-liquid diets consist of foods that are or may become liquid at room or body temperature. Full- liquid diets include juices with and without pulp, milk and milk products, yogurt, strained cream soups, and liquid dietary supplements. Oatmeal is not considered part of a full-liquid diet.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating a patient about a renal diet. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I need to eat a low-sodium diet.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I can have limited amounts of meat.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I can drink unlimited cola if it is diet.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should avoid or limit bananas.‖

questions.rationale: Renal diets restrict potassium, sodium, protein, and phosphorous intake. Fresh fruits (except bananas) and vegetables are excellent dietary choices for individuals on a renal diet. Meats, processed foods, peanut butter, cheese, nuts, caramels, ice cream, and colas are typically allowed in limited quantities or contraindicated. N. 6  RIGTOBP:.ECvalMuation

questions.stem: The nurse knows that initial verification of a nasogastric placement is important. Which method is considered the only reliable method to determine enteral tube placement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Auscultation of air bolus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measurement of pH of the aspirate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Radiographic image

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspirate contents to visually inspect appearance

questions.rationale: Studies support the use of radiographic confirmation as the only reliable method to date of confirming enteral tube placement. Using only pH and the appearance of aspirate from the newly inserted tube is not a safe method of verifying proper gastric tube placement, especially in patients receiving antacid medications. Auscultation of an air bolus to assess tube placement is no longer recognized as a reliable source in determining gastric tube placement.

questions.stem: The nurse is attempting to open an occluded PEG tube. Which intervention by the nurse requires re-education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Flushes the tube with a small amount of air. U S N

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flushes the tube using a 50- to 60-mL syringe and warm water.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reinserts the stylet to break up the clot.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Flushes the tube with a special enzyme solution.

questions.rationale: Once the stylet is removed, it is never reinserted because it can puncture the intestine. If the tube becomes occluded, flush it with a small amount of air. If this is unsuccessful in removing the occlusion, flush the tube using a 30 to 60 mL syringe and warm water. If flushing the tube with water is ineffective, research now suggests using special enzyme solutions or declogging devices rather than carbonated beverages or juices.

questions.stem: When the nurse is caring for a patient who is receiving total parenteral nutrition (TPN), the nurse will change the tubing at which interval?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Every 72 hours

choices.label: B
choices.text: Every 48 hours

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every 24 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every 12 hours

questions.rationale: Tubing should be changed every 24 hours, with aseptic technique used to minimize the risk of contamination, and the dressing over the site should be changed every 48 hours, with assessment for signs and symptoms of infection (redness, swelling, or drainage). Control

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing to insert a nasogastric (NG) tube in a patient. Which step in the process indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse lubricates 4 inches of the tube prior to insertion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The nurse marks the length of the tube with a marker for insertion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse measures the length of tube needed using the nose-earlobe-xiphoid process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse applies clean gloves for the procedure.

questions.rationale: Document the length of the tube to be used if the tube has a preprinted measurement scale. For any tube (with or without a preprinted scale), mark the measurement on the tube using a small piece of tape to ensure proper placement of the tube; fold the ends of the tape for easy removal. Do not use a permanent marker to mark the tube at this point of the procedure. When placement of the tube is confirmed as correct, then remove the measurement marking tape, mark the exit location on the tube with permanent marker, and proceed with the ordered treatment. Lubricate 4 inches of the tube tip with a water-soluble lubricant. For an NG tube, measure the length of tube needed for the patient by placing the tip of the tube at the tip of the patient‘s nose and extending it to the patient‘s earlobe and then to the patient‘s xiphoid process. Clean gloves are used.

questions.stem: The nurse has received an order from the health care provider to discontinue the nasogastric tube. Which action by the nurse indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse clears the tube with air prior to discontinuing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The nurse stops the tube feeding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse instructs the patient to cough while pulling out the tube.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse clamps the tube while pulling it out.

questions.rationale: To remove the tube, instruct the patient to take a deep breath and hold it; pinch the tube, and pull it out smoothly and quickly. The nurse should stop any feedings, and suction and flush the tube with water and/or air as appropriate. The nurse should not ask the patient to cough while pulling out the tube. Coughing during tube insertion may indicate the tube is entering the patient‘s lungs.

questions.stem: Based on research on aging, the nurse knows that improper nutrition may result in the onset of which specific diseases? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Type 2 diabetes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atherosclerosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Osteoporosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rheumatoid arthritis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic asthma

questions.rationale: Improper nutrition may result in the onset of specific diseases of the endocrine, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, and musculoskeletal systems, such as diabetes type 2, atherosclerosis, diverticulosis, osteoporosis, and some cancers. Rheumatoid arthritis is an inflammatory autoimmune disorder. Asthma is a respiratory disorder not related to poor nutrition.

questions.stem: The nurse is helping a patient understand the difference between macronutrients and vitamins and minerals. The nurse identifies which items that should be included in the list of macronutrients? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Water

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potassium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Starches

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fiber

choices.label: E
choices.text: Riboflavin

questions.rationale: The major nutrients, often referred to as macronutrients (nutrients that are needed in large amounts), include carbohydrates (sugar, starches, and dietary fiber). Water is also a macronutrient. Potassium is a mineral, and riboflavin is vitamin B2; these are micronutrients.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing dietary education to the patient to assist with inclusion of more complex carbohydrates in the diet. The nurse knows which foods would be beneficial to include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Green peas

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bananas

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beans

choices.label: D
choices.text: Potatoes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Apples

questions.rationale: Complex carbohydrates provide the body with vitamins and minerals. Food sources include bread; rice; pasta; legumes such as dried beans, peas, and lentils; and starchy vegetables such as corn, pumpkin, green peas, and potatoes. Bananas and apples are fruits, which are simple carbohydrates.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating a patient about including more omega-3 fatty acids in the diet and knows which food sources should be included? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Salmon

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flaxseed

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mackerel

choices.label: D
choices.text: Steak

choices.label: E
choices.text: Crayfish

questions.rationale: Dietary sources of omega-3 include fatty fish, such as salmon, tuna, mackerel, and lake trout, as well as nuts, seeds, and oils; flaxseed oil contains the highest amount of total omega-3 fatty acids. Steak and crayfish do not contain omega-3 fatty acids.

questions.stem: The nurse is planning dietary education for the patient. What food labeling considerations should the nurse be aware of when planning that education? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask patient if food labels are read routinely.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess patient‘s level of understanding of food labels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encourage patient to read the food labels.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Explain to patient all food labels are different.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Assess patient‘s understanding of recommended daily allowance

questions.rationale: Evidence indicates a consistent link between eating healthier foods and reading nutrition labels. Patients should be asked if they read food labels when shopping for groceries or food products. Evaluate their understanding of the main elements of a nutrient label (i.e., calories, fats, carbohydrates, sugar, and serving size). Assess patient understanding of the percentages of recommended daily allowances of fats, proteins, and carbohydrates listed on food labels. Uniform nutrition labeling for packaged food was introduced in the United States in 1994, as part of the Nutrition Labeling and Education Act (NLEA), to increase consumer awareness about the nutritional content of food and improve dietary practices; therefore, all labels are the same.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that a deficiency in vitamin C can result in which conditions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stiff joints

choices.label: B
choices.text: Osteopenia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Petechiae

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loose teeth

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bleeding gums

questions.rationale: Deficiencies of vitamin C interfere with normal tissue synthesis and may result in gingivitis, which produces swollen and NblUeeRdiSnIg NguGmTsBw.itCh loMo se ned teeth, and painful, stiff joints. Other problems associated with malabsorption include anemia (a deficiency of red blood cells), excessive bleeding, petechiae (bleeding under the skin), poor wound healing, and neural tube defects. Osteopenia results from poor absorption of calcium. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching a patient about the impact of obesity and a high body mass index (BMI). The nurse identifies that as the BMI increases, so does the risk for which conditions? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase in blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase in HDL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase in total cholesterol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Development of atherosclerosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decrease in triglycerides

questions.rationale: As BMI levels rise, blood pressure and cholesterol levels also rise and the average high- density lipoprotein (HDL), or good, cholesterol levels decrease. Hyperlipidemia (elevation of plasma cholesterol, triglycerides, or both) or low HDL levels contribute  to the development of atherosclerosis (the buildup of fat deposits on arterial vessel walls). Obesity contributes to higher triglycerides.

questions.stem: The nurse is completing a nutrition assessment on a patient. What are some important considerations? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse should include the patient‘s cultural influences in the assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The food diary accuracy is better for a 24-hour recall than a 3 to 5 day food journal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse should be nonjudgmental in the nutritional review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A consultation with a registered dietitian may be indicated.

choices.label: E
choices.text: A gathering of anthropometric measurements may be necessary.

questions.rationale: When collecting data, the nurse should take into consideration the patient‘s culture and ethnicity. Recognizing these influences on the patient‘s nutritional intake allows the nurse to make informed decisions. The data analysis may reveal the need to refer the patient to a registered dietitian for further evaluation of nutritional status. The 24-hour recall is dependent on the ability of the patient to remember consumption of foods and their quantities from the previous day. It is vital to remember that the patient‘s recall may not be factual and the intake may not be that of a typical day. The other means of assessing a patient‘s usual dietary pattern is to have the patient keep a written journal of food intake for a certain amount of time. The food diary should encompass entries for 3 to 5 days and includes dietary intake for a typical weekend. Anthropometric measurements may be needed for a full assessment.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the patient about the risk of heart disease from metabolic syndrome and describes a cluster of which symptoms? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevated blood glucose

choices.label: B
choices.text: High waist circumference

choices.label: C
choices.text: History of smoking

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevation serum cholesterol

questions.rationale: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of medical conditions characterized by insulin resistance and the presence of obesity, abdominal fat, elevated blood glucose, triglycerides, serum cholesterol, and hypertension. Smoking is not part of the syndrome.

questions.stem: The nurse is completing documentation after feeding a patient with aspiration precautions. Which items should the nurse document? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Episodes of coughing or gagging

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hesitation or fear of eating

choices.label: C
choices.text: Amount eaten

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspiration protocol used

choices.label: E
choices.text: Respiratory status

questions.rationale: It is important to document thoroughly the patient‘s experience during the feeding so the other nursing staff will be aware of patient‘s needs including any episodes of coughing, gagging, or choking; respiratory status; hesitancy or fear of eating; and occurrences of nausea, vomiting, regurgitation, and/or reflux symptoms. The nurse would document the amount the patient ate, but this is not part of the required documentation for aspiration precautions.

questions.stem: When the nurse is caring for a patient receiving enteral feedings, which tasks can that nurse delegate to the UAP? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verify tube placement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform oral care

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer tube feeding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain vital signs and report results

choices.label: E
choices.text: Measure oxygen saturation

questions.rationale: Administering an enteral feeding may be delegated, at the nurse‘s discretion, to UAP in accordance with state regulations and facility policies and procedures. The nurse should verify tube placement and assess the patient prior to delegating this procedure.
