The UAP can  perform oral care and o bt a i n Nv iUt aRl sSigInNs,GinTcBlui.dCngOoMxygen saturation, and report results. Effective Care Environment: Safety and Infection Control 3rd Edition

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with a stroke that has altered her ability to see. The nurse knows which area of the brain was likely impacted by the stroke that is responsible for visual function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Parietal lobes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Frontal lobes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Occipital lobes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Temporal lobes

questions.rationale: The occipital lobes process visual information. The frontal lobes of the cerebrum are the areas of the brain responsible for voluntary motor function, concentration, communication, decision making, and personality. The parietal lobes are responsible for the sense of touch, distinguishing the shape and texture of objects. The temporal lobes are concerned with the senses of hearing and smell.

questions.stem: The family of a patient who was in a motor vehicle accident tells the nurse ―I‘m just not the person I was before the crashN.‖ TRh e nIursGe reBco.gCnizeMs this is likely because of the injury to what area of brain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Parietal lobes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Frontal lobes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Occipital lobes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Temporal lobes

questions.rationale: U S N T O The frontal lobes of the cerebrum are the areas of the brain responsible for voluntary motor function, concentration, communication, decision making, and personality. The parietal lobes are responsible for the sense of touch, distinguishing the shape and texture of objects. The temporal lobes are concerned with the senses of hearing and smell. The occipital lobes process visual information. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the family of a patient in the intensive care unit a bout the patient‘s cognitive status, including the current problem of delirium. Which statement by the family indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―The delirium can be caused by sensory overload.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―The delirium is reversible.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―The delirium is a mood disorder.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―The delirium is a state of confusion.‖

questions.rationale: Delirium is a reversible state of acute confusion. It is characterized by a disturbance in consciousness or a change in cognition that develops over 1 to 2 days and is caused by a medical condition. Delirium may occur in intensive care patients as a result of sensory overload. It is not a mood disorder.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with depression. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Depression can be caused by chemical changes in the brain.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Depression is always treated with medication.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Depression is a mood disorder. ‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Depression can have a rapid onset.‖

questions.rationale: Depression is usually reversible with treatment either by eliminating the underlying cause, providing counseling, or prescribing antidepressive agents. Depression is a mood disorder and is believed to be caused by chemical changes in the brain. Depression usually has a rapid onset, and the patient‘s mood is constant.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is complaining of tingling in the hands and fingers. The nurse knows this is a sign of what electrolyte imbalance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hyponatremia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypernatremia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypocalcemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypercalcemia

questions.rationale: Tactile disturbances, such as tingling and numbness around the mouth and in the fingers, are signs of hypocalcemia. Mental changes are associated with both hypercalcemia and hypocalcemia. Both hypernatremia and hyponatremia have symptoms of central nervous system disorder. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge instructions to an older adult who is being discharged with orthostatic hypotension. Which response by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should take my blood pressure once a day at home.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should get up quickly to avoid my blood pressure dropping.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should drink plenty of water during the day.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should get up slowly and carefully.‖

questions.rationale: In orthostatic hypotension, dizziness and loss of consciousness may occur if a patient changes position too quickly. Instead they should change positions slowly. A patient can take their blood pressure at home to monitor it. Drinking water will keep them hydrated.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing the patient‘s ability to hear and knows which is the correct procedure for the doing this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nurse whispers to the patient while standing on each side of the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The nurse speaks in a normal voice while standing on each side of the patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nurse speaks in a normal voice while standing directly in front of the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nurse speaks in a normal voice while standing slightly behind the patient.

questions.rationale: Hearing ability can be determined by observing the patient‘s conversation and responses and by talking with the patient in a normal conversational tone while standing slightly behind t he patient. If the patient does not respond appropriately, a hearing impairment may exist. Standing in front of the patient allows the patient to read your lips and will not  detect a hearing loss. A whispered voice will also give a false reading.

questions.stem: The nurse notices her 50-year-old patient is holding the lunch menu at arm‘s length while trying to read the choices. The nurse knows this is an indication of which condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retinopathy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presbyopia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cataracts

choices.label: D
choices.text: Macular degeneration

questions.rationale: The patient demonstrates presbyopia by holding reading materials at a distance or by being unable to read normal-sized or small print. Retinopathy is damage to the retina and occurs in diabetics. Cataracts are a clouding of the lens. Macular degeneration is a chronic condition that causes loss of vision in the center of your field of vision.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge education to the patient with diabetes regarding foot care. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I can go barefoot outside only in the summer.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should wear good fitting shoes.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I cannot soak my feet in a hot tub.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I can use lotion on my feet.‖

questions.rationale: Diabetic patients should not go barefoot outside even in the summer as they often have neuropathy, which decreases the patient‘s ability to discern touch, especially in the lower extremities. This can lead to foot injuries that can become infected and are slow to heal. The patient should wear good fitting shoes, should avoid extreme temperatures, and can use lotion to keep their skin moist to avoid overly dry skin.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which goal to be most appropriate for the Nursing diagnosis of acute confusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will use the call light before getting out of bed within 48 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will use a calendar to remember the date within 48 hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will respond appropriately to questions about place within 48 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will remain within the unit while in long-term care.

questions.rationale: The patient has acute confusion and therefore an appropriate early goal as the confusion resolves is to remember where they are. Remembering to use a call light would be appropriate for risk for falls. Using a calendar is appropriate for impaired memory and remaining in the unit is appropriate for chronic confusion.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which gNo alRUt o IbSe aGpNprBop.riCate fMo r the patient with a Nursing diagnosis of social isolation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will participate in cognitive exercises.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will interact with other residents during activities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will communicate basic needs through use of photos.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will remain within the unit while in long-term care.

questions.rationale: Interacting with others during activities is an appropriate goal to help the patient not feel so alone. Cognitive exercise is a goal for a patient with disturbed thought processes. Communication of basic needs through the use of photos is a goal for a patient with a diagnosis of impaired verbal communication and remaining in the unit is appropriate for chronic confusion.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the family to care for a patient at home with cognitive alterations. Which statement by the family indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should keep the home free of scissors.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should minimize the number of visitors.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should use push-button door locks.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―24-hour supervision may become necessary.‖

questions.rationale: Use of door locks that require a key may be necessary if the patient wanders. Keep the environment free of hazards such as sharp objects and minimize distractions. If the patient is not safe to be left alone, 24-hour supervision may be necessary.

questions.stem: The nurse is delegating care to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) to a patient who has sensory overload. Which statement by the UAP indicates a need for further orientation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should keep the noise levels low.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should schedule all the care together.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should keep the room well lit.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should allow the family to visit.‖

questions.rationale: To prevent or alleviate overload, the nurse reduces sensory stimuli, dimming unnecessary lights and turning down the sound on alarms if possible. Nursing care is planned so that the patient is not constantly disturbed. Visitation by family provides reality orientation and a soothing, recognizable presence for some patients experiencing overload.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge instructions to a patient with visual alterations. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should make sure the pNaUssaRgSewIaNyGs aTrBe w.iCdeO.‖M

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should remove all the throw rugs.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should keep the lights dim.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I can use a cane to feel for objects in front of me.‖

questions.rationale: Bright lighting in hallways and stairways prevents falls by the patient who has limited vision. Furniture is placed to allow wide passageways. Throw rugs, which are a tripping hazard, are removed. If vision is severely limited, use of a cane or walking stick held slightly in front helps the patient feel objects in his/her path.

questions.stem: The nurse is completing an assessment of an older adult and notices some cognitive impairment not normally associated with aging. Which of these alterations would prompt further follow-up? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient does not remember where her son lives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is unable to balance her checkbook.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient got lost in a city she never traveled to before.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient often has difficulty remembering words.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient got lost going to her usual grocery store.

questions.rationale: Symptoms of cognitive impairment include disorientation, loss of language skills, loss of the ability to calculate, poor judgment, and memory loss. If a patient exhibits these symptoms, further investigation is needed. Some decline in cognitive function occurs with aging, such as the ability to navigate easily in new areas.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing education to the family of a patient being discharged with dementia. Which statement by the family indicates an appropriate level of understanding of  dementia? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―The condition is permanent and has an acute onset.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Alzheimer is the most common type of dementia.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―The condition worsens over time.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I should observe for wandering behavior.‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―Agitation can be worse in the evening.‖

questions.rationale: Dementia, which is a permanent decline in mental function, has a subtle onset. The most common type of dementia is Alzheimer disease. Dementia is not reversible and worsens over time. Behavioral problems that arise in dementia patients include wandering, agitation, repetitive behaviors, and sundowning, or worsening of agitation and confusion in the evening.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who suffered a stroke on the right side of the brain. The nurse is careful to implement what safety measures? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Puts a picture board in the room to communicate with the patient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Places the call light on the patient‘s left side.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leaves a light on in the bathroom at night for good visibility.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Places the call light on the patient‘s right side.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Makes sure there are no trip hazards in the patient‘s room.

questions.rationale: If the damage is on the right side of the brain, there is loss of sensation and motor function in the extremities on the left side of the body and visual-spatial problems occur. Therefore, placing the call light on the side where the patient is likely to be strong is important. The patient will not necessarily have communication problems but might have visual problems, so the bathroom light is helpful. If the damage is on the left side of the brain, there is loss of sensation and motor function in the extremities on the right side of the body and problems with speech occur.

questions.stem: The nurse is performing a health history to determine the patient‘s cognitive status. Which questions will be best suited to elicit the information needed? (Select all that apply.) U S

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Are you able to drive to the store or do errands?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Do you have any pain?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Is your vision blurry?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Are you able to smell different foods?‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―Have you noticed any difficulty adding up numbers?‖

questions.rationale: Driving and adding numbers relates to cognitive ability. The remaining three options have a sensory focus.

questions.stem: The nurse is performing a health history to determine the patient‘s sensory status. Which questions will be best suited to elicit the information needed? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Do you ever lose your balance?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Do you wear glasses?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Do you like to read the newspaper?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Can you feel the difference between hot and cold water?‖

choices.label: E
choices.text: ―Do you wear a hearing aid?‖

questions.rationale: Balance, eyesight, hearing, and sensation are all sensory function. Asking if the patient likes the newspaper does not specifically address vision. M S C : NC L E X C l i e n t Nee d s CNa tegRor y:IP hyGs i o lBo g.i cCa l InMtegrity

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a diabetic patient who has had a long history of poor glucose control. For what complications is the patient at risk? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sudden loss of consciousness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diabetic retinopathy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stroke

choices.label: D
choices.text: Peripheral neuropathy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Memory loss

questions.rationale: Long-term complications of hyperglycemia may contribute to cognitive and sensory deficits such as memory loss. They also can lead to diabetic retinopathy, periphera l neuropathy, and stroke. Loss of consciousness is usually seen with hypoglycemia in diabetics.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is hospitalized with cognitive impairment and recognizes which interventions will assist the patient in orientation? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep a photo of the family in the room.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use a clock on the wall.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Make sure the room is kept bright and well lit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid moving the patient from room to room.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Have each nurse introduce himself or herself to the patient.

questions.rationale: The hospitalized patient with cognitive alterations is oriented by use of a clock, a calendar, and statements about the name of the location or name of the hospital. Orientation to person, place, and time is ongoing. Staff members are always identified by name, both verbally and nonverbally (with a name tag). The patient‘s environment is kept as constant as possible and moving the patient from room to room is avoided. Some familiar objects, such as a family photo, are placed near the patient if the hospital stay is longer than a few days. The environment is kept free of distractions such as loud noises and bright lights. Natural lighting to provide the patient with orientation to time of day can be accomplished by opening blinds or curtains during the day and darkening the room at night.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with expressive aphasia. Which interventions will assist the nurse in communicating with the patient? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use simple phrases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Speak loudly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use yes/no questions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use a picture board.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Be patient and unrushed.

questions.rationale: If a patient has expressive aphasia, he or she understands language but is unable to answer questions, name common obNjeUctsR, SorIstNatGeTsiBm.plCe Oi dMe a s . The patient can answer yes/no questions by shaking the head. The patient might be able to point to pictures to express needs. For any type of aphasia, being patient and not rushing will make communication less stressful.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with receptive aphasia. Which interventions will assist the nurse in communicating with the patient? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use simple phrases.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Speak louder than usual.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stand in front of the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use a picture board.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Be patient and unrushed.

questions.rationale: A patient with receptive aphasia cannot understand written or spoken language. Using simple phrases and talking either softly or loudly will not assist that patient. The sensory pathways are intact, but the words do not make sense. A picture board could be used by the nurse when assessing needs. As the patient participates in speech therapy, the ability to understand simple phrases may develop. Standing in front of the patient when talking may give non-verbal clues to the message.

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing discharge instructions for a patient who has tactile alterations in his legs. Which instructions would be included? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verify bath water temperature is approximately 39.5 °C.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Do not use hot or cold therapy on any extremity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use sturdy shoes when walking outside or on hard surfaces.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Report any changes in skin color on your legs to your health care provider.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Set your water heater so that scalding is not possible.

questions.rationale: Bath water temperature should be approximately 37.8 °C (100 °F), so 39.5 (103.1 °F) is too hot. Hot and cold therapy should not be used on the affected extremities, although it can be used on other areas of the body. Sturdy shoes can prevent foot injuries when there is decreased sensation in the lower extremities. Any decrease in sensation, change in the color of the skin, or wounds are reported to the health care provider. Water heaters are set so that scalding is not possible.

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing discharge instructions for a patient who has equilibrium alterations. Which instructions will the nurse include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use grab bars in the tub and/or shower at home.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keep rooms well-lit and focus ahead when walking.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Change positions quickly NtoUaRvSoiIdNdiGzzTinBe.ssC. OM

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use a cane or walker for stability.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ride in the back seat of the car and look ahead.

questions.rationale: The patient experiencing dizziness or vertigo exercises caution when changing positions. The patient suffering from motion sickness needs to ride in the front seat of the car and look far ahead through the car windshield. Keeping rooms well-lit and focusing ahead when walking, using grab bars in the shower and/or tub, and using canes or walkers are all good safety measures. Changing positions quickly may lead to dizziness. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse knows the one theory explaining the variation in response to stress among individuals is identified by which term?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stress appraisal

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sense of coherence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allostasis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Homeostasis

questions.rationale: Sense of coherence (SOC) is a characteristic of personality that references one‘s perception of the world as comprehensible, meaningful, and manageable. Stress appraisal is the automatic, often unconscious assessment of a demand or stressor. Allostasis is an alternative term for the stress response. Homeostasis is the tendency of the body to seek and maintain a condition of balance or equilibrium. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Psychosocial Integrity

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a new diagnosis of diabetes type 2. Which statement indicates a negative coping response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I will look up informatioNn oRn t hIe InGternBet.aCbouMt diabetes.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I will join a support groupU.‖ SNT  O

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I will only focus on learning to manage my medication first.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I will make changes slowly so I can adapt to each change.‖

questions.rationale: When the patient puts limits on learning by stating he/she will only learn about medication, he/she is using avoidance strategies to alleviate stress. Using strategies such as information gathering (seeking information about diabetes) is positive. Joining support groups and making changes slowly to adapt is also taking direct action by moving forward.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is undergoing a major cardiac procedure. When the patient complains of a racing heart and nausea, the nurse recognizes these complaints as part of what hormone response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sense of coherence

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stress appraisal

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fight or flight

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sympathoadrenal response

questions.rationale: In the ―fight or flight‖ response, the corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) released by the hypothalamus stimulates the pituitary to release adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH). These hormones increase the heart rate, resulting in increased cardiac output, and the motility of the digestive tract is decreased, slowing digestive processes that could result in abdominal distress. Sense of coherence (SOC) is a characteristic of personality that references one‘s perception of the world as comprehensible, meaningful, and manageable. Stress appraisal is the automatic, often unconscious, assessment of a demand, or stressor. The sympathoadrenal response is a consequence of hypothalamic activation in sympathetic stimulation, which triggers epinephrine and norepinephrine release from the adrenal medulla.

questions.stem: When the nurse measures the patient‘s blood glucose levels after an acute myocardial infarction (MI), the nurse knows this action is based on which rationale?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Damaged muscle tissue releases glucose.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Corticosteroids increase glucose.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myocardial infarctions are often seen in diabetics.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All patients should have their blood glucose checked.

questions.rationale: The endocrine system responds to stress on the body such as what happens during an acute MI. Corticosteroids are important in the stress response because they increase serum glucose levels and inhibit the inflammatory response. Although MIs can be seen in diabetics, there is nothing to indicate this patient is diabetic. All patients do not routinely have their blood glucose checked regularly. Applying

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching a patient about the difference between mild anxiety and moderate anxiety. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Mild anxiety can help me remember things.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Moderate anxiety will narrow my focus.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Mild anxiety will help me be creative.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Moderate anxiety will increase my perception.‖

questions.rationale: Moderate anxiety narrows a person‘s focus, dulls perception, and may challenge a person to pay attention or use appropriate problem-solving skills. Mild anxiety can be motivational, foster creativity, and increase a person‘s ability to think clearly.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge instructions for a patient with multiple sclerosis. Which discharge instruction is aimed at preventing a future exacerbation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Engage in some form of exercise as tolerated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid highly stressful situations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check your skin regularly for pressure sores.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Eat a diet with lots of fiber. U S N T O

questions.rationale: High stress levels are known to exacerbate multiple sclerosis and other autoimmune diseases. Exercise helps keep muscles loose and helps with balance. Assessing skin for pressure sores and eating a diet with high fiber prevents complications from multiple sclerosis. Analyzing

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing level of stress in a patient from another culture. Which question is the most appropriate in helping the nurse understand the impact of the patient‘s belief system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Do you engage in prayer to help you during times of stress?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Do you go to church or other form of organized worship?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Do you have certain beliefs that are helpful during times of stress?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Do you want spiritual counseling while you are here?‖

questions.rationale: The nurse needs to obtain a knowledge base of the patient‘s culture as well as identify health beliefs and cultural values from the patient‘s worldview. Asking the patient specific questions about prayer or church or spiritual counseling is inappropriate until the nurse first understands what the patient‘s own beliefs and practices are.

questions.stem: The nurse is performing a physical assessment of patient who is undergoing a bone marrow biopsy. What finding by the nurse indicates the patient is experiencing stress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure of 120/84N R I G B.C M

choices.label: B
choices.text: Temperature of 99.5 °F (37.5 °C)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heart rate of 110 beats/min

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respiratory rate of 10 breaths/min

questions.rationale: The release of hormones increases the heart rate, resulting in increased cardiac outp ut and elevated blood pressure. A reading of 120/84 is a normal blood pressure, and temperature is elevated is indicative of an infection. The respiratory rate increases in stress not decreases.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing the patient‘s use of coping skills in response to stressful situations. The nurse identifies which question to be the most useful?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Have you been evaluated for stress?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Do you have someone you can go to for help when you are stressed?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―How have you managed stressful situations in the past?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Does stress cause you to experience muscle tension or headaches?‖

questions.rationale: The use of open-ended questions assists in obtaining accurate information regarding the patient‘s stressors and coping skills. Questions that elicit yes/no answers will not allow the patient to provide as much information. Asking the patient about headaches and tension is asking about physical symptoms, not coping skills.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient on a medical-surgical inpatient unit when the patient tells the nurse he is very sad and is considering suicide. What is the first thing the nurse should do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the health care provider.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Make a referral to psychiatric services.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implement one-on-one observations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document in the electronic medical record.

questions.rationale: Verbalization of suicidal ideation or a suicide plan must be taken seriously. In the case of a hospitalized patient, one-on-one observation should be implemented to ensure patient safety. Once the patient is under observation, the health care provider is notified to put in the referral; nurses generally do not put in the referral. Documentation is always done after the patient‘s safety is ensured.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which goal to be appropriate for the nursing diagnosis of Difficulty coping?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will report an ability to remember discharge instructions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s family will understand how to access respite care services.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will discuss possible coping strategies during weekly counseling sessions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will attend an NoUnlRinSeIsuNpGpoTrtBg.roCuOp Mw e ekl y.

questions.rationale: An appropriate goal for Difficulty coping would be to discuss coping strategies. Remembering discharge instructions is an appropriate goal for Anxiety. Understanding how to access respite care services is an appropriate goal for Caregiver stress. Attending a support group is an appropriate goal for Difficulty coping.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which goal to be appropriate for the nursing diagnosis of Anxiety?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will attend a weekly support group.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will discuss possible coping strategies during weekly office visits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will report increased ability to concentrate on care instructions before discharge.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s family will use respite care once a week for the next month.

questions.rationale: Attending a weekly support group is an appropriate goal for Difficulty coping. An appropriate goal for Ineffective coping would be to discuss possible coping strategies during weekly visits. Using respite care once a week for the next month is an appropriate goal for Caregiver stress.

questions.stem: The nurse knows which goal to be appropriate for the nursing diagnosis of Caregiver stress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will report an ability to focus on discharge instructions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The caregiver will attend a coping skills class on a weekly basis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Caregiver will use respite care for the family loved one once a week for the next month.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will discuss strategies for coping with relationship violence within 24 hours.

questions.rationale: The patient will discuss possible coping strategies during weekly office visits is an a ppropriate goal for Difficulty coping. The patient will report an ability to focus on disc harge instructions is an appropriate goal for Anxiety. Relationship violence is not related.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that when coordination between multiple health care disciplines is needed, which role should be utilized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pastoral care

choices.label: B
choices.text: Case manager

choices.label: C
choices.text: Social worker

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dietitian

questions.rationale: If coordination of care between multiple health care disciplines is needed, a case manager is used. Pastoral care plays a s i gNn i fRi caUnIt ro Gle i nBa.dCd r e sMs i n g stress and anxiety issues when thepatient has a preferred religion or strong faith background. A social worker identifies appropriate services and resources. A dietitian can provide education regarding dietary needs and food choices.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing education to a patient around anger management strategies. Which statement indicates a need for further education by the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Exercise can help me deal with the anger.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I can use humor.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I can punch things.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I can take a time-out.‖

questions.rationale: Strategies should focus on nonviolent methods. Some anger management interventions include expressing feelings in a calm, non-confrontational manner; exercising; identifying potential solutions; taking a time-out; forgiving; diffusing the situation with humor; owning one‘s feelings; and breathing deeply.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the patient about alternative therapies. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for more information?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alternative therapies can include relaxation techniques.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alternative therapies are used in conjunction with medical therapies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alternative therapies can be used when patients are experiencing stress.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Some alternative therapists require certification.

questions.rationale: Alternative therapies are used in place of medical treatment. These types of interventions are useful when patients are experiencing physiologic and psychological responses to stress. Some complementary and alternative therapies such as therapeutic touch, Reiki, biofeedback, and massage therapy require additional certification and training, whereas muscle relaxation and guided imagery do not.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating the patient on the use of relaxation therapy. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should relax my muscles from head to toe.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I visual the relaxed muscle.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should do this three times a week.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I focus on muscles that are tense.‖

questions.rationale: This technique should be done daily. Typically, relaxation progresses from head to toe. With practice, the patient visualizes an image of the relaxed muscles and will be able to relax muscles from the mental i m aNg eU. RPrSoIgrNesGsiTveBr.elCaxOatMion is implemented by having patient‘s focus on muscles that are tensed and then intentionally relax those muscle groups.

questions.stem: The nurse is seeing a patient during a follow-up visit after discharge in which the patient had a nursing diagnosis of Difficulty coping. Which statement by the patient would be a cause for concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I am sleeping better most nights.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I feel less anxious.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I do not need to do the relaxation exercises anymore.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I am continuing my exercises every day.‖

questions.rationale: Patients need to continue using the stress-reduction techniques to maintain a feeling of well- being. Once stress decreases, patients typically report feeling better, sleeping more soundly, and feeling less anxious. Continuing their positive activities such as exercising is good.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that when patients are experiencing stress, which physiologic changes can be seen in their signs and symptoms? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase in heart rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flaccid muscles

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pupil dilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decrease in blood pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increase in respiratory rate

questions.rationale: The physiologic response to stress, whether physical or psychological, is activation of the autonomic nervous system, resulting in an increase in heart rate, blood pressure, and respirations along with pupil dilation and muscle tension and decreased blood flow to the skin.

questions.stem: The nurse knows when the body responds to the release of hormones during ―fight or flight,‖ that response includes which physiological signs? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased respiratory rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Slowing of the digestive process

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glucose being mobilized from the liver

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pupils dilating

choices.label: E
choices.text: Smooth muscles in the bronchi constricting

questions.rationale: The release of hormones increases the heart rate, resulting in increased cardiac output, and elevated blood pressure. TheNre isRanIi n c rGeUa s eBSi.n Ct h e Mf low of blood to muscles at the expense ofthe digestive and other systems not immediately needed in the fight-or-flight response. Smooth muscles in the bronchi relax and dilate the bronchi and smaller airways, and the respiratory rate increases, allowing for an enhanced flow of well-oxygenated blood to muscles and other organs. The motility of the digestive tract is decreased, slowing digestive processes, but glucose and fatty acids are mobilized from the liver and other stores to support increased mental activities (alertness) and skeletal muscle function. Pupillary dilation produces a larger visual field. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Psychosocial Integrity

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which personality factors that have been shown to buffer the impact of stress? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resilience

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sense of coherence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gender

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hardiness

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coping style

questions.rationale: Personality factors such as resilience, hardiness, and sense of coherence can buffer t he impact of stress, reducing the negative consequences. Gender is not a personality factor . Coping style refers to a pattern of measures taken to relieve stress but is not a personality factor.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which factors that center on the childhood stress related to school experiences? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Goal achievement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Family dissolution

choices.label: C
choices.text: Life changes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Test anxiety

choices.label: E
choices.text: Competition

questions.rationale: Childhood stress related to the school experience centers on competition, goal achievement, and test anxiety. Family dissolution and life changes are not related to the school experience. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Psychosocial Integrity

questions.stem: The nurse working with older adults wants to support healthy coping strategies. What actions by the nurse are most appropriate? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Installing boxing equipment in the recreation room

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide reminiscing sessions for the adults to share personal stories

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arrange for gentle yoga to be provided at the senior center

choices.label: D
choices.text: Create activities designed to distract them from their losses

choices.label: E
choices.text: Encourage the adults to eat frequent, healthy snacks

questions.rationale: To promote health coping in older adults, the nurse would provide reminiscing sessions, yoga, and would encourage small healthy snacks as this population frequently loses their appet ite when stressed. Boxing equipment might cause the adults to focus on anger. Distraction can be a negative or positive coping mechanism.

questions.stem: The nurse manager of a busy oncology unit is concerned about compassion fatigue among the nursing staff. Which signs and symptoms would alert the nurse to this problem? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nurses become very emotionally upset without an apparent cause.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nurses start to avoid caring for certain patients.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nurses start to call in sick more often.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nurses begin working more overtime.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nurses have difficulty showing empathy for patients.

questions.rationale: Compassion fatigue occurs when deeply caring and empathetic nurses become overwhelmed by the constant needs of patients and families. Symptoms include mood swings, avoidance of working with some patients, frequent sick days, irritability, reduced memory, poor concentration, and a decreased ability to show empathy.

questions.stem: The nurse manager of the unit is implementing a program to assist the nursing staff in managing compassion fatigue. Which interventions will be the most successful? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Support group that nurses can participate in that meets on the unit

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exercise competitions to encourage nurse to exercise and log their time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Organized break times so nurses can get off the unit for breaks and lunches

choices.label: D
choices.text: Quiet area on the unit where the nurses can go during break

choices.label: E
choices.text: Promotion of work-life balance

questions.rationale: To care most effectively for others, nurses must first take time to care for themselves. Many of the stress reduction interventions incorporated into patient care plans can be effective in addressing the stressors faced by nurses. Exercise, balanced nutrition, and mindfulness therapy have been shown to help health care professionals in coping with the demands of patient care. Interventions designed specifically to prevent nurse burnout and address compassion fatigue include mentoring programs, quiet areas on a nursing unit for relaxation, availability of pastoral care, the sharing of feelings with trusted colleagues, and promotion of work-life balance. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse identifies what physiological response occurs with the onset of darkness and in preparation for sleep?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cortisol levels peak

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cortisol levels increase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Core body temperature increases

choices.label: D
choices.text: Melatonin levels increase

questions.rationale: Melatonin levels increase and core temperature and cortisol levels decrease with the onset of darkness. Cortisol levels peak at 6 a.m.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes what function of the reticular activating system (RAS)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Records brain waves and other variables.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relays motor impulse to the hypothalamus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Influences patterns of biological functioning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Is affected by the light-d aNr k cRyclIe. GB.CM

questions.rationale: The RAS receives sensory impulses from the spinal cord and relays motor impulses to the thalamus and all parts of the cerebral cortex. Polysomnography is the recording of brain waves and other physiologic variables, such as muscle activity and eye movements, during sleep. The circadian rhythms influence patterns of biological and behavioral functions, and the sleep-wake circadian rhythm is affected by the light-dark cycle.

questions.stem: The nurse is educating a patient about taking measures to help avoid disruption to the circadian rhythm. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I know the circadian rhythm influences biological functions.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I know the circadian rhythm exists only in humans.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I know the sleep-wake circadian rhythm is impacted by the light-dark cycle.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―The most familiar circadian rhythm is the day-night 24-hour cycle.‖

questions.rationale: Biological rhythms exist in plants, animals, and humans. In humans, these biorhythms, along with internal and external factors, affect sleep. The most familiar rhythm is the day-night, 24-hour circadian rhythm cycle. Circadian rhythms influence patterns of biological and behavioral functions. Some creatures are diurnal, or primarily active during the day, whereas others are nocturnal, with most of their activity during the night.

questions.stem: The nurse teaches the patient what information about polysomnograpy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: This is the recording of brain waves and other variables.

choices.label: B
choices.text: This is the relay of motor impulse to the hypothalamus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: This is the patterns of biological functioning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: This is the recording of seizure activity in the brain.

questions.rationale: Polysomnography is the recording of brain waves and other physiologic variables, such as muscle activity and eye movements, during sleep. The reticular activating system (RAS) receives sensory impulses from the spinal cord and relays motor impulses to the thalamus. The circadian rhythms influence patterns of biological and behavioral functions. An electroencephalogram is used to record seizure activity in the brain.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which se NquUeRncSeItoNbGeTthBe.uCsuOalMprogression of sleep?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NREM 1-3 then REM, then back through NREM 1 and 2

choices.label: B
choices.text: REM then NREM 1-4, then back through NREM 2 and 3

choices.label: C
choices.text: NREM 1-3 then back through NREM 3 and 2 then REM

choices.label: D
choices.text: REM then NREM 1-4 then back through NREM 3

questions.rationale: The usual sleep sequence for a person is a fairly rapid progression through NREM 1 through 3, back through NREM 3 and 2, and then into REM sleep. There is no NREM stage 4.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge instructions to the parents of a toddler about sleeping habits. Which statement indicates further education is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Sleep needs may change during growth spurts.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Children sleep 12 hours a day.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Toddlers will often resist going to bed.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―The bedtime routine can vary.‖

questions.rationale: The regular bedtime routine should be consistently followed. Children need 11 to 14 hours of sleep a day, and toddlers and preschoolers may exhibit resistance to going to bed. Sleep nee ds fluctuate with growth spurts.

questions.stem: When the nurse is explaining cataplexy to the patient, which description should be included?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is an uncontrolled desire to sleep.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is falling asleep for several minutes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is loss of voluntary muscle tone.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a sleep cycle that begins with NREM.

questions.rationale: Cataplexy is characterized by the sudden loss of voluntary muscle tone; vivid hallucinations during sleep onset or on awakening; and brief episodes of total paralysis at the beginning or end of sleep. An uncontrolled desire to sleep and falling asleep for several minutes define narcolepsy. Narcolepsy begins with REM sleep.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge instructions to a patient who has had sleep alterations. Which statement by the patient indicates further education is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should avoid drinking caffeine too close to bedtime.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I should not eat anything too close to bedtime.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I should exercise regularly to help with sleeping.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I can gain weight if I don‘t sleep enough.‖

questions.rationale: Going to bed hungry or eating a large, heavy, or spicy meal just before going to bed can interfere with sleep. Bedtime snacks that contain complex carbohydrates are recommended to promote calmness and relaxation. Highly caffeinated substances such as coffee, cola, and chocolate are central nervous system stimulants that can disrupt the sleep cycle. Exercise can assist with weight- loss efforts and promote fatigue and relaxation, but excessive exercise, especially in the evening, interferes with sleep. Both children and adults who sleep less than the recommended number of hours each night are more likely to be overweight.

questions.stem: The nurse is performing an assessment of the patient‘s sleep patterns. Which question by the nurse will elicit the best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Do you feel rested when you awaken?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―What is your normal eating pattern?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Do you awaken during the night?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Do you drink beverages with caffeine?‖

questions.rationale: When gathering information about the patient‘s sleep patterns, the best questions will be to ask if they awaken during the night. Asking the patient if they feel rested evaluates quality of sleep. Normal eating pattern and drinking beverages with caffeine establishes if they have any habits that might interfere with sleep.

questions.stem: A patient has Sleep deprivation. What finding by the nurse best indicates goal achievement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remains asleep for 6 to 7 hours consistently for 1 week.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Falls asleep within 15 minutes of going to bed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reports an ability to concentrate on tasks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Verbalizes understanding of medication instructions.

questions.rationale: The patient remaining asleep for 6 to 7 hours consistently for 1 week is an appropriate goal for Sleep deprivation. The patient falling asleep within 15 minutes of going to bed is a goal for Insomnia. The patient reporting an ability to concentrate on tasks is a goal for Anxiety. The patient repeating medication instructions is an appropriate goal for any patient.

questions.stem: The nurse is working with a patient who has been advised to take 2 20-minute naps during the day for fatigue. After a week, the patient states the naps have not helped. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Maybe that is too much sleep for you during the day.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Why don‘t you try one 40-minute nap instead?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Let‘s explore some sleeping medications for you to try.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―It often takes a few weeks for napping to help.‖

questions.rationale: Taking 2 20-minute naps is an appropriate action for the patient with fa tigue to implement. Many non-pharmacological interventions take weeks to become effective, so the nurse would explain that fact and encourage the patient to continue the napping.

questions.stem: The nurse wants to help a hospitalized patient get more sleep. What intervention will be most helpful?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Allow the patient an hour nap during the day shift.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer sleeping medication if the patient can‘t go to sleep after an hour.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place a ―do not disturb‖ sign on the door for the duration of the night shift.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cluster cares so the patient gets at least 90 minutes of uninterrupted sleep at night.

questions.rationale: Being able to sleep for at least 90 minutes without interruption will best help this patient as the normal sleep cycle is approximately 90 minutes long. An hour nap is probably too long even in the hospital. Administering sleeping pills is an alternative if the patient truly can‘t sleep while hospitalized, but this is not the preferred solution. Not disturbing a hospitalized patient for an entire night shift is unrealistic.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge education for a patient with narcolepsy. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Daytime naps are helpful.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Taking the medication will cure it.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―High protein meals are helpful.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I need to avoid alcohol.‖

questions.rationale: There is no cure currently for narcolepsy. Treatment includes a regular exercise routine, a regular sleep routine, daytime naps if possible, light meals high in protein to maintain alertness, and vitamins. Avoiding alcohol, heavy meals, long-distance driving, and long periods of sitting is helpful.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing d isch aNrgeRUed uIcSat iGNon TBfo.r aCpaMtie nt with restless leg syndrome. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further instruction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I should avoid all caffeine. ‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I can do leg massage and knee bends.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Taking magnesium supplements may be helpful.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Taking a walk regularly may be helpful.‖

questions.rationale: Decreasing caffeine is helpful but does not need to be eliminated. Massaging the legs, walking, or doing deep knee bends may temporarily relieve symptoms. Supplements to correct deficiencies in iron, folate, and magnesium may be helpful.

questions.stem: Which task is inappropriate for the nurse to delegate to the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Providing oral care

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evaluating sleep patterns

choices.label: C
choices.text: Providing bedtime routines

choices.label: D
choices.text: Documenting sleep hours

questions.rationale: The nurse may not delegate assessment activities such as evaluating sleeping patterns. The nurse may delegate activities to help the patient prepare for sleep such as oral care and other bedtime routines. The UAP may document the hours slept.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing discharge instructions for the patient with sleep pattern disturbances. Which statement by the patient indicates a need for further education?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―It is a good idea to have a bedtime routine.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―My bedtime routine can include watching TV in bed until I fall asleep.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I will keep my regular sleep pattern on the weekend.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―If I can‘t fall asleep, I will get out of bed and do something relaxing.‖

questions.rationale: Use the bedroom for sleep and sex only; do not watch television, read, study, or eat in there. A consistent bedtime routine signals to the mind and body that it is time to go to sleep. Maintaining a regular sleep pattern helps maintain circadian rhythms. Continue the regular sleep pattern on weekends and on holidays. Getting out of bed if unable to sleep trains the mind to sleep when in bed. If unable to fall asleep within 20 minutes, get out of bed and engage in a relaxing activity such as reading or listening to music until sleepy.

questions.stem: The nurse knows that during rapid eye movement (REM) sleep, which activities occur? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Memories are stored

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase in cerebral blood flow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Slow rhythmic scanning eye movements

choices.label: D
choices.text: Release of epinephrine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Repair of brain cells

questions.rationale: Rapid eye movement (REM) sleep occurs during deep sleep and is manifested by quick scanning movements of the eyes that are associated with dreaming. REM sleep is associated with memory storage, learning, increased cerebral blood flow, and epinephrine release. Repair of brain cells occurs during non–REM sleep.

questions.stem: The student nurse learns that during non–rapid eye movement (NREM) sleep, which activities occur? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Brain waves slow

choices.label: B
choices.text: Slow rhythmic scanning eye movements U S N

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dreaming

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drop in blood pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Conservation of energy

questions.rationale: During non –rapid eye  movement (NREM)  sleep, in  which REM does not occur, physiological activity is reduced, brain waves, breathing and heart rate slow, and blood p ressure drops. Slow scanning eye movements do not occur in either REM or NREM. Dreaming occurs in REM.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which changes in sleep patterns occur in the older adult? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sleep increases to approximately 8 to 10 hours a night.

choices.label: B
choices.text: REM sleep is shorter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stage 4 NREM is decreased.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The use of medication may interfere with sleep.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Older adults awaken more at night.

questions.rationale: Older adults sleep approximately 7 to 8 hours a night. The first REM stage is longer. There is no stage 4 REM. Deeper stages of sleep are shortened, resulting in less restorative sleep. A decline in health or the use of medications may interfere with sleep. Older adults awaken more at night and take longer to go back to sleep.

questions.stem: The nurse is providing community education on sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS). What information does the nurse include? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: SIDS is the second most common cause of death among infants (1 to 12 months).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The etiology remains largely unknown.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The most modifiable risk factor is sleeping supine.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk factors include being exposed to cigarette smoke.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is defined as sudden unexpected death.

questions.rationale: SIDS is the leading cause of death among infants 1 to 12 months of age. The etiology remains largely unknown. The most important modifiable SIDS risk factor appears to be prone sleeping. Risk factors include the infant‘s being exposed to cigarette smoke. Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) is defined as the sudden unexpected death of an infant younger than 1 year of age that remains unexplained after a thorough postmortem investigation.

questions.stem: The nurse recognizes which sleeping conditions are identified as dyssomnias? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Difficultly getting to sleep

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nocturnal enuresis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inability staying asleep

choices.label: D
choices.text: Being excessively sleepy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Falling asleep during the day

questions.rationale: Dyssomnias are disorders associated with getting to sleep, staying asleep, or being excessively sleepy. Nocturnal enuresis is a parasomnia.

questions.stem: The nurse manager is concerned about nursing staff who are working the night shift. What interventions can the manager suggest to help the nurses overcome shift-related sleep disturbances? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Power nap before leaving for the first night shift.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Get a minimum of 4 hours of sleep.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wear dark glasses when driving home from work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Seek exposure to bright light when waking.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Maintain a regular sleeping schedule when working and on nights off.

questions.rationale: Obtain a minimum of 6 hours of sleep. Maintain a regular sleep schedule when working and on nights off. Wear dark glasses that block blue light when driving home after night work. Seek exposure to bright light (sunlight is best) as soon as possible after waking. Before the first night shift, power-nap 3N0 tUoR9S0 ImNinGutTesBb.efCorOeMleaving for work.

questions.stem: The nurse conducting a sleep workshop in the community would identify which patients to be at risk for obstructive sleep apnea (OSA)? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deviated septum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recessed chin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alcohol use

choices.label: D
choices.text: Large neck

choices.label: E
choices.text: Recent tonsillectomy

questions.rationale: Risk factors for OSA include obesity, large neck circumference, smoking, alcohol use, and a family history of OSA. Structural abnormalities such as a recessed chin, abnormal upper-airway structures, deviated septum, nasal polyps, or enlarged tonsils can predispose a person to OSA.

questions.stem: The nurse is admitting a patient to the general medical–surgical unit. What should the nurse assess as part of a routine sleep assessment? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Usual sleeping and waking times

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bedtime routines

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sleeping environment preferences

choices.label: D
choices.text: Medications used for sleep

choices.label: E
choices.text: Any current life events

questions.rationale: A sleep assessment should be completed when a patient is admitted to a health facility. The nurse assesses the patient‘s usual sleeping and waking times, medications, illnesses, bedtime routines, and sleeping environment preferences and incorporates the information into the plan of care when possible. The nurse should also assess current life events and emotional status.

questions.stem: The nurse will include which interventions to help improve sleep quality during hospitalization on all patients‘ care plans? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maintaining sleep routines

choices.label: B
choices.text: Minimizing disruptions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Providing light snacks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using sleep medications

choices.label: E
choices.text: Using relaxation measures

questions.rationale: Medications would be used cNarUeRf ulSlyIaNnGd dToBn.otCalwMays improve sleep. Addressing the sleep environment, maintaining sleep routines, providing light snacks if allowed, and instituting relaxation measures will all improve sleep. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has diabetes. The patient reports compliance with the medical regime. Which test result indicates to the nurse that the patient has not been compliant with the treatment plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hemoglobin A1c 16%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Random blood sugar (RBS) 112 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 55 units/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 14 mm/hr

questions.rationale: Hemoglobin A1c (Hgb A1c), or glycosylated hemoglobin, testing evaluates blood sugar levels over a period of 2 to 3 months This blood test is performed to provide the primary care provider (PCP) with information about long-term blood sugar control. The normal value of Hgb A1c in patients without diabetes is 4% to 5.9%. The American Diabetes Association (2016) states that diabetes is diagnosed for Hgb A1c levels greater than 6.5%. A higher level indicates that the patient has had poor blood glucose control during the past few weeks, and increases the patient‘s risk of long-term complications from hyperglycemia. The other tests are not related to long-term diabetes control.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for an elderly patient with dementia. Which laboratory finding indicates to the nurse that that patient is often forgetting to eat meals?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Serum bilirubin 0.4 mg/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: PLT (platelet count) 425,000/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Serum cholesterol 175 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Albumin 1.4 g/dL

questions.rationale: Albumin level is an indicator of the patient‘s protein intake and nutritional status. Normal albumin level is 3.3 to 5 g/dL. It is an essential component of fluid balance, responsible for maintaining colloidal oncotic pressure in the vascular and extravascular spaces. Low levels of albumin may indicate malnutrition.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a deep leg wound that is badly infected. Which laboratory test results will the nurse expect to find in the patient‘s chart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) 6.5 mg/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevated serum bilirubin 0.5 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prothrombin time (PT) 11.5 sec U S N

questions.rationale: C-reactive protein (CRP) is produced by the liver in response to inflammation, tissue damage, and infection. Blood levels of CRP have been used as a marker for inflammatory and autoimmune disorders. The nurse would expect to see an elevated CRP in a patient with an infected wound. Creatinine is an indicator of kidney function, and bilirubin is an indicator of liver function. Prothrombin time indicates clotting ability of the blood, particularly when the patient is taking warfarin (Coumadin).

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a bleeding gastric ulcer. How will the nurse expect the patient‘s stool to appear?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Soft and formed with bright red streaks

choices.label: B
choices.text: Watery with particles of undigested food

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sticky and black

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hard lumps that are difficult to pass

questions.rationale: Bleeding anywhere along the GI tract results in blood in the stool. Bleeding that occurs in the upper GI tract produces stools that are black and tarry in appearance. Bleeding within the lower GI tract presents with soft stools that have bright red streaks. Watery stool with particles of food is indicative of gastroenteritis. Hard lumps that are difficult to pass indicate constipation, often from medications or lack of fiber in the diet. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is to have a noncontrast MRI scan performed. Which assessment finding leads the nurse to report that the patient may not be able to have the test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient has an implanted insulin pump.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is breastfeeding her newborn infant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient is severely allergic to iodine and latex.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has profound hearing loss.

questions.rationale: Any metal implants are a contraindication for an MRI scan because the scan uses powerful magnets. Insulin pumps often contain metal that can react with the strong magnets in the MRI machine. Breastfeeding is not a contraindication to MRI because there is no radiation exposure. No latex or iodine is used during MRI testing. Profound hearing loss will not be a problem, although MRI scanning is very loud.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has had severe acid reflux. Which test will allow the physician to directly check for damage to the esophagus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)

choices.label: B
choices.text: MRI scan with contrast

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abdominal ultrasound

choices.label: D
choices.text: Positron emission tomography (PET) scan

questions.rationale: EGD is performed using a lighted tube that allows for direct visualization of the esophagus, stomach, and upper duodenum. MRI, ultrasound, and PET scanning do not allow physicians to see the esophagus directly.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a woman who has a cyst in her breast that was found at her recent mammogram. The physician wants to make sure that the cyst is not malignant. Which test will be used to determine this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Needle aspiration with biopsy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paracentesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thoracentesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fiberoptic endoscopy

questions.rationale: Needle aspirations are procedures that are used to remove fluid and tissue for testing. A biopsy involves removing a larger collection of cells, as in a tumor or mass, and may be used to detect cancer in the skin, breast, or liver. Paracentesis is drainage of fluid from the abdomen, and thoracentesis is drainage of fluid from the pleural cavity. Fiberoptic endoscopy allows the physician to see inside the upper and/or lower GI tract.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is scheduled for a needle aspiration and biopsy to rule out cancer. Which Nursing diagnosis is appropriate and important for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anxiety related to potential for cancer diagnosis depending on biopsy results

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired health maintenance related to delayed insurance coverage for procedure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Powerlessness related to lengthy wait for diagnosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ineffective coping related to patient stated she is a little nervous about the test results

questions.rationale: Fear is an emotion commonly experienced by patients waiting for diagnostic tests and biopsy results. Impaired health maintenance related to delayed insurance coverage is not a priority diagnosis for this patient at this time. Powerlessness is about the patient‘s ability to control an outcome and is not related to the wait for test results. The patient statement of feeling a little nervous about the test results is not indicative of ineffective coping.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will be undergoing flexible sigmoidoscopy testing to screen for colon cancer. What goal will the nurse include in the patient‘s plan of care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient will verbalize understanding of preprocedure preparation to be completed at home the day before the test.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will feel comfortable about the upcoming test and have trust in the health care providers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will learn common side effects of the medications used to prepare the GI tract for endoscopy testing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will realize how important regular sigmoidoscopy testing is in the prevention of colon cancer.

questions.rationale: The patient will need to complete colon preparation prior to the sigmoidoscopy testing. The nurse must determine that the patient understands how and when to complete the prep. Having the patient verbalize understanding of the prep procedure is an objective goal so that the nurse can readily determine whether or not it has been met. The other goals are not objective or measurable, so the nurse cannot determine whether or not they have been met.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will be receiving iodine-based contrast medium for a CT scan. Which allergy should be reported to the technician and radiologist before the test is performed? a. Gluten and lactose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Strawberries and blueberNriesR IGB.CM

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peanuts and cashews

choices.label: D
choices.text: Shrimp and scallops U S N T O

questions.rationale: If the patient is undergoing an examination that involves an iodine contrast medium, check for a history of adverse reactions or allergies to iodine-containing food (e.g., shellfish, cabbage, kale, iodized salt). The other allergies are not related.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has just undergone bronchoscopy. The patient requests a drink of water. What is the nurse‘s best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide ice chips.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient for a gag reflex.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provide a small cup of ice water with a straw.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep the patient NPO.

questions.rationale: Numbing medication is applied to the back of the throat just before bronchoscopy. This may lead to swallowing difficulty and risk for aspiration until the gag reflex returns. The nurse should keep patient NPO until swallow, gag, and cough reflexes have returned. The nurse does not need to keep the patient NPO after the gag reflex returns so it should be checked in order to allow the patient to have fluids as soon as possible to relieve thirst. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will be undergoing bone marrow biopsy. Which statement by the patient indicates that additional teaching is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I will count the ceiling tiles when the doctor inserts the numbing medicine.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I will take acetaminophen later today if the site becomes uncomfortable.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I will squeeze your hand to help calm my fears about the test.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I will keep the biopsy site clean and dry for the next 24 hours.‖

questions.rationale: The patient will be positioned in the prone or lateral position for the test, so the patient will not be able to count ceiling tiles as a distraction during the numbing step of the test. The patient may take acetaminophen as needed for discomfort afterward. The biopsy site must be kept clean and dry for 24 hours after the biopsy to prevent infection. Holding the nurse‘s hand will help calm the patient before and during the procedure. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is sedated following a colonoscopy. Which is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide a quiet, dark envNiroUnRmSenIt NsoGtThaBt .thCe Op aMt i e n t can rest comfortably.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Monitor the patient‘s pulse oximetry and respirations closely.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inform the patient that the procedure has been completed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s bowel sounds and passage of flatus.

questions.rationale: The priority intervention for sedated patients is to monitor pulse oximetry and respirations closely because sedation may suppress the respiratory drive. The nurse should monitor vital signs until the patient is fully awake and observe stools for visible blood. The nurse should also instruct the patient to report any abdominal pain as these assessment findings are alerts for possible perforation of bowel, hypotension, and hemorrhage. Providing a quiet environment is nice for the patient, but dim lighting may impair the nurse‘s ability to assess the patient. Informing the patient that the procedure has been completed is not a priority. Assessing the patient‘s bowel sounds and passage of flatus is not as important as careful respiratory monitoring.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who recently had a liver biopsy. To whom must the nurse give the results?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s health care provider

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s insurance provider

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s spouse

questions.rationale: HIPAA protects the patient by requiring that testing results be shared only with health care professionals who need the information to provide treatment and with individuals designated in writing by the patient. The patient‘s provider will need the biopsy results to determine the patient‘s plan of care. The nurse does not give test results to the insurance company. The nurse may share the results with the patient or spouse, but it is not required, unless designated by the patient in writing.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is to collect a 24-hour urine specimen. Which statement by the patient indicates that additional teaching is required?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I will keep the urine container on ice to keep it chilled until I bring it to the lab.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I will start the test over if I forget and urinate into the toilet during the testing time.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I will start the test tomorrow after I urinate first thing in the morning.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I will drink extra fluids so that the lab will have a large specimen to test.‖

questions.rationale: Drinking extra fluids so that the lab will have an extra-large specimen to test is not done as part of 24-hour urine collection, and it may skew the test results. The specimen should be kept chilled on ice or in a refrigerator until it is brought to the lab. If the patient accidentally urinates in the toilet, the test must be started over again.
