Urine collection is started after the patient‘s first void of the morNnUinRg SinItoNthGeTtoBil.etC. OM NCLEX Client Needs Category: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient whose immune system is destroying red blood cells at a very rapid rate. Which test result will the nurse expect to see in the patient‘s chart as a result?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bilirubin level 4 mg/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: Platelet count 450,000/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Serum uric acid level 1.7 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Partial thromboplastin time 45 seconds

questions.rationale: An elevated bilirubin level is the result of increased red blood cell destruction. Normal bilirubin levels are 0.3 to 1.0 mg/dL. Increased platelet count, decreased serum acid level, and decreased partial thromboplastin times are not indicative of increased red blood cell destruction.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a urinary tract infection. Which test will indicate which antibiotics will be effective to treat the infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Complete blood count (CBC)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Culture and sensitivity (C&S)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal scan and angiography

choices.label: D
choices.text: Radioreceptor assay for HCG

questions.rationale: Culture and sensitivity are performed on specimens to determine which bacteria are causing the infection and which antibiotics will be effective treatment. CBC, renal scan, and radioreceptor assay for HCG will not indicate which antibiotics may be used to treat an infection.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has just undergone paracentesis. For which complication will the nurse carefully monitor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Collapse of the lung with shortness of breath

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fecal impaction from retained barium in the colon

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cerebrospinal fluid leak resulting in severe headache

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perforation of the bowel resulting in abdominal infection

questions.rationale: Paracentesis is drainage of fluid from the abdominal cavity. Since the needle is near the intestines, bowel perforation can occur, manifested by abdominal pain and fever as infection (peritonitis) sets in. Possible complications do not include lung collapse, CSF leak, or impaction.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is having blood drawn as part of preoperative testing. Which step is the most important to ensure the safety of the patient and the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensuring that the tourniquet is not left in place for too long

choices.label: B
choices.text: Using the smallest possible needle for venipuncture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Properly disposing of the needle after the specimen is obtained

choices.label: D
choices.text: Making sure that all of the collection tubes are filled completely

questions.rationale: Proper disposal of needles and sharps after procedures is essential for safe nursing practice to ensure the safety of staff as well as patients. Ensuring that the tourniquet is not left on too long, using the smallest needle possible, and making sure that all of the vials are filled are important steps in venipuncture, but only proper sharps disposal will help ensure the safety of the patient and the nurse.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with diabetes who will be doing fingerstick blood glucose testing at home. What is the best way for the nurse to ensure that the patient can perform the procedure correctly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Quiz the patient on the steps of the procedure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Have the patient perform the procedure in front of the nurse.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the patient if he has any questions about the test.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use terminology that the patient can easily understand.

questions.rationale: Having the patient successfully perform the procedure in front of the nurse is an excellent way for the nurse to ensure that the patient knows how to do it correctly. Quizzing the patient about the procedure, asking the patient if he/she has questions, and using understandable terminology are fine, but only a return demonstration will assess the patient‘s ability to perform the procedure successfully and correctly.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is anemic. Which CBC test results demonstrate that the patient‘s treatment plan is effective and the anemia is resolving? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Red blood cell count (RBC) 5.8 million/mm3

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hematocrit (HCT) 25%

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemoglobin (HGB) 14 g/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: White blood cell count (WNBCR) 4I5U0 0G/Sm mNB3.CT M O

choices.label: E
choices.text: Platelet count (PLT) 255,000/mm3

questions.rationale: Red blood cell count of 5.8 million and hemoglobin value of 14 g/dL are both norm al. Hematocrit level of 25% is very low and indicative of ongoing anemia. White blood cell and platelet counts are not checked for anemia.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has been having abdominal pain. The doctor suspects that the patient may have an abdominal aortic aneurysm. Which tests would confirm the doctor‘s suspicion? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan

choices.label: B
choices.text: Needle aspiration with biopsy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fiberoptic endoscopy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Computed tomography (CT) scan

choices.label: E
choices.text: Flexible sigmoidoscopy

choices.label: F
choices.text: Thoracentesis

questions.rationale: CT scan and MRI may be used to determine the presence of an abdominal aortic aneurysm. Endoscopy, needle aspiration, sigmoidoscopy, and thoracentesis will not help make this diagnosis.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is undergoing a liver biopsy. Which interventions will be included in the patient‘s care plan for the diagnosis of risk for infection: r/t invasive diagnostic procedure? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Monitor for and report redness, warmth, discharge, or fever promptly to the physician.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carefully maintain the sterile field during the biopsy procedure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach patient how to care for the biopsy site when procedure is completed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide a supportive, caring presence to minimize patient anxiety.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Provide information about the pathophysiology and treatment options for liver cancer.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Consider using healing touch and other mind-body-spirit interventions.

questions.rationale: Interventions for the Nursing diagnosis of risk for infection involve monitoring for signs and symptoms of infection, preventing contamination of supplies by maintaining a sterile field during the procedure, and teaching the patient how to care for the site afterward. Providing a caring presence, providing information about liver cancer, and using healing touch may be helpful for the patient but will not minimize the risk of infection. NURSIN GOTBBJ : .3C4O. 6M

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who needs to collect a 24-hour urine specimen at home. Which steps of specimen collection may be delegated to the assistant? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Label the urine container and lab slips with the patient‘s name and information.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s ability to collect the specimen as required.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Explain the procedure to the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain the urine container from the utility room or laboratory.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transport the specimen to the laboratory once it is collected.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Ensure that the correct test is ordered and collected.

questions.rationale: The assistant may label the container and lab slips, obtain the urine container from the utility room, and transport the specimen to the lab. These are tasks that do not require nursing judgment. Assessment of the patient is always done by the nurse, as well as explaining the procedure to the patient and ensuring that the correct test is performed.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is taking medication that is toxic to the liver. Which laboratory test results will be reviewed by the nurse to ensure that the patient‘s liver is tolerating the medication without damage to the organ? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR)

choices.label: F
choices.text: Fibrin degradation products (FDP)

questions.rationale: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) are indicators of liver function, and increased levels indicate liver damage from a variety of causes. BUN, ANA, ESR, and FDP are not indicators of liver function. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Reduction of Risk Potential 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which medication that has the highest potential for abuse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methylphenidate (Ritalin)—schedule II

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alprazolam (Xanax)—schedule IV

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acetaminophen & codeine (Tylenol #3)—schedule III

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diphenoxylate & atropine (Lomotil)—schedule V

questions.rationale: According to the Controlled Substances Act, drugs that have the potential for abuse/dependency are classified as schedule I-V. Schedule I drugs have no approved medical applications in the United States. Schedule II drugs have high potential for abuse/dependency and have multiple restrictions for prescriptions. Schedule III, IV, and V have lower risks of dependency/abuse and fewer restrictions for prescriptions. Methylphenidate has the highest risk of abuse in this selection. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will self-administer medication injections at home after discharge. How can the nurse bNestRdeteIrmGine tBha.t Cthe patient understands the technique and can administer the injections c oUr r e Sc t l y N? T O

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide written instructions about how to administer the injections.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Watch the patient self-administer an injection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Call the patient the next day to ask if there is any difficulty with administering the injections.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the patient to express understanding as to how to administer the injections.

questions.rationale: The nurse should watch the patient self-administer an injection to make sure that the patient is doing it correctly. This will give the nurse an opportunity to point out and correct any mistakes and offer the patient reassurance about the technique.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is in agonizing pain. All the following options  are listed on the patient‘s medication order sheet to relive pain. The nurse knows which option that will provide the most rapid pain relief for the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Morphine (MSContin) 10 mg PO

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hydromorphone (Dilaudid) 1 mg IV push

choices.label: C
choices.text: Meperidine (Demerol) 75 mg IM

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fentanyl (Duragesic) 50 mcg transdermal patch U

questions.rationale: IV administration has the most rapid onset of action and will provide the patient with the quickest pain relief. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse administers a medication to a patient. Shortly afterward, the patient develops an itchy rash over the entire body and reports feeling very unwell. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Leave the patient to notify the provider and the pharmacist.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Determine if the patient is having any difficulty breathing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the reaction in the patient‘s chart.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain an order for hydrocortisone cream to relieve the itching.

questions.rationale: The nurse must first determine if the patient is having any difficulty breathing, since the patient may be starting to have an anaphylactic reaction to the medication, which can lead to shortness of breath and airway swelling. After assuring that the patient is stable, the nurse can notify the appropriate personnel and request any treatments for the reaction. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which medication order to be administered PRN?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Zolpidem (Ambien) 10 mNg PRO t oIn i gGh t i fBt.h eCp a tMi e n t cannot sleep

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prednisone 10 mg PO today, then taper down 1 mg each day for the next 10 days

choices.label: C
choices.text: Humulin R 10 units subcutaneously before each meal and at bedtime

choices.label: D
choices.text: Kefzol (Ancef) 1 g IVPB 30 minutes prior to surgery

questions.rationale: The nurse is to give the zolpidine (Ambien) if the patient cannot sleep. Therefore, this is the PRN (as needed) medication order. The other orders have specific time frames. Therapies

questions.stem: After administering an antibiotic to the patient, the nurse notes the patient complaining of feeling ill, is scratching and has hives. The patient soon starts having difficulty breathing and is hypotensive. What is the nurse‘s assessment of the situation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is having a mild allergic reaction and an antihistamine will make the patient feel better.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is having an anaphylactic reaction and epinephrine should be administered right away.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s infection is worsening and progressing to septic shock so blood cultures should be drawn.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has developed toxic shock syndrome and the antibiotic orders must be changed right away.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s symptoms are indicative of anaphylaxis: a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction. The airways close, the throat swells closed, and the blood pressure drops dangerously low. The patient may go into shock and die. This is a medical emergency. Anaphylaxis can occur immediately after the administration of medication and can be fatal. Treatment includes immediate discontinuation of the drug and administration of epinephrine (an antagonist), intravenous (IV) fluids, steroids, and antihistamines while providing respiratory support. Patients may have very mild allergic reactions to medications and experience a rash or itching. This patient is not developing septic shock or toxic shock syndrome. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse makes a medication error. Which action will the nurse take first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prepare an incident report.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Explain to the patient that a medication error has occurred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the patient for any adverse reactions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the medication given, the response, and corrective actions taken.

questions.rationale: When a medication error occurs, the nurse‘s priorities are to determine the effect on the patient and intervene to offset any adverse effects of the error. Actions include immediate and ongoing assessment, notification of the prescribing health care provider, initiation of interventions as prescribed to offset any adverse effects, and documentation related to the event. Error reporting is an essential component of patient safety and should be completed as soon as the patient is a s s e s s e d NaUnRd Ss tIa bNl eG. TTBhe.nCurOse should follow facility guidelines for medication error reporting.

questions.stem: The nurse prepares to administer the following medication to the patient. Which instruction will the nurse be sure to give before the patient takes the medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Be sure to swallow the pill whole.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Crush the medication and place the powder in applesauce.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Place the pill under your tongue.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Let the pill slowly dissolve in your mouth.‖

questions.rationale: Extended release medications must always be swallowed whole without crushing or dissolving the tablet. They are not given sublingually or allowed to dissolve in the mouth. MS Contin Morphine sulfate Extended release tablets, USP 15 mg CII only NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse begins a shift on a busy medical–surgical unit and will be caring for multiple patients. Which patient does the nurse assess first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A patient who would like some acetaminophen (Tylenol) for a mild headache.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A patient who has a question about her daily medications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A patient who needs discharge teaching about an antibiotic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A patient who just received nitroglycerin for chest pain.

questions.rationale: The nurse‘s first priority is always: ABCs—Airway, Breathing, and Circulation. This includes any patients who are having chest pain and/or difficulty breathing. The nurse needs to see this patient first to determine if the chest pain has been relieved or not and to determine if the patient is now stable or if additional interventions need to be done. The other patients‘ needs are less critical and can be met after this patient is assessed.

questions.stem: The nurse carefully reviews the patient‘s medication list. Which observation about the list indicates the highest risk for serious drug–drug interactions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient has been taking the same medications for a long time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is taking a large number of medications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Most of the drugs on the NlisUt RarSe IprNesGcrTibBe.d aCt OhiMgh doses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient takes oral, injected, and inhaled medications.

questions.rationale: The risk of drug–drug interactions increases when a patient takes many drugs. One of the most important ways to prevent adverse drug interactions is to minimize the number of drugs that the patient is taking. The other options do not show a high likelihood of drug–drug interactions. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is taking many prescription medications for various health problems. Which direction from the nurse will help the patient avoid dangerous drug interactions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only take over-the-counter medications.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Have all of the prescriptions filled at the same pharmacy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Avoid taking generic preparations of prescribed medications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only take the medications that the patient feels are necessary.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s risk for dangerous drug interactions is increased when many medications are taken. Filling all the prescriptions at the same pharmacy will allow the pharmacist to check for possible interactions. Therapies

questions.stem: During discharge teaching, the nurse is to give the patient a signed, dated, and timed prescription from the physician for medications to be taken at home. Which prescription drug order needs to be corrected before it is given to the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warfarin (Coumadin) 5 mg PO daily before dinner

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methotrexate (Trexall) 8 tablets PO once weekly on Saturdays

choices.label: C
choices.text: Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 137 mcg PO daily before breakfast

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zolpidem (Ambien) 5 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep

questions.rationale: All prescriptions must have the name of the drug to be administered along with dosage, route, and frequency. The methotrexate order does not contain a dosage for the drug, just the num ber of pills to be taken. The other orders are complete. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse administers a medication to the patient. Which symptoms indicate to the nurse that the patient is having an allergic reaction rather than a side effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hair loss and sweaty skin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nausea and constipation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heartburn and nasty taste in the mouth

choices.label: D
choices.text: Itchy rash and difficulty bNrUeaRthSinIgNGTB.COM

questions.rationale: Itchy rash and difficulty breathing are indicative of an allergic reaction to a medication. The other symptoms are common side effects of medications. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse suspects that the patient is experiencing a drug toxicity rather than a side effect. Which question will the nurse ask to help confirm this suspicion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―When did you take your last dose of the medication?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Have you been taking extra doses of the medication?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Are you taking any other medications?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Have you ever taken this medication in the past?‖

questions.rationale: Asking if the patient has been taking extra doses of the medication will allow the nurse to determine if the patient has been taking too much of the drug or more than  was prescribed. Toxicity occurs when the patient receives/takes excessive amounts of the drug. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is receiving vancomycin (Vancocin) to treat a severe infection. The next dose is due to be administered at 10:00 a.m. What time will the nurse draw the vancomycin serum trough level?
questions.rationale: The trough is the lowest serum level of the medication. Serum trough levels are to be drawn just prior to the administration of the medication. Therapies

questions.stem: When administering phenytoin (Dilantin) through the patient‘s IV line, the nurse carefully flushes the IV with normal saline before and afterward to avoid crystal formation of the medication that occurs when it mixes with dextrose in water (D5W) solution. Which type of drug interaction is the nurse being careful to avoid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antagonism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Potentiation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Synergism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Incompatibility

questions.rationale: When medications combine t No foRrm Ic r y sGt a l sBo.r Ca d v Me r s e chemical reactions, the result is a drug incompatibility. Compatibility must be assessed prior to medication preparation and administration. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is noting an order for a medication to be given TID. Which times will the nurse plan to administer the medication to the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 9 a.m., 1 p.m., 5 p.m., and 10 p.m.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 9 a.m. and 9 p.m.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 9 a.m., 1 p.m., and 5 p.m.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nightly before the patient goes to sleep

questions.rationale: TID indicates that the medication is to be administered three times daily. Common times for TID medications are 9 a.m., 1 p.m., and 5 p.m. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who was just made NPO. The nurse is to administe r carvedilol (Coreg) 25 mg PO to the patient for control of high blood pressure. What is the best action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Crush the medication and administer it to the patient mixed with applesauce.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer the medication to the patient with a small sip of water.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Contact the patient‘s provider to clarify the order.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer the equivalent medication dose through the patient‘s IV.

questions.rationale: The nurse should contact the patient‘s provider to clarify the order. Oral medications should never be administered to NPO patients without specific orders to do so from the provider. Not all medications can be administered intravenously. and Parenteral Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is to administer 1 mL of prochlorperazine (Compazine) 10 mg IM to an adult patient. Which syringe will the nurse select to administer the medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1 mL tuberculin syringe with 27 gauge, 1/2 inch needle

choices.label: B
choices.text: 3 mL syringe with 23 gauge, 1 1/2 inch needle

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1 mL syringe with 27 gauge, 5/8 inch needle

choices.label: D
choices.text: 3 mL syringe with 18 gauge, 1 inch needle

questions.rationale: Intramuscular injections for adults are usually administered with a 3 mL syringe and a 1 to 3 inch, 19 to 25 gauge needle. Tuberculin syringes are typically used for subcutaneous injections. The inch needles aNre tRo o Is horGt f o Br i.n tCraUmMuSs cular injections into adults. The 18 and 27 gauge needles are too small for adult intramuscular injections. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is to administer 15 mg of morphine liquid to the patient. How much morphine liquid will the nurse draw up to administer to the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 0.5 mL

choices.label: C
choices.text: 1.3 mL

choices.label: D
choices.text: 1.5 mL

questions.rationale: Morphine sulfate oral solution (CONCENTRATE) 100 mg/5 mL (20 mg/mL) CII only Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with multiple chronic illnesses who is having  difficulty remembering to take multiple medications at the correct times. Which is the appropriat e Nursing diagnosis for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activity intolerance related to inability to take medications on time

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired health maintenance related to complexity of medication schedule

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk for aspiration related to need to swallow many pills during day

choices.label: D
choices.text: Powerlessness related to inability to figure out medication dose times

questions.rationale: The patient is not able to manage the prescribed medication regimen because of the complexity of the schedule, so Impaired health maintenance is an appropriate diagnosis. Activity intolerance does not relate to the ability to take multiple medications at once and manage medication times. The patient does not state any difficulty swallowing pills, so risk for aspiration is not applicable. Inability to figure out medication dose times does not constitute powerlessness.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who takes 6 tablets of methotrexate once ev ery week on Fridays. How many mg of methotrexate does the patient take per dose? Trexall Methotrexate tablets, USP 2.5 mg tablets only

choices.label: A
choices.text: 10 mg

choices.label: B
choices.text: 15 mg

choices.label: C
choices.text: 20 mg

choices.label: D
choices.text: 25 mg

questions.rationale: 2.5 mg tablets 6 = 15 mg. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is to administer 45 mg of phenobarbital to the patient. How many tablets will the patient receive? Phenobarbital tablets, USP 15 mg CIV only

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1 tablet

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2 tablets

choices.label: C
choices.text: 3 tablets

choices.label: D
choices.text: 4 tablets

questions.rationale: Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is NPO with a new PEG (percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy) tube. Which of the patient‘s medications can the nurse administer through the tube? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Zolpidem tartrate (Edluar) sublingual tablet 5 mg nightly at bedtime

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ondansetron (Zofran) oral disintegrating tablet 8 mg q 8 hours PRN nausea

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cefaclor (Ceclor) for oral suspension 250 mg q 6 hours

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oxymorphone hydrochloride extended release (Opana ER) 40 mg q 12 hours

choices.label: E
choices.text: Phenytoin (Dilantin) chewable tablet 100 mg q 12 hours

choices.label: F
choices.text: Potassium chloride oral solution 20 mEq daily

questions.rationale: Extended-release, oral disintegrating, and sublingual tablets may not be administered through feeding tubes. Suspensions and oral solutions are ideal for feeding tube administration. Chewable tablets may be crushed and dissolved in liquid for administration through feeding tubes. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which medications that are to be administered via parenteral routes? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bisacodyl (Dulcolax) 10 mg suppository daily PRN constipation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prochlroperazine (Compazine) 10 mg IM q 6 hours PRN nausea

choices.label: C
choices.text: Brimonidine (Alphagan) 0.1% solution 2 drops to each eye daily

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proventil (Ventolin) inhaler 2 puffs as needed for shortness of breath

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fentanyl (Duragesic) 50 mcg transdermal patch apply every 72 hours

choices.label: F
choices.text: Insulin lispro (Humalog) insulin 15 units subcutaneously ac meals

questions.rationale: Parenteral medications are administered by injection into tissue, muscle, or a vein rather than through the gastrointestinal or respiratory route. Therapies 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient recovering from knee replacement surgery. The patient complains of severe pain in the knee after receiving hydrocodone with acetaminophen (Vicodin) 2 hours previously. What is the nurse‘s best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer another dose of the medication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply ice packs to the knee.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply heat packs to the knee.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform gentle range of motion.

questions.rationale: Application of cold decreases swelling and pain, produces local analgesia, and slows nerve conduction, which improves functioning. Examples of cold therapy are ice bags and cold compresses. The nurse should not administer another dose of medication without an order from the provider. Heat will increase blood flow to the area rather than reduce swelling. Gentle ROM will increase pain if done at this time.

questions.stem: The nurse is checking on the patient after administering pain medication 30 minutes previously. Which assessmen Nt UfinRdSinIgNbeGsTt iBn d.i c a Ot e s to the nurse that the pain medication was effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is sleeping quietly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient states a reduction of the pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s respirations are slow and regular.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s blood pressure has returned to baseline.

questions.rationale: The best way for the nurse to determine that the pain medication was effecti ve is for the patient to state a reduction of the pain. The other assessment findings cannot definiti vely determine whether the patient is still in pain. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has severe abdominal pain caused by acute cholecystitis. The nurse recognizes which type of pain is this patient experiencing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Visceral pain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Somatic pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Radiating pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Referred pain

questions.rationale: Visceral pain arises from the organs of the body and occurs when inflammation and tissue damage occur, such as with cholecystitis. Somatic pain occurs when there is tissue damage to skin, muscle, joints, and bones. Referred pain occurs when the discomfort is felt at a location other than the origin of the pain. Radiating pain extends to another area of the body. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse knows which is the best pain medication option for a patient to manage severe long-term cancer pain at home?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fentanyl (Duragesic) 50 mcg transdermal patch q 72 hours

choices.label: B
choices.text: Meperidine (Demerol) 50 mg IM q 6 hours

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hydromorphone (Dilaudid) 0.2 mg q 10 minutes IV via PCA pump

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hydromorphone (Dilaudid) 0.08 mg/hour infusion through epidural catheter

questions.rationale: An opioid transdermal patch is the best pain management option for home use with patients who have long-term, severe cancer pain as no injections are required and the opioid is slowly released over 72 hours. Epidurals and PCA pumps are intended for hospital use. Frequent IM injections require nursing administration, are not comfortable for the patient and are not optimal for chronic long-term pain. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patiNent wRiUt hIsSe vGeNr eBc.h rCon i cMp ain and applied the first 50 mcgtransdermal fetanyl (Duragesic) patch 2 hours ago. The patient states that the pain is presently rated at 9 on a 1 to 10 scale. What is the nurse‘s best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient that the fentanyl patch will start to work soon.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the provider‘s orders for a short-acting narcotic medication to administer for breakthrough pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give the patient a gentle back rub and encourage guided imagery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply a second 25-mcg transdermal fentanyl patch now.

questions.rationale: Transdermal administration of medication does not become effective for 12 to 16 hours after application. Short-acting narcotic medication should be given in the meantime to make the patient comfortable. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has been taking ibuprofen (Motrin) 800 mg TID for the last several months to relieve knee pain from arthritis. Which assessment finding must be reported by the nurse to the provider promptly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient has abdominal pain and pale skin.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient has constipation and takes stool softeners daily.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient enjoys a glass of wine every Friday and Saturday evening.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has gained 15 lb in the last 3 months.

questions.rationale: A side effect of ibuprofen and other NSAIDs is the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding, especially with long-term use. Abdominal pain with pale skin in this patient may be indicative of a bleeding ulcer and should be reported to the provider promptly. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who just underwent laparoscopic appendectomy. The patient complains of severe postoperative pain between the shoulder blades. Which term best describes the pain that this patient is having?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Referred pain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phantom pain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neuropathic pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Psychogenic pain

questions.rationale: Referred pain is pain that occurs when discomfort is felt in a different  area than the source of the pain. Phantom pain occurs in amputees when pain is felt in the missing limb. Neuropathic pain occurs in the nervous system and often feels like burning or tingling. Psychogenic pain is discomfort felt by the patient that has no physical cause. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse administered 100 mcg sublingual fentanyl spray (Subsys) at 10:00 a.m. to a patient experiencing severe breakthrough pain. At what time will the nurse ask the patient if pain relief was obtained?

choices.label: D
choices.text: 12:00 noon

questions.rationale: Sublingual pain medications should be working well 15 to 30 minutes after administration, so the nurse should reassess the patient‘s pain at 10:30 a.m. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will be using a hydromorphone (Dilaudid) PCA analgesia pump following surgery. Which intervention is the highest priority for the nurse to include in the patient‘s care plan related to this pump?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s respiratory status frequently after PCA pump started.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Review patient‘s medication profile to check for interactions with hydromorphone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach the patient how to use PCA pump when the pain level is still tolerable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Keep naloxone (Narcan) available at the bedside in case of respiratory depression.

questions.rationale: For patient safety, the nurse would check the patient‘s respirations frequently after the pump has been initiated due to possible respiratory depression. Reviewing the medication profile would occur prior to initiating the pump. Teaching the patient how to use the pump is important, but not the priority. Naloxone should be close by to treat respiratory depression but monitoring the respirations frequently would hopefully prevent depression. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a 6-month-old infant who has just undergone surgery. T he in fant‘s facial muscles are tight with a furrowed brow and the infant‘s respirations are shallow and irregular. The infant is mildly fussy and softly crying without muscular rigidity in the arms and legs. What score will the nurse give to the infant on the Neonatal Infant Pain Scale?
questions.rationale: Tight muscles and furrowed brow = 1 point. Softly crying = 1 point. Shallow, irregular respirations = 1 point. Relaxed arms and legs = 0 points. Mild fussiness = 1 point. Total = 4 points.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is recovering from thoracotomy surgery and notes t hat the patient‘s pain is rated 9/10 and is unable to focus on anything. Which intervention by the nurse is the highest priority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer prescribed IV pain medication and evaluate impact in 30 minutes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the patient to describe prior pain experiences and methods used to manage pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Explain that comfort is a priority goal of nursing care in the postoperative period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assist the patient to minimize the effects of pain on interpersonal relationships with family members.

questions.rationale: The highest priority intervention for a patient in acute pain is to provide pain relief. The other interventions do not address acute pain relief.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with rheumatoid arthritis who is in constant severe pain. Which nursing diagnosis is the highest priority for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired mobility r/t patient‘s need to use a cane or walker with ambulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired health maintenance r/t sedentary lifestyle and poor physical condition U S

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anxiety r/t mistrust of health care personnel

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic pain r/t ongoing inflammatory tissue damage and joint destruction

questions.rationale: Chronic pain is the highest priority diagnosis for this patient because it is severe. The other diagnoses may be addressed once the patient‘s pain is controlled. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a cancer patient with ongoing pain from widespread metastasis t o the bones. The nurse notes that the patient‘s morphine dosage had to be increased to sufficiently manage the discomfort. What is the nurse‘s interpretation of this assessment finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient became tolerant to the previous morphine dosage.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is becoming addicted to the pain medication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient has been abusing the prescribed pain medications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient may be seeking to end life with an overdose of morphine.

questions.rationale: Drug tolerance is an adaptation to the medication, which eventually leads to less effective pain relief. The patient is requiring higher doses of narcotic pain medication because of this tolerance. This is common when patients require long-term pain medication. Since the patient is taking morphine to control ongoing pain, the patient is not addicted to it. Need for increased morphine dosage is not indicative of drug abuse or a wish to die.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which patient to be best suited for PCA analgesia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A patient who is confused after a head injury

choices.label: B
choices.text: A patient recovering from total hysterectomy surgery

choices.label: C
choices.text: A patient who has severe psychogenic pain

choices.label: D
choices.text: A patient with arthritis who is unable to push the nurse call button

questions.rationale: Patients recuperating from surgery are often good candidates for PCA analgesia. Confusion, inability to push the PCA button, and psychogenic pain are all contraindications for PCA analgesia. Therapies

questions.stem: What is the priority nursing assessment for a patient who is receiving postoperative epidural analgesia with hydromorphone (Dilaudid)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Respiratory rate, depth, and pattern

choices.label: B
choices.text: Skin underneath the epidural dressing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bladder scanning to check for urinary retention

choices.label: D
choices.text: Itching on the trunk and/or extremities

questions.rationale: The respiratory system is the priority nursing assessment for patients receiving narcotic pain medication via any route. This is because narcotics can cause respiratory suppression. The other assessments are a lower priority and may be done after a respiratory assessment is completed. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a diabetic patient who has painful foot neuropathy. The patient asks why the nurse is administering gabapentin (Neurontin) when there is no history of seizure disorder. What is the nurse‘s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Gabapentin will help you sleep at night so you can deal with the pain more effectively.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Long-term diabetes can put patients at risk for certain type of seizures.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―This medication can help relieve your anxiety from being admitted to the hospital.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Gabapentin works on the nervous system to help relieve the burning pain in your feet.‖

questions.rationale: Anticonvulsant medication like gabapentin and tricyclic antidepressants are often used to relieve neuropathic pain as they work directly on the nervous system. The other statements do not correctly indicate why the patient is receiving this medication.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a PCA pump following total hyster ectomy surgery. The nurse sees the visitor push the PCA button while the patient is sleeping quietly. What  is the best response of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Thank you for pushing the button for her to help keep her comfortable after surgery.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Please do not push the button for the patient—she could receive more medication than she needs.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―You can push the button for her now, but please have her do it herself when she awakens.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―PCA pumps are great because she doesn‘t have to wait for me to administer her pain  medication.‖

questions.rationale: Only the patient should operate the PCA and push the administration button. Family members and visitors should never activate the PCA pump for the patient because too much medication could be delivered, resulting in overdose and respiratory suppression. Therapies

questions.stem: Which assessment question helps the nurse determine the character of the patient‘s pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―What does the pain feel like?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―When did the pain first start?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―What interventions make the pain better?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Is there any pattern to when the pain occurs?‖

questions.rationale: Pain character should be assessed using questions to learn more about what the pain  feels like. Examples like stabbing, aching, burning may be used so that patients can under stand what the nurse is requesting. Onset is determined by asking when the pain started. Exacerbating/relieving factors are determined by asking which interventions make the pa in better. Time course is determined by asking if there is a pattern to when the pain occurs. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who only speaks a foreign language. What is the best method for the nurse to assess the patient‘s pain level?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Perform a pain assessment using a translator.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check the patient‘s vital signs and pulse oximetry.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient‘s respiratory rate, depth, and rhythm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Look to see if the patient appears to be resting comfortably.

questions.rationale: The best method to determine pain in a patient who speaks only a foreign language is to use an interpreter. The Universal Pain Assessment Tool is available with foreign-language phrases that may also be used. MeasuNrin gRvitIal sGi gUn sBa.n dCs eMe i n g if the patient is resting comfortably are not accurate pain assessment techniques. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a trauma patient with the Nursing diagnosis of acute pain r/t fracture and muscle spasms. Which is an appropriate goal for this Nursing diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will experience less pain when participating in physical therapy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will describe meditation techniques that can be used to cope with pain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nursing staff will explain the ordered pain management approach to the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will feel less pain each day when range-of-motion therapy is performed.

questions.rationale: Goals must be measurable and objective so that nursing staff can determine  when each of the goals has been met. Having the patient describe meditation techniques is measurable because  the nursing staff can determine whether he can actually describe them. Goals are achieved by the patient, not nursing staff. The nursing staff cannot accurately measure whether the pat ient is experiencing or feeling less pain. The goal statements ―The patient will report less pain … or state that he has less pain …‖ are not measurable and appropriate. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has pain following abdominal surgery. Which actions are independent nursing interventions that can be used to make the patient more comfortable? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encourage the patient to relax and imagine resting on a tropical beach.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide headphones so that the patient can listen to favorite music.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase pain medication dosage if prescribed regimen is ineffective to manage pain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Teach the patient to take pain medication before discomfort becomes severe.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Switch the patient from IV to oral pain medication when bowel sounds return.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Demonstrate the use of relaxation breathing before painful procedures.

questions.rationale: Independent nursing interventions may be carried out without an order from the provider. Changing medication orders must be done by the provider; increasing pain medication dosage and switching the patient to PO pain medications are not independent nursing interventions.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has severe burning pain in the right arm caused by a compressed nerve in the neck. Which medications can be used along with a narcotic pain reliever to relieve the patientN‘s p aRi n Iu ntiGl surBg.erCy c aMn be performed to release the nerve? (Select all that apply.) U S

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg PO daily

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amitriptyline (Elavil) 50 mg PO BID

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ondansetron (Zofran) 8 mg PO q 4 hours PRN

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gabapentin (Neurontin) 400 mg PO BID

choices.label: E
choices.text: Senna (Senokot) 8.6 mg PO daily

choices.label: F
choices.text: Naloxone (Narcan) 0.4 mg IV now, may repeat in 1 hour PRN

questions.rationale: Tricyclic antidepressants like amitriptyline and anticonvulsants like gabapentin are often used to treat neuropathic pain because they work directly on the nervous system. They may be given along with narcotic pain medication to make the patient comfortable. Senna will relieve constipation and diphenhydramine will relieve itching. Ondansetron is used to relieve nausea and vomiting, whereas naloxone will reverse narcotic-induced respiratory suppression. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who just had knee replacement surgery. Which factors will affect how the patient experiences pain after this surgery? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient has had rheumatoid arthritis for the last 16 years.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is allergic to aspirin and strawberries.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient owns a business and is self-insured.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has been a vegetarian for the last 8 years.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient had the other knee replaced 2 years ago.

choices.label: F
choices.text: The patient was a marathon runner in high school and college.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s history of rheumatoid arthritis, previous knee replacement surgery, and marathon running indicate that the patient has had significant experience dealing with pain, which will affect how he or she experiences pain after this surgery. The other factors will not affect how the patient experiences pain. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is about to have surgery. Which intervention will be included in the patient‘s care to meet the goals for risk for perioperative positioning injury related to immobilization during surgical procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use adequate assistance to move patient onto the OR table.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Watch for early signs of hypovolemia caused by patient‘s NPO status since midnight.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use therapeutic touch and guided imagery to allay patient‘s fears of surgery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pad all bony prominences and avoid hyperextension of extremities.

questions.rationale: Risk for perioperative positioning injury is addressed by ensuring that the patient‘s skin and bony prominences are well padded during the surgery. In addition, hyperextension of extremities may lead to joint damage, so this should be avoided as well. The other interventions are appropriate for perioperative care but do not relate directly to the Nursing diagnosis of positioning injury potential. Procedures

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a m a l Ne pUaRtieSnIt wNhGoTisBh.aCviOngMopen heart surgery. The patient‘s chest is covered with thick hair, so the surgical technician begins to shave the patient‘s skin near the operative site. Which action by the technician requires intervention by the nurse to correct the technique?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A straight safety razor and antibiotic foam is used.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Disposable electric trimmers are used to trim the hair.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibacterial soap is used prior to hair removal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the hair directly around the surgical site is removed.

questions.rationale: Disposable electric trimmers should be used to remove excess hair from operative sites. Antibacterial soap is commonly used to clean the skin before surgical procedures. Only the hair around the surgical site is removed. A straight safety razor would never be used because small nicks in the skin can occur, increasing infection risk.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has just been brought to the post operative unit following major surgery and notes that the patient has many tubes and monitors in place. Whi ch will the nurse assess first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient‘s intravenous lines

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s urinary catheter U S

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s nasogastric tube

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s endotracheal tube

questions.rationale: Airway maintenance and protection is the highest priority for this  patient, so the nurse should assess the endotracheal tube first to ensure that it is patent and positioned correctl y. The other tubes may be assessed afterward.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a family history of reactions to general anesthesia. Which medication will the nurse anesthetist have ready as a precautionary measure before the patient‘s surgery is started?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protamine sulfate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dantrolene sodium (Dantrium)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activated charcoal with sorbitol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Folinic acid (Leucovorin)

questions.rationale: Malignant hyperthermia is a dangerous anesthesia reaction caused by a genetic defect that may be passed down via family history. Knowing this, the anesthesiologist would have dantrolene ready as a precaution because it is a mainstay of treatment for malignant hyperthermia. The other medications are not related. Procedures

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best demonstrates accountability in the operating room?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Applying warm blankets when the patient reports feeling chilly

choices.label: B
choices.text: Holding the patient‘s hand to allay anxiety before anesthesia is administered

choices.label: C
choices.text: Double-checking that the surgical site is clearly marked and visible after draping

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using calming speech with a reassuring tone of voice when speaking with the patient

questions.rationale: Accountability is accomplished by ensuring that proper precautions are taken to prevent errors from happening. The nurse can prevent wrong-site surgery by making sure that the surgical site is clearly marked and visible after the draping is completed. The surgeon could inadvertently operate on the incorrect site if the markings are covered by the surgical drapes. The other actions of the nurse are appropriate but do not demonstrate accountability and error prevention.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will be having surgery. The patient has just signed the consent form for the operation. What does the patient‘s signature indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient agrees with the doctor‘s diagnosis. U

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient gives permission for the surgery to be performed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient has agreed to pay for any costs not covered by insurance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has been told of all the available treatment options.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s signature on the consent form indicates that the patient gives permission for the surgery to be performed. It does not indicate that the patient agrees with the physician‘s diagnosis, agrees to pay for costs not covered by insurance, or has been informed of all the possible treatment options.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient who is very sleepy following general anesthesia and administration of pain medication. The nurse notes that the patient is making snoring sounds and his pulse oximetry has dropped to 88%. What is the best action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess the airway and administer oxygen.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Call for anesthesia to immediately reintubate the patient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remove the pillow from behind the patient‘s head.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevate the head of the patient‘s bed.

questions.rationale: The snoring sounds made by the patient and low oximetry levels indicate that the patient‘s airway is partially occluded because of anesthesia and pain medication. Patency of the airway should be assessed and oxygen administered to maintain the airway and oxygenation. Reintubation should be perfo Nrm eRd i fIt heGo raBl a.i rCw a yMa lone does not maintain the airway andimprove oxygenation. Removing the pillow from the head of the patient‘s bed or elevating the head of the patient‘s bed will not be sufficient to open the patient‘s airway. Signs

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient on his first day after surgery. The nurse informs the patient that the plan is to sit in the chair and ambulate in the hallway. The patient states that he is in pain and has no intention of getting out of bed. What is the nurse‘s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―It‘s important to move around so you don‘t get a blood clot in your leg.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Your doctor ordered that you are to get out of bed at least twice every day.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I understand. You can rest in bed until tomorrow when the pain is better.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I will call the doctor and let him know that you do not want to get up.‖

questions.rationale: The nurse should teach the patient why it is important to ambulate after surgery. Early ambulation helps to prevent many complications postoperatively, including constipation, deep vein thrombosis, atelectasis, pneumonia, and urinary stasis. Many patients who experience abdominal distention and gas pain obtain some relief from ambulating. Simply telling the patient that the physician ordered ambulation is not sufficient. Allowing the patient to stay in bed will increase the risk of DVT. Procedures

questions.stem: The nurse is walking a postoperative patient in the hallway when she notices a large red stain of fresh blood on the patient‘s gown over the abdominal incision. The patient states, ―I felt something just ripped open.‖ What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lift up the patient‘s gown and assess the incision.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assist the patient to the floor and call for assistance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Return the patient to bed and irrigate the wound with sterile saline.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check the patient‘s vital signs and pulse oximetry.

questions.rationale: The large red blood stain over the incision and feeling of ripping open most likely indicates that the patient‘s wound has dehisced or eviscerated. The nurse should immediately lower the patient to the floor to reduce tension on the wound. Patient modesty and privacy should be maintained, so the incision should be assessed once the patient is transported back to  his room. Checking the patient‘s vital signs and pulse oximetry can be performed once the patient has been lowered to the floor.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with advanced colon cancer. The patient is to have surgery to relieve a bowel obstruction that has been causing unrelenting vomiting and abdominal pain. What type of surgery will this patient undergo?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Palliative

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reconstructive

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diagnostic

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ablative

questions.rationale: Palliative surgery is performed to alleviate distressing symptoms such as unrelenting pain, nausea, and vomiting. Palliative surgery will not attempt to cure the underlying disease process but will make the patient more comfortable. Reconstructive surgery restores function or appearance of traumatized tissue. A diagnostic surgical procedure establishes or confirms a diagnosis. Ablative surgery removes diseased tissue.

questions.stem: After general anesthesia is administered, the patient is carefully placed in the prone position. What is the primary consideration of the nursing staff as the patient is positioned?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Making sure that the patient‘s endotracheal tube does not become kinked

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensuring that the patient‘s head is positioned to prevent cervical nerve injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carefully taping the patient‘s eyes shut to avoid corneal abrasions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Padding the operating table carefully and keeping linens free of wrinkles

questions.rationale: Ensuring that the patient‘s respiratory status is not compromised is the priority of all staff during repositioning after general anesthesia has been administered. Positioning and protection of eyes is important but less than protection of the endotracheal tube patency. Procedures

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a preoperative patient who has just received sedation prior to general anesthesia in the OR. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check to make sure that the consent form was signed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turn off the lights and provide a quiet environment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Raise the side rails on the patient‘s stretcher.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Indicate the surgical site with an indelible marker.

questions.rationale: Safety of the preoperative patient is a priority after sedation has been administered. Raising the side rails of the stretcher will help prevent the patient from falling. Turning off the lights is nice but is not a priority. Marking the surgical site and signing the consent form must be performed prior to administration of sedation.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient who is recovering from abdominal surgery. The nurse notes that the patient‘s breath sounds are clear but diminished, shallow, and slightly labored. The patient‘s pulse oNximRetrIy i s G9 6U%BSo.nCr o oMm air. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer a dose of the prescribed pain medication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer 2 L of oxygen via nasal cannula.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Obtain an order from the physician for a chest x-ray.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that the patient is using the spirometer 10 times every hour.

questions.rationale: The assessment findings indicate that the patient most likely has atelectasis, so the nurse should ensure that the patient is using the incentive spirometer 10 times every hour to facilitate expansion and reinflation of alveoli. Administering prescribed pain medication is not the priority and may further suppress the patient‘s respiratory drive. The patient‘s pulse oximetry is 96¯ on room air so supplemental oxygen is not needed. A chest x-ray may be ordered if the patient‘s condition worsens or does not improve with regular spirometer use.

questions.stem: The nurse will be caring for a patient who has just arrived on the medical–surgical unit following surgical repair of his fractured right ankle. Which is the priority action of the nurse when the patient arrives on the unit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient how to call for assistance using the call light.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the color and warmth of the toes on the patient‘s right foot.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determine when the patient‘s next pain medication is due.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check pulse oximetry and obtain a full set of vital signs.

questions.rationale: Checking pulse oximetry and vital signs is the priority action when the patient first arrives on the medical–surgical unit from the postoperative area. The other actions can wait until the vital signs have been obtained.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is recovering from chest surgery. Which action by the patient indicates that additional teaching is needed about how to use the ordered incentive spirometer correctly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient breathes into the spirometer so that the marker rises slowly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient uses the spirometer 5 to 12 times every 1 to 2 hours while awake.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient seals his lips tightly around the spirometer mouthpiece.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient should hold each inhaled breath 3 to 5 seconds.

questions.rationale: The patient must take in a deep breath while holding the spirometer to the mouth s o that the device can indicate how much air is being inhaled into the lungs. The remaining responses are correct components of the procedure.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is headed to the operating room for abdominal surgery. Which goal is appropriate for the Nursing diagnosis risk for perioperative positioning injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient will deny numbness or tingling in extremities after surgical procedure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient will maintain urine output of at least 30 mL/hr during and after surgery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient will maintain elastic skin turgor as well as moist tongue and mucus membranes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient will have no emesis and deny nausea following arousal from general anesthesia.

questions.rationale: Numbness and tingling of extremities may be indicative of injury following imprope r positioning for surgery. The other goals do not relate to positioning injury but relate t o Nursing diagnoses of fluid balance and nausea.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who requires emergency surgery for injuries sustained in a motor vehicle accident. The patient was on his way back to work after having lunch with colleagues when the accident happened. What is the highest priority Nursing diagnosis for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Risk for imbalanced body temperature

choices.label: B
choices.text: Risk for aspiration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk for perioperative positioning injury

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk for delayed surgical recovery

questions.rationale: Risk for aspiration is the highest priority because the patient has not been NPO and his stomach is filled with food after lunching with his colleagues. The patient may easily aspirate stomach contents into the airway when general anesthesia is administered, so precautions must be taken to prevent this from happening. The other Nursing diagnoses certainly apply but are not as important as risk for aspiration.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient who has a history of COPD. What is the priority Nursing diagnosis for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ineffective airway clearance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Readiness for enhanced knowledge

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk for delayed surgical recovery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Activity intolerance

questions.rationale: Ineffective airway clearance is the priority diagnosis for the COPD patient undergoing surgery because the patient is at high risk for bronchoconstriction, increased mucus, and ineffective cough, which may easily become worse after the patient has received general anesthesia. The other Nursing diagnoses are applicable to the patient but are not as high priority.

questions.stem: The nurse is assigned to care for several patients on the surgical unit. Which patient need will the nurse address first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A patient who is waiting for discharge teaching before going home.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A patient who needs to be ambulated for the first time postoperatively.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A patient who has not voided since the catheter was removed 8 hours ago.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A patient who requires a daily dressing change to the surgical incision.

questions.rationale: Urinary retention is common after removal of urinary catheters and must be addressed promptly. The nurse should address the patient with urinary retention first because it is the highest priority. The other patients may wait until later.

questions.stem: The nurse is obtaining preoperative information for a patient who will be having  emergency surgery shortly for a ruptured appendix. Which information is crucial for the nurse to assess? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: All medications that the patient is taking

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use of tobacco, alcohol, or recreational drugs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allergies to medications, foods, or other substances

choices.label: D
choices.text: Date of last tetanus shot and flu vaccination

choices.label: E
choices.text: Insurance coverage and preauthorization requirements

choices.label: F
choices.text: Possibility of pregnancy

questions.rationale: Priority assessment must be completed prior to emergency surgery, including use of medications, alcohol, tobacco, or recreational drugs because these may interact with anesthesia medications. Allergies must be identified to prevent reactions in the operating room. Special precautions may be taken if the patient is pregnant, so this must also be determined preoperatively . Asking the patient about vaccinations or insurance coverage is not a priority prior to surgery. Procedures

questions.stem: The nurse is working with a nursing assistant to care for several postoperative patients. Which interventions can the nurse delegate to the assistant for completion? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assess patients‘ comfort levels and need for pain medication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Empty urinary catheter bags and record urine output.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach patients how to use incentive spirometers hourly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide ice chips and juicNeUtoRpSatIieNnGtsTwBho.aCrOe nMo longer NPO.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Monitor incisions for signs of infection.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Apply TED hose and assist with oral care.

questions.rationale: Basic patient care tasks that do not require assessment or critical thinking may be assigned to the nursing assistant for completion. These include emptying drainage bags, providing ice chips to patients who are allowed oral intake, and applying TED hose. Teaching, monitoring, and assessing patients are done by the nurse.

questions.stem: Which patients would benefit from preoperative teaching about splinting of incisions to minimize discomfort? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patient having coronary bypass graft surgery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patient having open breast biopsy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient having total hip replacement surgery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient having lumbar spine decompression surgery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patient having surgery to repair retinal detachment

choices.label: F
choices.text: Patient having total abdominal hysterectomy

questions.rationale: Postoperative splinting is done by supporting the abdominal and chest muscles to minimize the pain of coughing and deep breathing after surgery. Patients who have just had heart or abdominal surgery will benefit from splinting. The other surgical procedures do not affect the chest or abdomen, and these patients would not benefit from teaching about splinting. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Basic Care and Comfort: Non-Pharmacological Comfort Interventions

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is recovering from bowel resection surgery. Which assessment findings indicate to the nurse that the patient no longer needs to remain NPO and may progress to oral intake of food and fluids? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient passed flatus while ambulating this morning.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s abdomen is soft with active bowel sounds 4 quadrants.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient denies nausea or vomiting and states that he feels hungry.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s abdominal incision is clean, dry, and intact with staples.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient ambulated in the hallway with a slow, steady gait.

choices.label: F
choices.text: The patient‘s urinary catheter is patent with clear, yellow urine.

questions.rationale: The patient may indicate readiness for oral intake when passing flatus and relating feelings of hunger. The absence of nausea and vomiting along with active bowel sounds in a soft abdomen also indicate that the patient‘s GI tract is ready for oral feedings.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who underwent abdominal surgery the previous day. Which assessment findings indicate to the nurse that the patient may be experiencing serious internal bleeding? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient‘s urinary output increased to 40 mL/hr.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s pulse has risen from 76 to 112 beats/min.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient states that his abdominal pain is worse than yesterday.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient complains of generalized itching.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient‘s hematocrit dropped from 14.6 to 11.0 g/dL.

choices.label: F
choices.text: The patient has not been able to have a bowel movement since before surgery.

questions.rationale: Signs of internal bleeding include tachycardia, increased abdominal pain and a drop in hematocrit/hemoglobin. Urinary output would decrease with internal bleeding because the kidneys work to conserve fluids. Itching and constipation are not signs of internal bleeding. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse finds the patient in cardiopulmonary arrest with no pulse or respirations. Which oxygen delivery device will the nurse use for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Non-rebreather mask

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bag-valve-mask unit

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP)

choices.label: D
choices.text: High-flow nasal cannula

questions.rationale: The priority of the nurse is to ventilate the patient manually using a bag-valve-mask unit (also called by the proprietary name Ambu bag). This allows air to be forced into the patient‘s lungs when there are no spontaneous respirations. The non-rebreather mask and nasal cannula require the patient to breathe on his or her own. CPAP is used for patients who are awake, oriented, and in respiratory failure.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is slow to awaken following general anesthesia. The patient is breathing spontaneously but is minimally responsive and having difficulty maintaining a patent airway. NWUhRicShIinNteGrvTeBnt.ioCn iOs the most appropriate for the patient to improve oxygenation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insert an oral airway.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lower the head of the bed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turn the patient‘s head to the side.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monitor the patient‘s pulse oximetry.

questions.rationale: An oral airway will prevent the patient‘s tongue from falling back and occluding the airway. Lowering the head of the bed will only increase airway occlusion and risk of aspiration. Turning the patient‘s head to the side will not clear the back of the patient‘s tongue from the airway. Monitoring the patient‘s pulse oximetry will not improve oxygenation or clear the airway.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a history of left-sided congestive heart failure who is acutely short of breath. The nurse hears fine crackles throughout both lung fields and notes that the patient‘s pulse oximetry is only 88¯ on 4 L of oxygen. What is the priority intervention of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer the ordered intravenous diuretic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prepare for insertion of a chest tube.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Suction secretions from the patient‘s respiratory tract.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Have the patient use the ordered incentive spirometer.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s respiratory distress is due to pulmonary edema and fluid overload from left-sided congestive heart failure. A diuretic will pull the excess fluid out of the body through the urine and relieve the patient‘s distress. A chest tube is not needed as the fluid is within the alveoli rather than between the lung and chest wall. Suctioning and use of an incentive spirometer will not address fluid overload or improve the patient‘s symptoms.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has been intubated with an oral endotracheal tube for several weeks. The physicians predict that the patient will need to remain on a ventilator for at least several more weeks before he will be able to maintain his airway and breathe on his own. What procedure does the nurse anticipate will be planned for the patient to facilitate recovery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Placement of a tracheostomy tube

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diagnostic thoracentesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulmonary angiogram

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lung transplantation surgery

questions.rationale: Placement of a tracheostomy tube will secure the patient‘s airway directly through the trachea, eliminating the need for the endotracheal tube. This will make the patient more comfortable and may allow eating while minimizing damage to the oropharynx from the endotracheal tube.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a chest tube who was transported to radiology for testing. When the patient returns to the nursing unit, the transporter shows the nurse the patient‘s chest tube collection device, which was badly damaged after being caught in the elevator door. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clamp the chest tube until the collection device is replaced.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cover the insertion site with a new occlusive dressing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure that there is gentle bubbling in the water seal chamber.

choices.label: D
