choices.text: Check the patient‘s lung sounds and pulse oximetry.

questions.rationale: The broken collection device may no longer be used to collect chest tube drainage. Clamping the chest tube until the collection device is replaced will prevent air from entering the lung space until the new collection device is attached.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is hospitalized for pneumonia. Which Nursing diagnosis has the highest priority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activity intolerance r/t generalized weakness and hypoxemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired nutritional intake r/t poor appetite and increased metabolic needs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Impaired airway clearance r/t thick secretions in trachea and bronchi

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lack of knowledge r/t use of nebulizer and inhaled bronchodilators

questions.rationale: Airway maintenance and patency is the highest priority for all patients, especially patients with respiratory disorders. Oxygenation is the most important human need. The other diagnoses can apply once the patient‘s airway is kept patent.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who developed a pulmonary embolism after sur gery. Which goal statement is the highest priority for the nurse to include in the patient‘s care plan for the diagnosis impaired gas exchange r/t impaired pulmonary blood flow from embolus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will maintain pulse oximetry values of at least 95% on room air.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will verbalize understanding of ordered anticoagulants.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will report chest pain of no greater than 3 on a 1 to 10 scale.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will ambulate 50 feet in hallway without shortness of breath.

questions.rationale: Oxygenation is the most important human need, so adequate oxygenation of tissues as evidenced by pulse oximetry values of at least 95% on room air is the highest priority goal. The other goals may be a d d r eNs Us eRd SonIcNe tGhTe oBx.ygCeOnaMtion goal has been met.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with severe COPD who is becoming increasingly confused and disoriented. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain an arterial blood gas to check for carbon dioxide retention.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase the patient‘s oxygen until the pulse oximetry is greater than 98%.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lower the head of the patient‘s bed and insert a nasal airway.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer a mild sedative and reorient the patient as needed.

questions.rationale: Confusion and disorientation in a patient with severe COPD may likely be due to carbon dioxide retention. An arterial blood gas should be drawn to determine if this is the case. COPD patients should be kept on low oxygen flow rates whenever possible to avoid impeding the drive to breathe. Lowering the head of the bed will increase the difficulty of breathing as the abdominal contents press on the diaphragm. A sedative will cause respiratory depression and should be avoided.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has been prescribed warfarin (Coumadin) therapy after being diagnosed with atrial fibrillation. The patient asks the nurse what could happen if the prescription doesn‘t get filled. What is the nurse‘s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―You could have a stroke.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Your kidneys could fail. ‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―You could develop heart failure.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―You could go into respiratory failure.‖

questions.rationale: A major complication of chronic atrial fibrillation is formation of blood clots within the atria due to sluggish blood flow. Anticoagulation therapy is common to prevent blood clot formation that could travel to the brain, causing a stroke.

questions.stem: The preceptor is working with a new nurse to provide care for a patient with a chest tub to relieve a pneumothorax. Which action by the new nurse indicates need for additional teaching about chest tube care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The suction is discontinued when the patient is ambulated to the bathroom.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The collection device is emptied at the end of the shift and output recorded in the chart.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s bed is placed in the semi-Fowler‘s position to facilitate lung reexpansion.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient is encouraged to use his incentive spirometer at least 10 times every hour.

questions.rationale: The chest tube collection device is not emptied at the end of the shift. I nstead, the amount of drainage present at the end of the shift (or specified time) is marked on t he collection device and the amount of drainage is documented in the patient‘s chart.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient who has just been diagnosed with a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in the right leg. Which focused assessment question has the highest priority for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Do you have a headache or any dizziness?‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Do you have any chest pain or shortness of breath?‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―When did you first notice the swelling and redness in your leg?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Do you have any cramping or muscle spasms in your leg?‖

questions.rationale: The highest risk of a DVT is the potential for the clot to break free and travel through the bloodstream to cause a pulmonary embolus (PE). The nurse should ask the patient about chest pain or shortness of breath to assess if a PE may have occurred.

questions.stem: The nurse identifies which patient who would benefit from postural drainage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A patient with a heart murmur and jugular venous distention

choices.label: B
choices.text: A patient with asthma and audible wheezing

choices.label: C
choices.text: A patient with right-sided heart failure and pitting edema

choices.label: D
choices.text: A patient with chronic bronchitis and congested cough

questions.rationale: Postural drainage is used for patients who have difficulty removing thick secretions from the airway. A patient with chronic bronchitis and a congested, productive cough would benefit from postural drainage because it would help clear the airway. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a history of congestive heart failure with generalized pitting edema. Which laboratory results will the nurse expect to find in the patient‘s chart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glycosylated hemoglobin 12%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Platelet count 450,000/mm3

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hematocrit 32%

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prothrombin time 8.8 seconds

questions.rationale: Hemodilution is a common finding when patients are in fluid overload caused by congestive heart failure. A normal hematocrit result is 42% to 52% for a male and 37% to 47% for a female, so the patient‘s 32% Nhe mRat oIcrit Gl eveBl .isCm arMked ly low. The other laboratory results are not expected due to congestive heart failure or fluid overload.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with high cholesterol who has been prescribed atorv astatin (Lipitor). Which laboratory result indicates that the patient has been taking the medication as ordered and following the physician‘s dietary recommendations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Serum triglyceride level 325 mg/dL

choices.label: B
choices.text: High-density lipoproteins (HDL) 56 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low-density lipoproteins (LDL) 155 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Total cholesterol level 185 mg/dL

questions.rationale: Total cholesterol levels should be less than 200 mg/dL, so a cholesterol level of 185 mg/dL indicates that the patient has been compliant with the prescribed therapy. The other laboratory results are abnormal and would not indicate compliance.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has presented to the ER with chest pain. Which diagnostic test will best indicate if there is significant blockage of important blood vessels that provide oxygen to the heart muscle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cardiac catheterization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chest x-ray

choices.label: C
choices.text: Echocardiogram

choices.label: D
choices.text: Electrocardiogram

questions.rationale: Cardiac catheterization includes the use of contrast dye to visualize the coronary arteries and determine blood flow to cardiac muscle. The other tests will not allow the physician to determine which (if any) coronary arteries are occluded. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse hears a loud murmur when listening to the patient‘s heart. Which diagnostic test will best display the condition of the valves and structures within the patient‘s heart that could be causing the murmur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chest x-ray

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac catheterization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Echocardiogram

choices.label: D
choices.text: Electrocardiogram

questions.rationale: Echocardiograms allow for ultrasound visualization of the structures of the heart along with function of the heart valves aNndUcRaSrdIiaNc GmTusBcu.laCtuOreM. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will be returning to the nursing unit following a cardiac catheterization via the right femoral artery. Which assessment is the highest pri ority for the nurse to perform when the patient arrives on the unit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Checking the patient‘s right pedal pulse and warmth of the right leg

choices.label: B
choices.text: Checking pulse oximetry and listening to the patient‘s lung sounds

choices.label: C
choices.text: Checking bilateral radial pulses to check for a pulse deficit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Estimating the patient‘s jugular venous pressure

questions.rationale: Cardiac catheterization includes the insertion of a large IV needle into the patient‘s femoral artery. Occlusion of the femoral artery may develop after the procedure leading to faint or absent pedal pulses and loss of warmth to the right leg. The nurse should check the patient‘s right pedal pulses and leg warmth to ensure that the femoral artery has not become occluded. The other assessments may be performed once the patient‘s right leg is found to be warm with strong pulses.

questions.stem: The home care nurse is caring for a patient who has severe COPD and home oxygen therapy. The patent tells the nurse that she feels much better after increasing the oxygen flowmeter from 2 L to 5 L/min. The patient‘s pulse oximetry is 98¯. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduce the oxygen flow rate until the patient‘s pulse oximetry value is more than 90%.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inform the patient‘s physician and obtain an order for oxygen at 5 L/min.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the intervention and findings in the patient‘s medical record.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Listen to the patient‘s lung fields and reinforce pursed-lip breathing techniques.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase the patient‘s oxygen to 4 L/min via nasal cannula.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Suction the patient‘s airway using sterile technique.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maintain eye contact and provide calm reassurance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Turn the patient onto the side for postural drainage.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administer the ordered nebulized bronchodilator.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Elevate the head of the patient‘s bed to fully upright.

questions.rationale: The goal of long-term therapy for the patient with COPD is usually to have an oxygen saturation level of more than 90%, which represents adequate delivery of oxygen to the tissues. Oxygen saturation may decrease during exercise, sleep, or deterioration of the respiratory status. For the patient with COPD, use low-flow oxygen delivery only ( 2 L/min) unless a higher level of oxygen administration is indicated by low oxygen saturation levels. High-flow oxygen may lead to respiratory suppression caused by loss of the patient‘s drive to breathe. The nurse should reduce the oxygen flow rate until the patient‘s pulse oximetry is more than 90% and educate the patient about oxygen therapy for COPD. The nurse is caring for a patient with advanced COPD who reports feeling short of breath. The nurse notes that the patient‘s lung sounds are diminished bilaterally and the patient‘s pulse oximetry is 91% on 2 L/min oxygen via nasal cannula. What actions will t he nurse take to make the patient more comfortable? (Select all that apply.) Patients who are acutely short of breath due to advanced COPD will benefit from nebulized bronchodilator medication to open the airways. Elevating the head of the bed will prevent pressure on the diaphragm from the abdominal contents. A caring demeanor with eye contact will help the patient remain calm until the medication begins to work and the shortness of breath is eased. Patients with COPD should be kept on low-flow oxygen to maintain pulse oximetry of more than

questions.stem: The nurse is performing a respiratory assessment on a patient. Which assessment findings indicate to the nurse that the patient has a history of long-standing chronic respiratory disease? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: All the patient‘s fingernails are noticeably clubbed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient needs to sleep on at least four to five pillows at night.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s chest has equal antero-posterior and transverse diameters.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s lower legs have large areas of brownish spotted discoloration.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient reports puffiness of both feet when standing for long periods.

choices.label: F
choices.text: The patient‘s forced vital capacity test result is 3.8 L of air.

questions.rationale: Clubbing of fingernails, the need to sleep in an upright position, and a barrel chest are all indicative of long-standing chronic respiratory disease like COPD. Brownish spotted discoloration is indicative of venous insufficiency. Edema can be seen in renal and heart failure. Forced vital capacity of almost 4 L is found in patients with good respiratory function.

questions.stem: The nurse notes the following findings when assessing a patient with COPD. Which require prompt nursing intervention? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is unable to speak without gasping.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s fingernails are noticeably clubbed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s sputum has turned from yellow to greenish-brown.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has stridor with wheezes heard in all lung fields.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient‘s forced vital capacity has increased from 2.8 to 3.4 L.

choices.label: F
choices.text: The patient has become cNonUfuRsSedIaNnGd TmBild.lyCdOisMoriented.

questions.rationale: A patient who is unable to speak without gasping is indicative of poor airflow through the airways, which must be addressed promptly. Greenish-brown sputum may indicate pneumonia requiring antibiotic treatment. Stridor and wheezes are indicative of an acute asthma attack. Confusion and disorientation in a patient with COPD may indicate retention of carbon dioxide. Clubbed fingernails are indicative of a chronic respiratory condition. Increased forced vital capacity is a positive sign.

questions.stem: The nurse is working with a nursing assistant to care for a patient with a new tracheostomy. Which tasks may the nurse delegate to the assistant? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtaining masks, gloves, and suction supplies from the utility room

choices.label: B
choices.text: Helping to reassure the patient before, during, and after suctioning

choices.label: C
choices.text: Changing the Velcro or twill ties used to secure the tracheostomy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transporting sputum specimens to the lab for culture and sensitivity testing

choices.label: E
choices.text: Assessing need for suctioning of the oropharynx or tracheostomy

choices.label: F
choices.text: Teaching the patient how to remove and clean the inner cannula

questions.rationale: Care of a new tracheostomy may not be delegated to a nursing assistant. Obtai ning supplies needed for care, helping to reassure the patient, and bringing specimens to the lab are tasks that may be assigned to the assistant.

questions.stem: The preceptor is working with a new nurse to provide care for a patient with a new tracheostomy. Which actions by the new nurse indicate need for additional teaching about the procedure? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The outer cannula is cleaned with the brush and half-strength H2O2.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The new tracheostomy holder is secured before the old soiled one is removed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A Yankauer suction catheter is used to remove secretions from the patient‘s mouth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sterile gloves are applied before the soiled dressing is removed from the tracheostomy.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Half-strength H2O2 is used to remove crusted secretions around the tracheostomy site.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Pain medication is administered to the patient prior to suctioning.

questions.rationale: Only the inner cannula of the tracheostomy is removed for cleaning. The outer cannul a stays in the trachea to maintain airway patency. Clean gloves are applied before the soiled dress ing is removed. Normal sterile saline is used to remove secretions that have built u p on the inner cannula and also is used to clean the patient‘s skin as needed.

questions.stem: The preceptor is working with a new nurse to suction a patient through a new tracheostomy. Which actions by the new nurse indicate need for additional teaching about the procedure? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The suction is not applied to the catheter until it is being withdrawn.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is placed in the supine position prior to suctioning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The suction catheter is twirled side to side as it is being withdrawn.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suction is applied continuously as the catheter is withdrawn.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient‘s oxygen is reapplied between suction attempts.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Water-soluble lubricant is applied to the suction catheter before insertion.

questions.rationale: The head of the patient‘s bed should be elevated prior to suctioning to facilitate coughing out secretions. Suction is always applied intermittently as the catheter is withdrawn. Water-soluble lubricant is used when suctioning the naris but not a tracheostomy  because the secretions negate the need for additional lubrication. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse will be caring for a patient who is severely malnourished. Laboratory test results show that the patient‘s albumin level is critically low. What assessment finding will the nurse expect to note when assessing the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient has generalized 3+ pitting edema.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is confused and disoriented.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s urine is dark and very concentrated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient lung sounds are very diminished.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s low albumin level will lead to generalized pitting edema because there isn‘t enough protein in the blood to keep water within the bloodstream. Lack of oncotic pressure from low serum albumin leads to edema. The other findings are not related to malnutrition. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is reviewing the patient‘s laboratory results. Which result must be communicated to t hephysician immediately? a .  Serumchloride level 85 m E qN/ L  R I GB.CM

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serum sodium level 134 m UE q / LS NT O

choices.label: C
choices.text: Serum potassium level 6.8 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serum magnesium level 2.3 mEq/L

questions.rationale: Normal serum potassium level is 3.5 to 5.0 mEq/L. A serum potassium level of 6.8 mEq/L is very high and puts the patient at risk for cardiac arrhythmias. The potassium level should be reported to the physician immediately. The chlorine and sodium levels are slightly low and the magnesium level is slightly elevated.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is at risk for fluid overload due to a history of congestive heart failure. Which intervention will the nurse teach the patient to perform at home to monitor fluid balance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Check to make sure that your urine is a bright yellow color.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Weigh yourself every morning before breakfast.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Count your heart rate every evening before you go to bed.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Drink plain water rather than soda, coffee, or fruit juice.‖

questions.rationale: Checking the weight every morning before breakfast is a sensitive indicator of the patient‘s fluid volume status. Weight gain of 2 kg in 3 days generally indicates fluid retention and should be reported to the physician.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is admitted to the hospital with diabetic ketoacidosis. Which assessment finding indicates an attempt made by the patient‘s body to correct the pH?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient‘s respirations are very deep and rapid.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s urine is dark and concentrated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s skin is pale, cool, and diaphoretic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient is sleepy and difficult to arouse.

questions.rationale: The patient with diabetic ketoacidosis is in a state of metabolic acidosis. The body will attempt to compensate for the acidosis by blowing off extra amounts of carbon dioxide through deep, rapid respirations. Since carbon dioxide is converted to carbonic acid, removal of carbon dioxide will help shift the body‘s pH to a less acidotic state.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who takes furosemide (Lasix) daily to treat congestive heart failure. The nurse will watch for which electrolyte imbalance that may occur due to this therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypocalcemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypernatremia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypokalemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hyperphosphatemia

questions.rationale: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that causes loss of potassium through the urine. Patients taking this medication are at risk for hypokalemia, so the nurse should check the patient‘s electrolyte values closely, particularly the serum potassium level. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who was brought to the ED after overdosing on narcotic pain medication. The patient was found unresponsive with no respirations. Arterial blood gases were drawn shortly after the patient‘s arrival to the hospital. Which results will the nurse expect to see?

choices.label: A
choices.text: pH 7.56, PaCO2 32 mm Hg, HCO3 32 mEq/L, PaO2 90 mm Hg

choices.label: B
choices.text: pH 7.35, PaCO2 45 mm Hg, HCO3 26 mEq/L, PaO2 70 mm Hg

choices.label: C
choices.text: pH 7.45, PaCO2 38 mm Hg, HCO3 28 mEq/L, PaO2 80 mm Hg

choices.label: D
choices.text: pH 7.27, PaCO2 58 mm Hg, HCO3 24 mEq/L, PaO2 60 mm Hg

questions.rationale: The patient who overdosed on narcotic pain medication will be in respiratory acidosis due to respiratory suppression. Low pH of 7.27 and elevated PaCO2 are consistent with respiratory acidosis as insufficient carbon dioxide is removed from the blood. The low 60 mm Hg PaO2 is due to insufficient oxygen intake.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is admitted to the hospital with dehydration and gastroenteritis. The patient attempted to walk to the bathroom and fainted right after getting out of bed. The nurse knows which condition to be the most likely cause of the patient‘s collapse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Orthostatic hypotension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Circulatory overload

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemolytic reaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Catheter embolism

questions.rationale: The patient with dehydration is at risk for orthostatic hypotension or falling of the blood pressure when the patient rises to a standing position. When the blood pressure falls sufficiently, fainting may occur. The patient should be assisted to rise slowly from a supine to a sitting position first before slowly getting to his feet. Circulatory overload, hemolytic anemia, and catheter embolism are unlikely to be causative factors. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient whose ABG results reveal the following: pH 7.56, PaCO2 32 mm Hg, HCO3 42 mEq/L, PaO2 90 mm Hg. Which condition will the nurse expect to see in the patient‘s chart as the underlying cause of these results?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gastroenteritis with severe nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea

choices.label: B
choices.text: Widespread tissue ischemia caused by cardiogenic shock

choices.label: C
choices.text: Respiratory failure caused by pneumonia with pleural effusions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hyperventilation after a panic attack

questions.rationale: Gastroenteritis with nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea will lead to a metabolic alkalosis resulting from loss of electrolytes and acids through emesis and loose stools. Metabolic alkalosis features the elevated pH of 7.56, elevated HCO3 42 mEq/L, and normal PaCO2 of 32 mm Hg. Widespread tissue ischemia would lead to metabolic acidosis with low pH resulting from release of lactic acid from the tissues. Respiratory failure leads to a respiratory acidosis with a low pH and elevated PaCO2 level. Hyperventilation leads to respiratory alkalosis with an elevated pH and elevated HCO3 level.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a 1200 mL daily fluid restriction. The patient has consumed 250 mL with each of the three meals and had another 150 mL with medication administration. The patient has received 150 mL of IV fluids during the day. How many mL of fluid may the patient still consume to stay within the prescribed fluid restriction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 100 mL

choices.label: B
choices.text: 150 mL

choices.label: C
choices.text: 250 mL

choices.label: D
choices.text: 300 mL

questions.rationale: The patient has had an oral fluid intake of 900 mL and an IV fluid intake of 150 mL, giving a total of 1050 mL. This leaves 150 mL that the patient may consume for the rest of the evening to stay within the prescribed fluid restriction.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a history of congestive heart failure. The nurse includes the diagnosis hypervolemia in the patient‘s care plan. Which goal statement has the highest priority for the patient and nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient‘s lung sounds will remain clear.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will have urine output of at least 30 mL/hr.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will verbalize understanding of fluid restrictions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s pitting pedal edema will resolve within 72 hours.

questions.rationale: Oxygenation is the highest prNioUriRtySfIorNthGeTpBat.ieCnt wMi t h congestive heart failure and hypervolemia. Keeping the patient‘s lungs clear is the most important goal for the nurse to consider when caring for this patient.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) who has a serum sodium level of 118 mEq/dL and symptoms of fluid overload. Which IV fluid will the nurse expect to administer to this patient to correct the patient‘s fluid imbalance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 0.33% normal saline

choices.label: B
choices.text: 0.45% normal saline

choices.label: C
choices.text: 0.9% normal saline

choices.label: D
choices.text: 3% normal saline

questions.rationale: A hypertonic 3% saline solution will be used to correct the patient‘s hyponatremia and fluid overload that have developed due to SIADH. A 0.9% normal saline solution can be used once the serum sodium level has been raised nearer to normal range. A 0.45% or 0.33% normal saline solution is hypotonic and will only worsen the patient‘s fluid overload and hyponatremia. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with congestive heart failure who requires intermittent IV bolus doses of furosemide (Lasix) to correct fluid volume overload. No continuous IV fluids are ordered. Which type of IV will the nurse insert to administer the patient‘s medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Peripherally inserted central catheter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Midline inside-the-needle catheter

choices.label: C
choices.text: Central venous catheter

choices.label: D
choices.text: Over-the-needle catheter

questions.rationale: Intermittent doses of IV diuretics are best administered via an over-the-needle angiocatheter that is connected to a saline lock. The other IV catheter options are used when the patient requires a vesicant drug that could cause significant damage to tissues or when the patient requires weeks of IV therapy. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a central venous catheter (CVC). Which nursing intervention is the most important for the nurse to include in the patient‘s plan of care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carefully document all assessments of the catheter site.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use strict sterile procedure when performing dressing changes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Label each new dressing with the date, time, and nurse‘s initials.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that the CVC is discontinued as soon as possible.

questions.rationale: Strict sterile procedure is mandatory when changing CVC dressings because of the high risk of septicemia and/or sepsis. The other actions are appropriate, but not of the highest priority.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a peripheral IV who tells the nurse that the IV site is painful and puffy. What is the nurse‘s best action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discontinue the IV and start another line in the other arm.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspirate to check for blood return and flush the IV with sterile saline.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Clean the IV site with chlorhexidine and apply a new sterile dressing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Change the IV tubing and administer prescribed pain medication.

questions.rationale: An IV site that is puffy and painful should be discontinued promptly because the fluid has infiltrated outside the vein and is causing localized irritation. The IV should be restarted in the other arm if possible. The other actions are inappropriate. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is to receive dopamine (Intropin) through the IV line. Which intervention has the highest priority when administering this medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check for IV blood return prior to administration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use a new IV tubing set each time the medication is administered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the date, time, and nurse‘s initials after each dose is administered.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use sterile gloves when drawing up and administering the medication.

questions.rationale: Dopamine is a vesicant and can cause significant irritation to blood vessels and tissues when administered via IV. For this reason, the nurse must ensure that the IV catheter is located correctly in the vein by checking for a blood return prior to administration. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is to receive a transfusion of pac ked red blood cells. The patient has a 22-gauge IV in his arm with 0.9% normal saline infusing. What intervent ion will the nurse perform before obtaining the packed red blood cells from the blood bank?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Identify the blood group, type, and expiration date with another nurse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess the patency of the current IV site for the administration of the packed RBCs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Program the IV infusion pump so that the transfusion will complete within 4 hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain a new microdrip tubing and extension tubing from the clean utility room.

questions.rationale: Before obtaining the blood from the blood bank, the nurse ensures the patient‘s IV is patent. Research confirms blood canNbe RsUa f eSIl y iNGnfusBed.iCn asMsmall as a 24 gauge IV. Then the nurse obtains the blood and double checks the blood group, type, expiration date, and patient ID with a second nurse. Next the nurse sets up the IV and IV pump. Blood is not run through a microdrip tubing set. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is receiving a blood transfusion. Fifteen minutes into the transfusion, the patient‘s blood pressure decreases significantly, and the patient complains of a severe headache. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check the patient‘s temperature and administer acetaminophen (Tylenol) if higher than 101 °F.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recheck the patient‘s blood pressure in 15 minutes after administering pain medication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stop the blood transfusion and administer 0.9% normal saline through new IV tubing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Double-check that the transfusion blood type is an exact match to the patient.

questions.rationale: A significant drop in blood pressure and a severe headache are signs that the patient may be experiencing a transfusion reaction. Also, most reactions to a transfusion occur within the first 15 minutes of initiation. Therefore the nurse should remain with the patient at the bedside during this time to observe for signs of a reaction. The transfusion should be stopped and 0.9% normal saline should be administered through new IV tubing to prevent infusion of additional blood through the tubing used for the transfusion. The physician should be notified immediately to evaluate the patient. Ensuring that the transfusion blood type is an exact match to the patient is done before the transfusion is begun. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is very dehydrated. Which goal best indicates that dehydration has been corrected and that the patient‘s fluid balance has been restored?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient had 1300 mL of light yellow urine in the last 24 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s lung sounds are clear bilaterally.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient has no jugular venous distention.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient verbalizes need for adequate daily fluid intake.

questions.rationale: The goal that best indicates that the patient‘s dehydration has been corrected is output of 1300 mL of clear yellow urine in the last 24 hours. Dark concentrated urine is a symptom of dehydration. Jugular venous distention and presence of crackles in the lungs are both indicative of fluid volume overload. NateUgRorSy:IPNhyGsiTolBog.icCalOInMtegrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is admitted with a serum sodium level of 120 mEq/L. Which is the most important intervention for the nurse to perform?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Perform regular neurologic checks and institute seizure precautions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encourage the patient to eat foods that are high in sodium.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer hypotonic IV solutions as ordered by the physician.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess for signs and symptoms of digoxin (Lanoxin) toxicity.

questions.rationale: A serum sodium level of 124 mEq/L is dangerously low and may cause neurol ogic problems including seizures, confusion, and weakness. Regular neurologic checks should be performed and the patient should be placed on seizure precautions until the sodium level is corrected. Encouraging the patient to eat high-sodium foods is fine, but it is not as important  as the patient‘s safety. A hypotonic saline solution will further lower the patient‘s sodium level. Digoxin toxicity is seen with hypokalemia rather than hyponatremia.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a history of congestive heart failure and takes once- daily furosemide (Lasix) to prevent fluid overload and pulmonary edema. The patient admits to stopping the medication due to nocturia. What is the nurse‘s best response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―You should ask your doctor to decrease the dose.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Take the diuretic early in the morning before breakfast.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Eat foods high in potassium and limit your salt intake.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―Restrict your fluid intake after dinner and in the evening.‖

questions.rationale: The patient should be instructed to take the diuretic early in the morning so that the effects will wear off before the patient goes to bed at night. Decreasing the dose could  lead to fluid overload and pulmonary edema. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with renal failure who has a serum potassium level of 7.1 mEq/L and serum magnesium level of 3.5 mEq/L. The nurse prepares to administer 10 units of insulin and an ampule of 50% dextrose to the patient. The patient asks why he will be receiving insulin when he is not diabetic. What is the nurse‘s best answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―The doctor has prescribed these medications for you to help heal your kidneys.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―These medications will lower your potassium level and prevent an irregular heart rate.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―These medications will prevent you from having a seizure from too little magnesium.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―These medications will iNn c rRea s eIyo Gu r uBr i n.eCo u tMp u t until your kidneys recover.‖

questions.rationale: Serum potassium levels above 7.0 mEq/L can lead to dangerous cardi ac arrhythmias, so the potassium level must be lowered promptly. Administration of IV insulin w ith 50% dextrose will push potassium into the cells to avoid hyperkalemia symptoms.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a history of hyperparathyroidism wh o presents with a serum calcium level of 14.5 mg/dL. What is the highest priority Nursing diag nosis for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Risk for injury related to weakened bones that may easily fracture

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lack of knowledge related to need for supplemental calcium in diet

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk for constipation caused by decreased gastrointestinal motility

choices.label: D
choices.text: Activity intolerance related to muscle cramping and spasms

questions.rationale: Chronic hypercalcemia can lead to weakened bones as strengthening calcium is removed over time. Pathologic fractures can easily result, so risk for injury is a high priority Nursing diagnosis for this patient. The other Nursing diagnoses apply but are less important than the safety of the patient.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has a serum magnesium level of 0.8 mEqL. Which is the highest priority goal to include in the patient‘s plan of care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will maintain urine output of at least 30 mL/hr.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will verbalize the importance of sufficient dietary intake of magnesium.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient‘s oral mucous membranes will remain free of ulceration and pain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will remain alert and oriented 3 with no confusion or seizure activity.

questions.rationale: A patient with low serum magnesium is at risk for neurologic symptoms including confusion, disorientation, and seizures. The highest priority goal for this patient is to avoid neurologic problems that could lead to injury. The other goals are applicable to the patient with low magnesium but are less important.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a hospitalized patient with hyperparathyroid disease and a serum calcium level of 14.2 mg/dL. What is the priority intervention of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Instruct the patient to always call for assistance before getting out of bed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assist the patient to change into dry clothing after episodes of diaphoresis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teach stress-relieving techniques, including progressive muscle relaxation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the provider if urine output is less than 30 mL/hr.

questions.rationale: The patient with hypercalcemia should always call for assistance before getting out of bed because of the risk of falling due to muscle weakness, soft bones, and lethargy. Diaphoresis and decreased urine output are not common symptoms of hypercalcemia. Teaching stress-relieving techniques is not a priority.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has B-positive blood. The patient is severely anemic and requires a blood transfusion. The nurse knows which types of blood can the p atient receive? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: AB positive

choices.label: B
choices.text: AB negative

choices.label: C
choices.text: B negative

choices.label: D
choices.text: B positive

choices.label: E
choices.text: O positive

choices.label: F
choices.text: O negative

questions.rationale: If a person produces the B antigen, the blood type is classified as B. Type O blood is classified as universal donors because their blood cells contain no antigens. Rh positive (Rh+) blood which means the person has the Rh factor on the surface of the red blood cells. Those who do not have the Rh factor are considered Rh negative (Rh). A person who is B positive can receive B or O blood, and it can be positive or negative Rh factor. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who periodically has small streaks of fresh red  blood in the stool. The patient denies abdominal pain or loss of appetite. The nurse identifies wh at to be the most likely cause of this patient‘s bleeding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hemorrhoids

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bleeding gastric ulcer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Colon polyps

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perforated colon

questions.rationale: Bleeding hemorrhoids can lead to small streaks of fresh red blood in the stool. Bleeding gastric ulcer would lead to black, tarry stools as the blood is digested. Colon polyps do not cause bleeding. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has diarrhea and identifies which priority nursing diagnosis for this patient? a. Lack of knowledge related to prescribed diet modifications

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired nutritional intakNeUreRlaSteIdNtoGpToBor.apCpOetMite

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diarrhea related to excessive loss of fluid through stool

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anxiety related to incontinence with loose stools and need for clothing change

questions.rationale: Dehydration is the priority nursing problem for this patient, so diarrhea is the most important Nursing diagnosis. Impaired nutritional intake, lack of knowledge, and anxiety can be addressed once fluid balance is restored.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is prescribed diphenoxylate-atropine (Lomotil). Which assessment finding by the nurse indicates a need to contact the prescriber and question t he order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient has skin breakdown from loose stools.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient is constipated with last BM 3 days ago.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient is on a low-fiber, gluten-free diet.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has painful bleeding hemorrhoids.

questions.rationale: Diphenoxylate-atropine is an antidiarrheal medication. It should not be given to patients who are constipated until the patient is checked for impaction. The other assessment findings are not contraindications. Therapies

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for an immobile patient who has abdominal pain and frequent small, liquid stools. The patient vomited his breakfast and is still nauseated. Which action by the nurse is the highest priority?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide oral care after each episode of emesis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply a skin barrier to the patient‘s perineal area.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Check the patient for a fecal impaction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer antiemetic medication with a sip of water.

questions.rationale: The patient who has abdominal pain and frequent small liquid stools should be checked for fecal impaction, especially since the patient is vomiting. Immobility is a risk factor for the development of fecal impaction. The other actions can be performed once fecal impaction is ruled out.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is recovering from bowel surgery. Which assessment finding best indicates that theNbUoRwSelIisNsGtaTrtiBng.tCoOreMsume function and the patient will be able to resume oral intake soon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient has bowel sounds x 4 quadrants and is passing gas.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient has no nausea, and abdominal pain is minimal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient feels hungry for chicken soup and hot tea.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s nasogastric tube was discontinued the previous day.

questions.rationale: The presence of bowel sounds and passage of flatus indicate that the patient‘s bowels are starting to resume function and the patient will be able to resume oral intake soon. Hunger, discontinuation of the NG tube, or absence of nausea are not definite indicators of readiness to resume oral feedings.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has an ileostomy. Which Nursing diagnosis has the highest priority for the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired skin integrity r/t localized skin irritation from liquid stool

choices.label: B
choices.text: Social isolation r/t potential leakage of stool from ostomy appliance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lack of knowledge r/t care and maintenance of ostomy appliance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Disturbed body image r/t presence of stoma and altered elimination

questions.rationale: U S The highest priority Nursing diagnosis for this patient is impaired skin integrity because the liquid stool from the ileostomy quickly leads to breakdown when in contact with the skin. Open sores can lead to bacterial infection and significant discomfort for the patient. In addition, ostomy appliances do not adhere well to open wounds, increasing the risk for continuing skin breakdown. The other nursing diagnoses are appropriate for this patient but are not the highest priority.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is taking narcotic pain medication after surgery. Which breakfast choices will help prevent constipation and promote return to regular bowel function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Raisin bran with skim milk, fresh fruit, and wheat toast

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pancakes with maple syrup, bacon, and coffee with cream

choices.label: C
choices.text: Omelet with cheddar cheese, green pepper, and onions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bagel with cream cheese, and strawberry nonfat yogurt

questions.rationale: The postoperative patient taking narcotic pain medications is at risk for developing constipation. A high-fiber diet with plenty of liquids will help prevent this from occurring. Raisin bran, fruit, and wheat bread are all good sources of fiber.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patiNe nt RwhoIhasGnoBt h.aCd a bMo w e l movement for 2 days. Which is the priority nursing intervention for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain an order to administer a soap suds cleansing enema.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Teach the patient how to use the Valsalva maneuver.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discontinue medications that can cause constipation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s usual pattern of bowel movements.

questions.rationale: The nurse should assess the patient‘s usual pattern of bowel movements to determine if it is normal for the patient to have a bowel movement every 2 to 3 days. Patients should be taught not to use the Valsalva maneuver because it can lead to bradycardia or death. Medications are not independently discontinued by the nurse and this would require a conversation with the provider.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will be undergoing upper GI series testing the next day. Which instruction will the nurse provide to the patient about the upcoming exam?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―The back of your throat will be sprayed with numbing medicine.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―You will need to have a clear liquid diet and take a laxative tonight.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―You will be given a milky liquid to drink shortly before the test starts.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―You should not take your dose of warfarin (Coumadin) tonight.‖

questions.rationale: The patient is given a milky barium liquid to drink as part of the upper GI series, so the patient should be informed of this. The back of the throat is numbed for upper GI endoscopy, not an upper GI series. Warfarin is not contraindicated prior to an upper GI series, and no bowel prep is required.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will undergo colonoscopy testing. Which intervention will the nurse include in the patient‘s plan of care for the day before the test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide the patient with zinc oxide skin barrier cream for the perineal area.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obtain an order for a gentle laxative to be given once the test is completed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carefully assess the patient‘s ability to swallow liquids through a straw.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check the patient for allergies to shellfish and iodine-based contrast dyes.

questions.rationale: Complete bowel evacuation is required prior to colonoscopy so that the physician can visualize the interior of the large intestine. The patient will have multiple soft-liquid bowel movements as part of the bowel prep for the test, so skin barrier cream will be helpful to prevent perineal irritation. Laxatives will not be needed after the colonoscopy, and no contrast dyes are used.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is to have testing for fecal occult blood. What step will the nurse perform during this testing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep the patient on a clear liquid diet for 72 hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Send the samples to the laboratory while they are still warm.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inform the patient that several stool samples will be needed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use a sterile container when collecting the stool samples.

questions.rationale: Three stool samples are required for fecal occult testing to avoid missing blood that appears intermittently. A sterile container is not required, and the patient does not need to be on a clear liquid diet for the test. Stool samples for culture and sensitivity should be sent to the laboratory when they are fresh and warm.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is to have a cleansing enema. Which assessment finding b the nurse indicates a need to contact the prescriber and question the order?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is recovering from a traumatic brain injury.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient has not had a bowel movement for 3 days.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient is to have a lower GI series the following morning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient had an upper GI series performed the previous day.

questions.rationale: Patients with a traumatic brain injury often have increased intracranial pressure, w hich can be worsened with enema administration, thus putting the patient at risk for additional  neurologic damage. The provider should be contacted and the order should be questioned. Constipation, preparation for a lower GI series, and removal of barium from the colon after up per GI series are all indications for a cleansing enema.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient who underwent bowel resection surgery that morning. The nurse assesses the patient‘s abdomen and notes that there are hypoactive bowel sounds. The patient is resting quietly without nausea or vomiting. What is the appropriate action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep the patient NPO and document the findings in the chart.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer a laxative suppository to stimulate peristalsis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insert a Salem sump nasogastric tube to low continuous suction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify the surgeon and prepare the patient to return to surgery.

questions.rationale: The presence of hypoactive bowel sounds is an expected finding for the first hours after abdominal surgery. The patient should be kept NPO to prevent nausea and vomiting. A laxative should not be administered. A nasogastric tube is not needed unless the patient starts vomiting or a paralytic ileus develops.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is constipated and has not had a bowel movement for 3 days. The nurse performs a rectal examination and finds hard dry stool in the rectum. What is the best option to help the patient have a bowel movement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glass of warmed prune juice

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loperamide (Imodium)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral fiber supplement

choices.label: D
choices.text: An oil retention enema

questions.rationale: The patient with hard, dry stool in the rectum will benefit from an oil retention enema because it will soften the stool and make it easier to pass. Imodium is an antidiarrheal that will worsen the constipation. An oral fiber supplement and prune juice should be given after the patient has a bowel movement to prevent constipation from recurring.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has just completed 2 weeks of IV antibiotics for a severe infection. The patient now has frequent loose watery stools and a low-grade temperature. What is the most likely cause of the patient‘s new symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clostridium difficile infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paralytic ileus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fecal impaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Salmonella food poisoning

questions.rationale: Diarrhea, abdominal pain, and low-grade temperature after completing IV antibiotics are often caused by C. difficile infection. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Physiological Adaptation

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who had a colonoscopy earlier that day. The patient states that he still feels very bloated after the procedure. What is the best action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assist the patient to ambulate in the hall.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insert a rectal tube to remove retained flatus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer an enema to stimulate peristalsis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage oral intake of fluids and high-fiber foods.

questions.rationale: Ambulation is a good way to promote peristalsis and relieve bloating. An enema should not be used after colonoscopy. A rectal tube is not needed. Eating high-fiber foods soon after colo noscopy may increase gas and bloating.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a history of dementia who is incontinent of stool because of the inability to communicate the need to defecate. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer a daily laxative and take the patient to the toilet afterward.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Digitally remove stool from the patient‘s rectum every other day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insert a rectal tube to facilitate drainage of soft or liquid stool.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begin a prompted toileting program to facilitate bowel continence.

questions.rationale: Patients who cannot communicate the need to use the toilet often benefit from a prompted toileting program in which the patient is brought to the toilet at the same times each day to promote urinary and bowel continence. A rectal tube should not be used. Digital removal of the impaction should be avoided whenever possible. Laxatives should be used only when necessary because continued use will lead to dependence.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is recovering after hip surgery. The patient requires assistance to use the bathroom because no weight bearing is allowed on the right leg. Which goal is most important for the nurse to include for the diagnosis Impaired self-toileting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will demonstrate safe transfer technique between wheelchair and toilet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The call light will be answered promptly when the patient needs to use the toilet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Toileting will be scheduled in the morning when the patient needs to defecate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Toilet paper and handwashing items will be kept within easy reach of the patient.

questions.rationale: The highest priority goal for this patient is the demonstration of safe transfer technique between the chair and the toilet. The other statements are interventions performed by staff rather than goals that will be accomplished by the patient.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is recovering from gastroenteritis. The nurse teaches the patient about dietary recommendations as the digestive system recovers. Which menu selection by the patient indicates that additional teaching is needed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Applesauce

choices.label: B
choices.text: Orange Popsicle

choices.label: C
choices.text: White toast

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coffee with cream

questions.rationale: Coffee with cream should be avoided by patients recovering from gastroenteritis because milk proteins are difficult for the digestive system and caffeine increases peristalsis. Caffeine is also a diuretic, which can lead to continued dehydration.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has had a severe stroke and requires assistance to use the toilet. Which goal is the highest priority for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will remain continent with no perineal skin breakdown.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will state satisfaction with use of gait belt for toilet transfers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will regain ability to pull up clothing after using the toilet.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will have privacy once properly positioned on the toilet.

questions.rationale: The highest priority goal for this patient is continence with no perineal skin breakdown to maintain skin integrity and self-esteem. Patient statements of satisfaction and the ability to pull up clothing are important but not the priority over preventing skin breakdown. Privacy is an intervention to be performed by the staff rather than a goal for the patient.

questions.stem: A student nurse is working with a preceptor to administer an enema to the patient. Which action by the student prompts intervention and redirection by the preceptor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Water-soluble lubricant is applied to the end of the enema tubing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The enema tubing is primed with solution that has been warmed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient is positioned comfortably in the right side-lying Sims position.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient‘s bedpan is put at the bedside in preparation for use.

questions.rationale: The patient should be placed in the left side-lying Sims position prior to enema administration so that the enema fluid will readily flow through the colon without having to go uphill. The other actions demonstrate correct enema administration steps.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient who had a colostomy placed 2 days ago. The appliance needs to be changed for the first time. Which ostomy care actions can  the nurse delegate to the nursing assistant? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gently cleaning the stoma with warm water and a washcloth

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assessing the stoma and incision for signs of infection or ischemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Obtaining needed supplies from the clean utility room

choices.label: D
choices.text: Teaching the patient how to care for the ostomy after discharge

choices.label: E
choices.text: Determining which type of ostomy appliance to use

choices.label: F
choices.text: Application of skin protectant to the area surrounding the stoma

questions.rationale: The nursing assistant can gently clean the stoma with warm water and a washcloth, obtain needed supplies, and apply skin protectant. The nurse is responsible for assessment, teaching, and determining which ostomy appliance to use. N.6URSINGTTOBP:.ICmOpMl ementation

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will be having a colonoscopy the following  morning. Which items must be removed from the patient‘s dinner tray since they are not allowed prior to the test? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cherry-flavored gelatin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cream of chicken soup

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glass of apple juice

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coffee with cream and sugar

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lemon-flavored Italian ice

choices.label: F
choices.text: Can of ginger ale

questions.rationale: Patients who will undergo colonoscopy testing should have a clear liquid diet the day before  the exam, so cream of chicken soup and coffee creamer should not be consumed. Foods with red food coloring should also be avoided prior to colonoscopy. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is recovering from septic shock. Whil e in the ICU, the patient developed renal failure. The nurse recognizes which type of renal fail ure the patient most likely developed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prerenal

choices.label: B
choices.text: Renal

choices.label: C
choices.text: Postrenal

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mixed

questions.rationale: Prerenal failure occurs as a result of reduction in blood flow to the kidneys, which would occur with septic shock. Causes of prerenal failure include dehydration, vascular collapse, and low cardiac output. Structural issues with the kidneys, from primary glomerular diseases or vascular lesions, result in renal failure. Postrenal failure is related to a mechanical or functional obstruction of the flow of urine.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a neurological condition that causes cons tant severe thirst, drinking fluids c o n t i n uNo uUs Rl yS, aInNd GvoTidBin.gC3OtoM4 L of clear yellow urine daily. Which term will the nurse use in the record to describe this patient‘s urinary output?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anuria

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oliguria

choices.label: C
choices.text: Polyuria

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enuresis

questions.rationale: Urinary output greater than 2500 mL/day is polyuria. Insufficient urine output is oliguria, whereas absence of urine is anuria. Enuresis is commonly known as ―bedwetting‖ at night.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is experiencing stress incontinence. The nurse identifies which goal to be the most important for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will carefully complete a voiding diary for the duration of 2 weeks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will not experience involuntary urination during coughing or sneezing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will be able to recognize and effectively manage perineal dermatitis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will demonstrate how to appropriately use urinary incontinence products.

questions.rationale: The patient with stress incontinence experiences loss of urine when coughing, sneezing, laughing, or exercising. The highest priority goal for this patient is to not experience incontinence at all and remain continent through all daily activities. If the patient remains continent, perineal dermatitis will not be a problem and urinary incontinence products will not be needed. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a postoperative patient whose urinary catheter was removed 8 hours previously. The patient has not been able to void since the catheter was removed and now reports suprapubic pain. What is the priority action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encourage oral fluid intake and administer a diuretic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obtain a urine sample to test for culture and sensitivity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calculate the patient‘s daily intake and output.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain an order to straight-catheterize the patient.

questions.rationale: The patient who has not voided for 6 to 8 hours after urinary catheter removal and is complaining of suprapubic pain has acute urinary retention. The physician should be notified to obtain an order for straight catheterization to drain the bladder. A urine sample for culture and sensitivity is not ordered. Encouraging fluid intake and administering a diuretic will increase the amount of urine in the bladder and make the patient even more uncomfortable.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who recently underwent ileal conduit surgery. Which nursing diagnosis is the highest priority for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired sexual function related to changed body structure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Social isolation related to potential for accidental leakage of urine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lack of knowledge related to care and maintenance of ostomy appliance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Disturbed body image related to presence of stoma and appliance

questions.rationale: The patient with a new ileal conduit needs to learn how to care for the urinary stoma and appliance prior to discharge from the hospital. If the appliance is not used and applied correctly, the patient may experience urinary leakage and significant skin breakdown from exposure to urine. The other diagnoses are less important than the patient‘s lack of knowledge about ostomy care.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who reports an urgent need to urinate but is unable to pass more than a few drops of urine in the toilet. Which is the priority assessment to be performed by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bladder scan to determine the amount of urine in the bladder

choices.label: B
choices.text: Auscultation to assess circulation through the right and left renal arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bimanual palpation to assess for possible enlargement of the kidneys

choices.label: D
choices.text: Calculate the patient‘s intake and output to check for fluid volume deficit

questions.rationale: The patient with suspected urinary retention should have a bladder scan performed to determine the amount of urine in the bladder. If a significant amount of urine is found in the bladder, the provider may be notified to obtain an order for straight catheterization.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a seriously ill patient whose laboratory results show a serum creatinine level of 3.5 mg/dL and a serum BUN of 35 mg/dL. Which conclusion can the nurse draw  from these test results?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is severely dehydrated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s kidneys have been damaged.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient has a urinary tract infection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has developed a renal calculus.

questions.rationale: Elevated BUN and creatinine are found in laboratory test results when the kidneys have been damaged and are unable to sufficiently clear metabolic wastes from the bloodstream. A dehydrated patient may have an elevated BUN, but the serum creatinine should be normal. Urinary tract infection and kidney stone (renal calculus) would not cause elevated BUN and creatinine levels.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has developed kidney failure. Which test finding leads the nurse to contact the nephrologist and arrange for emergency hemodialysis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Potassium level 6.8 mmol/L

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serum creatinine level of 2.8 mg/dL

choices.label: C
choices.text: Large amounts of protein in the urine

choices.label: D
choices.text: 1500 mL of retained urine in the bladder

questions.rationale: Patients in renal failure often require dialysis to reduce serum potassium levels to less than 5.5 mmol/L . Critically high serum potassium levels can lead to lethal arrhythmias and must be corrected promptly. Patients with advanced renal failure may require emergency hemodialysis if the potassium level does not lower with other methods (insulin and 50% dextrose, kayexalate). An elevated creatinine is consistent with kidney dysfunction. Large amounts of protein in the urine occurs in some diseases. 1500 mL of retained urine requires straight catheterization. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who will undergo ultrasound testing of the bladder and kidneys the next morning. Which instruction will the nurse provide to the patient about the test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―A small IV will be inserted into your arm to inject the contrast dye.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―You will need to drink lots of water but not use the toilet.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―You should not have anything to eat or drink after midnight.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―You will receive a cleansing enema before you have the test.‖

questions.rationale: No preparation is needed for kidney and bladder ultrasound other than having the patient drink lots of fluid beforehand. The patient is instructed not to use the toilet so that the bladder will be filled and easy to visualize. No contrast dye, enemas, or fasting is required.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has urinary retention resulting from benig n prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). The patient requires catheterization in order to drain the urine from his bladder. Which action will the nurse take to facilitate this procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain a Coudé catheter for insertion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Attach a leg bag to the catheter prior to insertion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trim the pubic hair before cleaning the perineal area.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wait until the bladder is full to perform catheterization.

questions.rationale: A Coudé catheter is used when there is narrowing or constriction of the urethra, making insertion of a regular indw ellNin g Rcat hIe terGdUifBfi.cuCl t. TMh e Coudé catheter has a special tip on theend that is designed to facilitate insertion of the catheter through the narrowed urethra caused by BPH. Coudé catheters may need to be placed using a metal wire introducer. Placement using an introducer typically is performed by a provider or the patient‘s urologist, to avoid damaging urethral tissue. Trimming the pubic hair will not facilitate catheterization. Attaching a leg bag to the catheter prior to insertion is not needed because a bedside collection bag will usually be used at first.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for an incontinent male patient who has a deep decubitus ulcer on his sacrum. Which intervention will best manage the patient‘s urinary incontinence and facilitate healing of the ulcer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use of disposable absorbable incontinence briefs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Daily application of perineal barrier cream containing zinc oxide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Careful perineal care and application of a condom catheter

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insertion of a single-lumen straight urinary catheter

questions.rationale: Condom catheters allow for collection of urine in the incontinent patient without the infection risks of an indwelling catheter. The condom catheter is applied to the outside of the penis like a condom instead of being inserted into the urethra. Careful perineal care is performed prior to application of the condom catheter and regularly thereafter. Use of disposable briefs or perineal barrier cream will not facilitate healing of the sacral ulcer. A single-lumen straight urinary catheter is used to drain the bladder to relieve urinary retention or to obtain a urine sample for testing. A straight catheter is not used for management of incontinence.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has urinary frequency. Which nursing diagnosis is the highest priority for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired urination r/t occasional incontinence

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anxiety r/t living alone at home with nocturia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk for infection r/t urine contact with perineal area skin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk for fall-related injury r/t hurried trips to the bathroom during the day and night

questions.rationale: Risk for falls is the highest priority diagnosis for this patient because rushing to the bathroom can lead to loss of balance and serious injury. Walking to the bathroom at night is even more dangerous because of low lighting conditions and sleepiness. The other nursing diagnosis may be appropriate but not higher than the injury risk. NateUgRorSy:ISNafGe aTnBd E.fCfeOctiMve Care Environment: Safety and Infection Control

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has just had an intravenous pyelography (IVP) completed. Which assessment is the nurse‘s highest priority after the patient returns from the test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Calculate the patient‘s intake and output.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Monitor for discoloration of the patient‘s urine.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess for possible iodine or shellfish allergies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inquire if the patient has burning or pain with urination.

questions.rationale: The nurse must carefully monitor the patient‘s intake and output after IVP testing to ensure that the patient‘s kidneys were not damaged by the contrast dye. PO fluid intake should be encouraged to facilitate excretion of the contrast dye. Urine is not discolored from the IVP. Burning or pain with urination should not occur after IVP testing because there is no instrumentation of the urinary tract. Assessment of allergies must be done before the IVP is done because iodine-based contrast is used.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with benign prostatic hypertrophy who states that he feels a constant urge to urinate but cannot pass more than 30 to 60 mL of urine at a time. The nurse performs a bladder scan and finds that there are 1100 mL of urine in the patient‘s bladder. What is the priority nursing diagnosis for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anxiety r/t continual urge to urinate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reflex incontinence of urine r/t over-distention of the bladder

choices.label: C
choices.text: Impaired urination r/t obstruction of urinary bladder outlet

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impaired self-toileting r/t inability to pass urine into the toilet

questions.rationale: The patient has acute urinary retention with overflow as evidenced by 1100 mL of urine in the bladder and frequent passage of small amounts of urine. The priority nursing diagnosi s is thus Impaired urination r/t obstruction of urinary bladder outlet. Urinary retention is the cause of the patient‘s discomfort and drainage of the bladder will result in relief of the patient‘s symptoms. The patient is able to get himself on and off the toilet so toileting self-care deficit is not a problem. Reflex incontinence of urine r/t over-distention of the bladder is not as specif ic to this scenario as the nursing diagnosis of impaired urination.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who had prostate surgery the previous day. The patient has had significantly decreased urine output over the last shift despite ample oral and IV fluid intake. The patient‘s urine from the indwelling catheter is cherry red with occasional small clots. What is the appropriate action of the nurse? a. Remove the urinary catheter and replace it with a new one.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gently irrigate the cathete NrUuRsinSgIwNaGrmTeBd.stCerOile normal saline.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Send a sample of the patient‘s urine to the laboratory for analysis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Call the provider and obtain an order for kidney and bladder ultrasound.

questions.rationale: The patient most likely has decreased urine output caused by clot formation that is blocking urine from draining through the catheter. The catheter should be gently irrigated using sterile technique and warmed sterile saline to loosen clots and facilitate urinary drainage. The catheter should not be removed. Ultrasound and urinalysis are not necessary.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with the nursing diagnosis of Urge incontinence of ur ine related to urinary tract infection. Which statement is appropriate for the ―as evidenced by‖ portion of the patient‘s diagnosis?

choices.label: A
