choices.text: Sudden leakage of urine when patient is unable to get to the toilet in time

choices.label: B
choices.text: Continuous urine flow from the bladder regardless of attempts to use the toilet

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leakage of urine from the bladder when the patient coughs, sneezes, or laughs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Leakage of urine because the patient is unable to indicate need to use the toilet

questions.rationale: Urge incontinence of urine occurs when the patient has a sudden need to urinate but cannot get to the toilet in time. Continuous flow of urine is deemed total urinary incontinence. Leakage of urine when sneezing or coughing is stress incontinence. Functional incontinence occurs when the patient cannot indicate need to use the toilet.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with an indwelling urinary catheter caused by severe prostate enlargement. Which is the priority nursing diagnosis for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Risk for infection r/t indwelling urinary catheter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Disturbed body image r/t presence of catheter

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk for contamination r/t potential leakage of urine on clothing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impaired urination r/t blockage of bladder outlet

questions.rationale: The presence of an indwelling urinary catheter puts the patient at high risk for urinary tract infection, and this is the highest priority diagnosis for the patient. Disturbed body image is not as important as the risk of infection. Risk for contamination is not a nursing diagnosis. Impaired urination was corrected by placement of the urinary catheter.

questions.stem: The preceptor is watching a nursing student care for a male patient who requires a condom catheter. Which action by theNnUuRrsiSnIg NstuGdTenBt .inCdiOcaMtes that the procedure is performed correctly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sterile gloves are donned before touching the catheter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adhesive tape is applied securely around the base of the penis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Water-soluble lubricant is applied to the end of the catheter.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The foreskin is returned to its natural position before the catheter is applied.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s penis should be cleaned with soap and water with the foreskin retracted prior to condom catheter application. The foreskin should then be returned to its natural position before the catheter is applied. Adhesive tape should never be applied around the base of the penis because circulation may be compromised. Sterile gloves and lubricant are not needed.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient with a history of type 1 diabetes. Which assessment finding indicates to the nurse that the patient may not be compliant with the diabetic treatment regimen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is always thirsty and frequently voids very large amounts of urine.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s urine is very concentrated with a dark amber color.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient complains of throbbing flank pain and burning with urination.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has urinary hesitancy and difficulty initiating a stream of urine.

questions.rationale: A noncompliant diabetic patient will have elevated blood sugars that cause thirst and polyuria. Concentrated urine indicates dehydration. Throbbing flank pain and burning with urination are indicative of urinary tract infection. Urinary hesitancy and difficulty initiating urine stream are not indicative of elevated blood sugar levels.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is to undergo computed tomography (CT) of the kidneys and ureters. Which assessment finding by the nurse must be reported to the provider and radiologist before the patient has the procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient is allergic to bananas and latex.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient thinks that she might be pregnant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient has a family history of bladder cancer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient currently has a urinary tract infection.

questions.rationale: CT requires exposure to radiation similar to an x-ray, so the patient‘s provider and radiologist should be notified promptly of the possibility of pregnancy. The other conditions do not preclude CT scan examination for the patient. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for an eldNerUlyRpSaiIteNntGwTitBh.aChistMory of arthritis, urinary incontinence and poor perineal hygiene practices. The patient has had four urinary tract infections in the past year. Which is the priority goal for the nursing diagnosis Impaired health maintenance for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will be provided with educational materials about risks of urosepsis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will allow family members to assist with daily bathing and perineal care.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will discuss the possible consequences of frequent UTIs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regular home care nursing visits and follow-up telephone contact will be arranged.

questions.rationale: The priority for this patient is to improve personal hygiene and perineal care in order to reduce the risk of future urinary tract infections. The patient‘s agreement to allow family members to assist with bathing and perineal care will greatly reduce this risk. Providing educational materials about the risk of urosepsis, discussion of UTI consequences, and regular follow-up care are interventions rather than patient goals.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for an elderly patient whose dementia has become worse over the last 24 hours. The nurse suspects that the patient may have developed a urinary tract infection and obtains a urine sample. Which assessment findings prompt the nurse to contact the provider to obtain an order for urine culture and sensitivity testing? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Urinary dipstick testing is positive for nitrates.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The urine appears cloudy with a foul odor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The urine is concentrated and dark amber in color.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The urine smells faintly like sweet fruit.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient is urinating more frequently than usual.

choices.label: F
choices.text: The patient is normally continent but has been incontinent twice.

questions.rationale: Concentrated dark urine indicates dehydration rather than infection of the urinary tract. Urine that smells of sweet fruit contains ketones from high blood sugar . Urine that is cloudy with a foul odor and positive for nitrites is most likely due to urinary  tract infection. Frequent urination and incontinence are signs of urinary tract infection in the elderly. NCLEX Client Needs Category: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a male patient who will be performing intermittent self-catheterization at home. Which actions by the patient indicate the need for additional teaching about this procedure? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patency of the balloon is tested prior to insertion of the catheter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The catheter is inserted another 2 inches after urine is seen in the tubing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The catheter is carefully secured to the leg to prevent accidental removal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The foreskin is returned tNo UitsRnSaItuNraGl pToBs i t.i o nOa f t e r the catheter is removed.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Catheterization is performed regularly before the bladder becomes distended.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Water-soluble lubricant is generously applied along the length of the catheter.

questions.rationale: Only 5 to 8 inches of the catheter tip are covered with water-soluble lubricant. Patency of the balloon is only checked when indwelling catheters are inserted. Intermittent catheters need not be secured to the patient‘s leg because they will be removed after the bladder is drained. The other actions are correct.

questions.stem: The nurse is working with a new nursing assistant who is providing care to patients with urinary difficulties. Which actions by the nursing assistant indicates that additional teaching is required? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The length of the urinary catheter is cleaned up to the patient‘s perineum.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A urine sample is obtained from the drainage bag immediately after catheter insertion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A fresh condom catheter is applied every other day following careful perineal care.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zinc oxide barrier cream is applied liberally to the perineal area for incontinent patients.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The catheter drainage bag is disconnected in order to put pants on the patient.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Clean technique is used to obtain a urine specimen for culture and sensitivity from the catheter.

questions.rationale: The urinary catheter must be cleaned from the urinary meatus down toward the drainage bag rather than up toward the perineum. A fresh condom catheter must be applied daily. The catheter drainage bag should not be disconnected to put pants on the patient. The drainage bag can be threaded through the pants leg before putting pants on the patient. Sterile technique should be used to obtain samples from the catheter.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is to complete a 24-hour urine collection to measure creatinine clearance. Which tasks related to this test may be delegated to the nursing assistant? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Teaching the patient about sterile specimen collection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keeping the urine collection container cool on ice

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dumping the urine from the patient‘s first void

choices.label: D
choices.text: Restricting the patient‘s oral fluid intake during the test

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transporting the specimen to the laboratory for testing

choices.label: F
choices.text: Reminding the patient not to put toilet paper in the urine

questions.rationale: The nurse assistant can help the nurse by keeping the urine collection container cool on ice, dumping the urine from the patient‘s first void, and reminding the patient not to put toilet tissue in the urine specimen. NTheRnurIse aGsUs i sBSt a.n tCca nMa l so transport the specimen to the laboratory after the urine has been collected for 24 hours. Fluid intake should be encouraged during the test. Teaching the patient about the testing procedure is done by the nurse, although creatinine clearance testing does not require sterile technique. 3rd Edition

questions.stem: The hospice nurse is caring for a terminally ill patient. The patient‘s son is distraught because the patient will probably die within the next few days and there is nothing he can do  about it. What is the most appropriate nursing diagnosis for the patient‘s son currently?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic grief related to impending death of mother

choices.label: B
choices.text: Death anxiety related to feeling powerless over situation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Powerlessness related to progression of mother‘s terminal illness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complicated grieving related to desired avoidance of mourning

questions.rationale: The patient‘s son is experiencing death anxiety because he is unable to change the outcome of his mother‘s imminent death. The son makes no mention of religious beliefs, so impaired religiosity is not appropriate. Complicated grieving is applicable to individuals who have recently experienced a loss. Chronic grief is grief that continues for a long period of time.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a terminally ill patient whose children have come home to be with their mother during her last few days. They spend time looking through picture albums, watching old home movi es , and r eme mNbUe rRi nSg If uNn Gt iTmBe s.s pCeOn tMto ge th er. The nur s e ident ifies whi ch ter m that best describes the activity of the patient‘s children?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anticipatory grieving

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bereavement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Caregiver role strain

choices.label: D
choices.text: Death anxiety

questions.rationale: The patient and her children are experiencing anticipatory grief as they prepare for the expected death of the patient. Reminiscence and life review are used to assist those experiencing anticipatory grief with the realization that death is approaching.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a female patient who died a few minutes previously. The patient‘s family comes in to the room and immediately starts to wash the body in preparation for burial. What is the most appropriate action of the nurse currently?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inform the patient‘s family that the body must be transported to the morgue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruct the patient‘s family that hospital staff will provide postmortem care.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Obtain needed signatures for organ donation and autopsy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Offer to provide any needed supplies and provide privacy for the family.

questions.rationale: The most appropriate action of the nurse currently is to allow the family to wash the patient‘s body in accordance with their wishes and cultural values. The family may wish to participate in this procedure or may complete this procedure in private. Health care personnel should abide by their wishes as much as possible. Signatures may be obtained from the next of kin when washing is complete. The patient‘s body may be transported to the morgue or funeral home after washing is completed.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for an emergency room patient who died because of a misha p with a loaded gun. The patient‘s body will be transported to the coroner‘s office for an autopsy. Which items will the nursing staff remove from the body before it leaves the hospital?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Endotracheal tube

choices.label: B
choices.text: Foley catheter and IV line

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dentures

choices.label: D
choices.text: Necklace and watch

questions.rationale: Medical devices and tubes are not removed from the body if an autopsy is to be performed. The patient‘s necklace and watch may be removed and given to the patient‘s family members before the body is transported to the coroner‘s office for autopsy. Dentures should be left in the patient‘s mouth.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who suffered a miscarriage at 24 weeks of pregnancy. The patient is devastated by the loss but her husband minimizes her grief by stating, ―Quit crying. It‘s not like you lost a real baby.‖ What term best describes the anguish felt by the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Disenfranchised grief

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed grief

choices.label: C
choices.text: Moral distress

choices.label: D
choices.text: Masked grief

questions.rationale: The patient is experiencing disenfranchised grief because she cannot share the pain of her loss with her husband. The husband is not willing to support his wife as she mourns the lo ss of her pregnancy or recognize the grief that she is going through. Delayed grief is supp ression of the grief process. Moral distress occurs when people cannot act according to their moral values. Masked grief occurs when a person‘s bereavement behaviors interfere with his or her life, but the person does not notice this.

questions.stem: A patient has recently been given a terminal diagnosis. When family members offer to help, the patient snaps and yells at them, but then angrily accuses them of not helping. The patient‘s spouse is frustrated and asks the hospice nurse what to do about this situation. What response by the nurse is best?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Don‘t worry. Your spouse will get over this phase soon.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―Anger is an expected part of the grieving process.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Would your spouse be open to professional counseling?‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―This diagnosis is difficult to handle; just be patient.‖

questions.rationale: Anger is one of the stages of grief as identified by Elizabeth Kubler-Ross. The nurse would first explain this to the spouse. Telling the spouse the patient will get over the phase soon or that the diagnosis is difficult to handle is false reassurance and dismissive of the concerns. It is too early to consider counseling although the patient may need it later. This is also a yes/no question which is not therapeutic.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a terminally ill patient who appears to be calmly having a conversation with someone even though there is nobody else in the room. The patient reaches out and appears to take something out of thin air and hold it close. Which is the appropriate action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reorient the patient and reassure that nobody else is in the room.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Be present but quiet and let the patient continue the conversation. c. Carefully assess the patient‘s mental status and level of attention.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain a set of vital signsNaUndRcShIecNkGthTeBp.atCienOt‘s pulse oximetry.

questions.rationale: Patients who are near death sometimes have a special communication with loved ones who have already died. It is important to recognize that these experiences can be comforting to the dying patient, and nurses would not contradict or argue with the person. It is imperative to simply be present with the person, listen, and be open to any attempts to communicate. It is acceptable to ask gentle questions such as ―What are you seeing?‖ or ―How does that make you feel?‖ Having an open discussion with the family while describing what is occurring may provide further insight to the nurse as the health care provider, as well as promoting a sense of understanding and acceptance for the family. As long as the patient is calm and content, the best action of the nurse is to be present but let the patient continue the conversation undisturbed.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who died a few minutes ago. The patient‘s family is at the bedside and very demonstrative in their grief, weeping loudly and holding on to the patient‘s body. What is the most appropriate action of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inform the family that the patient‘s body must be taken to the morgue shortly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the family members to step outside while postmortem care is provided.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Obtain required signatures for the body to be taken to the funeral home.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provide privacy and allow the patient‘s family to grieve over the body.

questions.rationale: The nurse should allow the patient‘s family to grieve in private over the loss of their loved one. Some cultures favor free expression of emotions after death, and the nurse should respect this. Signatures can be obtained, postmortem care can be provided, and the body brought to the morgue after an appropriate time of grieving has been provided to the family.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who lost her husband 1 year ago after 55 years of marriage. The patient no longer takes care of herself or cooks and rarely eats, stating she has no appetite. The nurse determines that the Nursing diagnosis of complicated grieving applies to the patient. Which is the priority goal for the patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will shower every other day and eat at least two meals a day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will identify personal strengths that will increase coping ability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient will discuss the meaning of her loss with a family member or friend.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will be provided with phone numbers for local community resources.

questions.rationale: The highest priority goal of this patient is self-care including showering and eating in order to protect her health and safety. The other goals are lower priority after the patient‘s necessary activities of daily living are addressed. Goals should also reflect what the patient accomplishes; so the goal of being provided with phone numbers is actually something for the nurse to do.

questions.stem: The hospice nurse is caring for a several adult children shortly after the death  of a parent. They have various reactions as they deal with their loss. The nurse recognizes which reactions to be in the cognitive domain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They let the house get filthy because they can‘t be bothered to clean it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are tossing and turning all night and are unable to get a good night‘s sleep.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are easily distracted and often lose train of thought during conversation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They have lost their appetites and have no desire to eat anything.

questions.rationale: Cognitive deficits include the inability to concentrate and follow a conversation. Letting the house get filthy is a sign of apathy, which is in the behavioral domain. Ins omnia falls within the behavioral and physical domains. Loss of appetite is within the physical domain.

questions.stem: The hospice nurse is caring for a patient who is terminally ill. The patient‘s spouse is the primary caregiver, providing constant care and spending all his or her time meeting the patient‘s needs. The spouse says to the nurse ―After my spouse dies, I will finally get that colonoscopy my provider has been bugging me about.‖ What does the nurse understand about this statement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The spouse is looking forward to being freed from the caretaker role.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The spouse has neglected his or her own physical needs for too long.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The spouse is making some realistic plans for life after the death.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The spouse is in denial that the patient is dying and the important role of caregiver will end.

questions.rationale: Often caregivers neglect their own needs while in the caregiver role. The spouse  understands the patient will die soon and is being realistic in understanding his or her o wn physical needs have been neglected. This shows healthy coping.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has just died in a motor vehicle accident. What is the priority action of the nurse before the patient‘s family arrives to see the patient‘s body?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gently wash the body and provide perineal care.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remove the patient‘s dentures and jewelry.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure that the death certificate has been signed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determine which funeral home will pick up the body.

questions.rationale: Release of bowel and bladder contents often occur at the time of death, and the perineal care is a priority before the family arrives. The body should be gently cleaned to remove blood and debris from the accident. The patient‘s dentures and jewelry should not be removed from the body. The death certificate does not need to be signed before the family arrives. The family can decide which funeral home will be used and notify the nurse after their arrival.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is terminally ill with metastatic bone cancer. The patient tells the nurse that he is not afraid of death but does not want to be in p ain and suffer before he dies. Which intervention by the nurse will be most appropriate to meet this patient‘s wishes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Establish around-the-clock dosing for pain medications with additional doses for breakthrough pain.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assist the patient to reminisce and review his life, spending as much time as possible with loved ones.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use therapeutic touch, guided imagery, and soft music to put the patient at ease and relieve anxiety.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage the patient to participate in prayer and meditation along with preferred religious practices.

questions.rationale: The patient‘s primary wish is to die without pain, and the best intervention to meet this goal is administration of pain medication around the clock with extra doses for breakthrough pain. The other interventions may make the patient more comfortable but will not address his primary desire for adequate pain management.

questions.stem: The home care nurse is caring for a terminally ill patient who states that he  wants to set up a scholarship in his name at the local university before he dies. What  is the best action by the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Suggest that the patient think it over and wait a few days before contacting the school.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct the patient to ask his family about the possibility of starting a scholarship.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assess the patient‘s mental status to ensure that he is competent to make the decision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assist the patient to find the necessary information about endowed scholarships.

questions.rationale: As the patient‘s advocate, the nurse should help provide the necessary information for the patient to set up a scholarship if that is his decision. The patient does not need to discuss the subject with his family first, and assessment of the patient‘s mental status is not needed. The patient may not have the time to wait a few days before contacting the university.

questions.stem: Which statement by the patient indicates to the nurse that it may be an appropriate time to consider hospice care rather than further aggressive measures to treat his terminal illness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―I am praying every day that this last round of chemotherapy will work.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I want to spend what time I have left at home with my grandchildren.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I need to meet with my financial planner to make sure my life insurance is all set.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I am concerned that my wife won‘t be able to live on her own after my death.‖

questions.rationale: Hospice care is provided to patients who are terminally ill and wish to have no further aggressive treatment in attempt to cure the disease. The patient‘s statement that she just wants to be home with her grandchildren indicates a readiness for hospice care.

questions.stem: The hospice nurse is caring for a terminally ill patient who will probably die within the next hour or two. The patient‘s daughter is keeping a vigil by the bedside and asks what she can do to help her father at this time. What is the appropriate response of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Just let him know you are here, talk to him, and let him know that you love him.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―You can try to feed him a few bites of ice cream to keep his mouth from getting dry.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―You can take this time to ensure that arrangements are set with the funeral home.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―You should let me know when your father‘s breathing pattern changes.‖

questions.rationale: The patient‘s daughter should be encouraged to spend the last moments of her father‘s life with him, reassuring him with her presence. The daughter should be encouraged to continue talking with him because the patient may still hear her even if his eyes are closed and he does not speak. The nurse is responsible for monitoring the patient for breathing changes. Oral intake will lead to nausea and/or aspiration. This is not the time to make arrangements with the funeral home.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for an Islamic patient who has just died. The family is traveling from overseas. Which action is the priority for the nurse to take right after the patient dies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Arranging for embalming to preserve the body until burial

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rearrange the furniture so the bed can face Mecca

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arranging for transportation of the body to the crematorium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bringing in fruit for the patient‘s journey to the other world

questions.rationale: After death, a patient‘s body can be turned to face Mecca which is the holy site for Muslims. The nurse would need to find out which direction that is. The family will work with the funeral home to determine when and where burial will take place. Buddhists often bring fruit when someone dies.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient whose mother recently passed away. The patient states that she has not been able to concentrate or sleep since the funeral and is consuming increasing amounts of alcohol to get through each day. The nurse knows which goal to be most appropriate for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient will be referred to medical social services for evaluation and counseling.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient will be encouraged to describe previous stressors and coping mechanisms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nursing staff support patient‘s coping attempts and encourage verbalization of feelings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will use effective coping strategies with no alcohol consumption.

questions.rationale: Goals are met by the patient rather than nursing or medical staff. The patient‘s use of effective coping strategies without drinking alcohol is an appropriate goal. Referring the patient for counseling and encouraging the patient to verbalize stressors are interventions rather than goals.

questions.stem: The nurse sees a young child in the clinic whose mother has only a few weeks to live. The child has been misbehaving at school recently and is suspended after picking fights with other students and defying teachers. The nurse identifies which stage of grieving that the patient is experiencing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Denial

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anger

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bargaining

choices.label: D
choices.text: Depression

questions.rationale: The patient is angry over the impending death of the mother and is acting out this anger at school by picking fights and defying his teachers. Denial is a temporary defense while processing the information. Bargaining is negotiation to change the predicted outcome. Depression includes crying and sadness.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a terminally ill patient whose family is insistent that additional chemotherapy be administered even though the patient will most likely die within the next few days. What is the best response of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―The insurance company will not pay for chemotherapy at this stage.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―The focus right now needs to be on keeping your loved one comfortable.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―I will call the provider aNndUrRelSayIyNoGurTwBis.hCesO.‖M

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―The patient needs to get stronger first before chemotherapy can be administered.‖

questions.rationale: Nurses advocate for patients to ensure that they are aware of their options for care that include interventions, treatments, anticipated outcomes, as well as risk and benefits of any decision made concerning medical care. The nurse must function as the patient‘s advocate and encourage what is in the best interest of the patient. Chemotherapy will not extend the patient‘s life when death is expected within the next few days and will only make the patient suffer needlessly when it is administered. The patient will not get stronger over t he next few days, and this criterion for chemotherapy will never be met.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who is having difficulty coping after being in a motor vehicle crash in which her brother was killed. The patient was driving the car and blames herself for the accident. What is the priority nursing intervention of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Check to make sure that the patient does not want to hurt or kill herself.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Educate the patient about available support systems for grief resolution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enhance the patient‘s coping skills to alleviate depression and anxiety.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encourage the patient to meet with a spiritual leader for guidance.

questions.rationale: The highest priority for the nurse is to ensure the safety of the patient, so assessment of potential suicidal tendencies is paramount. The other interventions can take place once the nurse is confident that the patient will not try to hurt or kill herself.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a terminally ill patient who will probably die within the next 2 weeks. What is the priority nursing intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encouraging the patient to limit fluid intake to minimize congestion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Limiting the use of pain medications so that the patient can visit with family

choices.label: C
choices.text: Helping the patient to identify and complete desired tasks and activities

choices.label: D
choices.text: Completing funeral arrangements with the patient‘s next of kin

questions.rationale: The priority intervention for the nurse currently is to help the patient identify and complete desired tasks and activities while the patient is still able to do so. Pain management is a high priority at this time, so analgesics should never be limited unless requested by the patient. The patient can drink as much or as little fluid as desired.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a terminally ill patient who is actively dying and refuses to eat anything other than a few bites of ice cream. The patient‘s family member approaches the nurse and requests that a feedNinUgRtuSbIe NbeGiTnsBer.teCdOsoMthat her loved one will not starve to death. What is the best response of the nurse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ―Loss of appetite is a natural part of the dying process. Tube feedings would be uncomfortable and cause nausea.‖

choices.label: B
choices.text: ―I will contact the provider to obtain an order to insert the tube and start tube feedings.‖

choices.label: C
choices.text: ―Intravenous fluids would be more comfortable for the patient than a tube feeding. I will call the doctor to get the order.‖

choices.label: D
choices.text: ―I will listen to the patient‘s abdomen to make sure that bowel sounds are present and try encouraging oral fluids.‖

questions.rationale: Common physical symptoms at the end of life include anorexia and cachexia. Tube feedings will cause discomfort as the tube is inserted and nausea as the GI tract is given food that it cannot handle. Encouraging oral intake will lead to increased secretions and congestion as well as possible aspiration of fluids. Intravenous fluids will increase congestion and edema. The nurse would educate the family on this part of the dying process.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who just died after a lengthy illne ss. Which portions of postmortem care may be delegated by the nurse to the nursing assistant? ( Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gently washing the body and closing the patient‘s eyes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Offering support and empathy to the patient‘s family members

choices.label: C
choices.text: Documenting the patient‘s time of death in the medical record

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notifying all of the patient‘s consulting providers of the patient‘s death

choices.label: E
choices.text: Removing the patient‘s hospital ID band, IV lines, and urinary catheter

choices.label: F
choices.text: Gathering the patient‘s belongings so they may be taken home by the family

questions.rationale: The nurse assistant can gently wash the patient‘s body, close the patient‘s eyes, and gather the patient‘s belongings. Offering support and empathy to the patient‘s family members would be done by all of the involved members of the nursing staff. Documenting the time of death in the chart and notifying all of the patient‘s providers is performed by the nurse. The nurse assistant can remove the patient‘s IV lines and urinary catheter if allowed by policy, but the hospital ID band would be left in place.

questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient who has just died. Which assessment findings by the physician and nurse are used to confirm that death has occurred? (Select all that apply.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient was incontinent of bowel and bladder.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The patient‘s pupils are fixed and dilated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The provider does not hear a heartbeat.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The pa t i e n t‘s extremities NarUe RcoSoIl aNnGd TmBot.tlCedO. M

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient has no palpable peripheral pulses.

choices.label: F
choices.text: The patient‘s face is relaxed and the mouth is open.

questions.rationale: Assessment findings that confirm death has occurred include lack of pulse/heartbeat and fixed dilated pupils. Cool, mottled extremities, relaxed muscles, and incontinence of bowel and/or stool are common assessment findings in patients who are dying.
